Calculate (i) total thermal resistance, (ii) U-value, and (iii) heat flux for a 25 K temperature difference. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total Thermal Resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{plaster,e} &= \frac{0.050}{0.70} = 0.0714 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W} \\
R_{straw} &= \frac{0.450}{0.055} = 8.1818 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W} \\
R_{plaster,i} &= \frac{0.025}{0.70} = 0.0357 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W} \\
R_{total} &= 0.04 + 0.0714 + 8.1818 + 0.0357 + 0.13 \\
&= 8.4589 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**R_total ≈ 8.46 m²·K/W** (2 Marks)

**(ii) U-value:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{8.4589} = 0.1182 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}
$$

**U ≈ 0.12 W/m²·K** (2 Marks)

**(iii) Heat Flux for ΔT = 25 K:**

$$
q = U \times \Delta T = 0.1182 \times 25 = 2.955 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**q ≈ 2.96 W/m²** (1.5 Marks)

**Conclusion:** The wall provides excellent thermal insulation. For comparison, a 230 mm brick wall with 12 mm plaster both sides has U ≈ 1.8 W/m²·K — about **15x worse** than this straw bale wall. (1.5 Marks for discussion and conclusion)

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Discuss the thermal, acoustic, and sustainability advantages of straw bale construction over conventional brick masonry. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Thermal Performance (2.5 Marks):**
- Straw bale walls achieve **U-values of 0.12 – 0.25 W/m²·K** versus **1.5 – 2.0 W/m²·K** for brick walls.
- Thermal mass of straw is low, but combined with **lime plaster** (higher mass), the wall exhibits a balanced **decoupling** between insulation and mass — ideal for Kerala's hot-humid climate.
- Reduces air-conditioning load by **60 – 80 %** in tropical conditions.

**2. Acoustic Performance (2 Marks):**
- Straw bale walls achieve **STC (Sound Transmission Class) ratings of 50 – 55 dB**, superior to brick walls (40 – 45 dB).
- The fibrous, porous bale structure **dissipates sound energy** through friction and viscous damping within straw capillaries.

**3. Sustainability (2.5 Marks):**
- **Embodied energy:** 100 – 250 MJ/m² (straw bale) vs. 1200 – 1500 MJ/m² (brick) — **6 – 12x lower**.
- **Carbon-negative:** Straw sequesters ~ 0.45 kg CO₂/kg of straw.
- **Agricultural waste utilisation:** Diverts straw from open-field burning (a major cause of NCR's and Delhi's winter smog, and a growing concern in **Pathanamthitta and Kuttanad's rice straw burning**).
- **Local sourcing & low transport cost** in Kerala's rural districts.

---

**(b) List and explain the **five most common defects** observed in straw bale buildings and their preventive measures. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Settlement cracks** at wall/roof junction | Uncontrolled bale compression after construction | Pre-compress with ratchet straps; leave **75 – 100 mm settlement gap**; fill with compressible material |
| 2 | **Moisture damage / rot** in bales | Capillary rise, roof leak, splashback, poor DPC | Provide DPC at base; **wide roof overhang (≥ 600 mm)**; plinth protection |
| 3 | **Plaster cracking** | Rigid cement plaster; insufficient mesh; movement | Use **lime-based** flexible render; reinforce with **galvanised mesh**; cure for 28 days |
| 4 | **Pest infestation (rodents, termites)** | Gaps at base; direct straw exposure | Seal all joints; embed **stainless steel mesh (1 mm opening)** at base; plinth height ≥ 300 mm |
| 5 | **Fire vulnerability of exposed straw** (during construction) | Unrendered bales in storage/staging | Store bales dry; render both faces within **4 weeks** of stacking; keep fire extinguishers on site |

(Each defect: 1 Mark — with **0.5 for cause + 0.5 for prevention**; 1.5 Marks for overall coherence and introduction)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
> 1. **Confusing straw with hay** — instant 1-mark deduction in any definition question.
> 2. **Forgetting to state moisture content (< 20 %)** when discussing bale selection.
> 3. **Using "cement plaster is stronger"** as an argument — this is **wrong** for straw bale; expect 0 marks.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — U-value without "W/m²·K" loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning settlement** in any construction-methodology answer — examiners treat this as an essential design consideration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU ESE Ready**

- ✅ **Straw ≠ Hay.** Only dried, threshed cereal stalks; moisture must be **< 20 %**.
- ✅ **Two system types:** **Load-Bearing (Nebraska)** vs **Non-Load-Bearing (Infill)** — know both.
- ✅ **Standard bale size (KTU reference):** **450 × 350 × 900 mm**, density **100 – 150 kg/m³**.
- ✅ **Thermal conductivity of straw:** **k ≈ 0.045 – 0.070 W/m·K** (use 0.055 as default in problems).
- ✅ **Key formulas (must memorise):**
    - $R = L/k$
    - $U = 1 / R_{total}$
    - $R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum R_{layers} + R_{se}$
    - $q = U \times \Delta T$
    - Settlement $= (0.02 \text{ to } 0.03) \times H_{wall}$
- ✅ **Standard surface resistances:** $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W (horizontal flow).
- ✅ **Lime render is mandatory** — never cement plaster on straw (vapour permeability, flexibility, biocide).
- ✅ **Wall thickness (finished):** **500 – 600 mm** (plaster + bale + plaster).
- ✅ **Typical U-value:** **0.12 – 0.25 W/m²·K** — far superior to brick (1.5 – 2.0).
- ✅ **Settlement allowance:** **75 – 100 mm** gap at top, filled with compressible material.
- ✅ **DPC at base** + **wide roof overhang (≥ 600 mm)** = moisture control.
- ✅ **Fire resistance:** With plaster, **FRL 2 – 4 hours** (comparable or superior to brick).
- ✅ **Carbon-negative material** — locks ~ 0.45 kg CO₂ per kg of straw; ideal for NZEB and Kerala's sustainable housing mission.
- ✅ **Applications:** Rural housing, post-disaster reconstruction (Kerala floods/cyclones), eco-tourism resorts, cost-efficient low-income housing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthen Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Earthen Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earthen Materials** are unfired, naturally occurring soil-based construction materials composed predominantly of clay, silt, sand, and gravel, either used in their natural raw state or stabilized with additives (cement, lime, bitumen, fibres) to enhance their mechanical and durability properties for use in walls, floors, plasters, and roofs.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Modern Construction Technology), earthen materials are repositioned as **sustainable, low-carbon, bio-compatible construction media** that align with green-building paradigms, GRIHA/LEED ratings, and the Indian Standard codes **IS 2110 (Code of Practice for Stabilized Soils)** and **IS 13827 (Improving Earthquake Resistance of Earthen Buildings)**.

### 1.1.1 Constituents of Earthen Materials

| Fraction | Size Range (mm) | Functional Role in Earth |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Clay | < 0.002 | Binder (cohesion, plasticity) |
| Silt | 0.002 – 0.075 | Filler (low activity) |
| Sand | 0.075 – 4.75 | Skeletal framework (friction) |
| Gravel | 4.75 – 80 | Bulk mass (reduces shrinkage) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Earthen Pancake"**
> Think of an earthen wall like a giant pancake batter: clay is the **egg** (sticky binder), silt is the **flour** (body), sand is the **sugar crystals** (skeleton that prevents slumping), and water is the **milk** (lubricant that makes the mix workable). If you put too much water, the pancake runs and cracks when it dries — too little, and it crumbles. Stabilizers (cement/lime) act like a slow oven: they "set" the batter permanently so it can carry a load and resist rain, just like a cooked pancake holds its shape.

## 1.3 Why Earthen Materials in Modern Construction?

- **Embodied carbon**: ~**22 kg CO₂/m³** (earth) vs ~**500 kg CO₂/m³** (burnt clay brick).
- **Thermal mass**: Walls of density **1800–2200 kg/m³** give **8–12 hours** thermal lag.
- **Acoustic absorption (NRC)**: **0.30 – 0.45** at 500 Hz.
- **Locally available**: Zero transportation emissions.
- **Biodegradable / recyclable at end-of-life**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Earthen Construction Systems

Earthen construction is broadly classified into four families:

1. **Monolithic / Rammed Earth (RE)** — moist earth compacted in formwork.
2. **Block / Masonry — Adobe, Compressed Earth Blocks (CEB), Stabilized Earth Blocks (SEB)**.
3. **Coherent / Plastic — Cob, Daub, Wattle-and-Daub**.
4. **Composite / Surface — Earth plasters, mud-mortars, floors (Kadapa, China-mosaic)**.

### 2.1.1 Detailed Sub-Classification

| System | Process | Typical Compressive Strength (MPa) | Wall Thickness (mm) |
|--------|---------|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| Rammed Earth | Moist earth pneumatically/manual tamped in 100–150 mm lifts | **2.5 – 5.0** | 300 – 600 |
| Adobe | Sun-dried mud bricks (often straw-reinforced) | **1.0 – 3.0** | 300 – 500 |
| CEB (Cinva-Ram) | Mechanically pressed blocks | **2.0 – 7.0** | 250 – 400 |
| Cob | Hand-shaped moist clay-sand-straw lumps | **0.5 – 1.5** | 450 – 600 |
| Earth Plaster | Surface coating for protection/finish | — | 12 – 25 |

## 2.2 Geotechnical Foundation: Atterberg Limits

The behaviour of earthen materials in the plastic state is governed by **Atterberg Limits** — a foundational concept from soil mechanics directly applicable to unfired earth construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Atterberg Limits (A. Atterberg, 1911)** are the water-content boundaries that define the transitions between the **solid, semi-solid, plastic, and liquid** states of a fine-grained soil.

### 2.2.1 The Four States and Three Limits

- **Shrinkage Limit (Wₛ)** — boundary between *solid* and *semi-solid* states.
- **Plastic Limit (Wₚ or PL)** — boundary between *semi-solid* and *plastic* states.
- **Liquid Limit (Wₗ or LL)** — boundary between *plastic* and *liquid* states.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-A and Part-B earth-materials question.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Plasticity Index | $PI = LL - PL$ | % | Range: 0 (sand) – 100+ (heavy clay) |
| 2 | Liquidity Index | $LI = \dfrac{W - PL}{PI}$ | dimensionless | W = in-situ water content |
| 3 | Consistency Index | $CI = \dfrac{LL - W}{PI}$ | dimensionless | $CI + LI = 1$ |
| 4 | Shrinkage Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{m_d}$ | m³/kg | $V_1$ = vol. at LL, $V_2$ = dry vol. |
| 5 | Volumetric Shrinkage | $VS = \dfrac{V_1 - V_{dry}}{V_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | — |
| 6 | Linear Shrinkage | $LS = \dfrac{L_{wet} - L_{dry}}{L_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Approximation: $LS \approx VS/3$ |
| 7 | Dry Density | $\rho_d = \dfrac{m_d}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Proctor test output |
| 8 | Degree of Compaction | $R_c = \dfrac{\rho_{d,field}}{\rho_{d,max(Proctor)}} \times 100$ | % | IS 2720 Part VIII |
| 9 | Optimum Moisture Content | $OMC = w$ at $\rho_{d,max}$ | % | Standard Proctor |
| 10 | Porosity | $n = \dfrac{e}{1+e}$ | dimensionless | $e$ = void ratio |
| 11 | Specific Surface Roughness | $R_{rough} = \dfrac{V_{sand}}{V_{clay}}$ | ratio | Sand:Clay balance indicator |
| 12 | Skempton's Activity | $A = \dfrac{PI}{\% \text{ clay fraction}}$ | dimensionless | Clay mineralogy indicator |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **PI directly governs crack-resistance of unfired earth** — high PI means more clay → more shrinkage cracks.
- **OMC** is critical for rammed earth quality control: too wet → slumping; too dry → laminations.
- **Activity (A)** distinguishes **inactive** (kaolinite, A<0.75), **normal** (illite, 0.75<A<1.25), and **active** (montmorillonite, A>1.25) clays — only inactive and normal clays are suitable for earthen walls.
- **Earthquake design** under IS 13827: recommendations for wall slenderness ratio ≤ 6, geomesh reinforcement, ring beams at lintel/roof level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plasticity Index from Liquid and Plastic Limits

Starting from the **definition of the Plasticity Index (PI)** as the arithmetical range of water content over which a soil behaves plastically:

$$
PI = LL - PL
$$

> **Logic line:** The PI is, by geotechnical definition, the length of the plastic-state interval on the water-content axis, bounded above by LL and below by PL. The minus sign yields a positive quantity (LL > PL always).

For a sample where $LL = 42\%$ and $PL = 18\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= LL - PL \\
   &= 42\% - 18\% \\
   &= 24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Both inputs are percentages; subtraction is dimensionally consistent. A PI of 24% classifies the soil as a **CI (clay of intermediate plasticity)** per IS 1498.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Shrinkage from First Principles

Consider a soil specimen at the **Liquid Limit (W_L)**: mass of water = $m_w$, mass of dry soil = $m_d$, total volume = $V_1$.

After oven-drying to constant mass (i.e. below the Shrinkage Limit), the volume reduces to $V_2 = V_{dry}$.

The **mass of water at LL** is:

$$
m_w = m_d \times \dfrac{W_L}{100}
$$

The **volumetric shrinkage (VS)** is defined as the percentage change in volume per unit dry volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VS &= \dfrac{V_1 - V_{dry}}{V_{dry}} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_{dry}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering implication:** High VS → high cracking potential. Earth-wall mix-design targets **VS < 6%** by adding non-plastic aggregates (sand) and fibres (sisal, straw).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Liquidity Index and Consistency Index Relation

Liquidity Index:

$$
LI = \dfrac{W - PL}{PI}
$$

Consistency Index:

$$
CI = \dfrac{LL - W}{PI}
$$

Adding them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LI + CI &= \dfrac{W - PL}{PI} + \dfrac{LL - W}{PI} \\
        &= \dfrac{(W - PL) + (LL - W)}{PI} \\
        &= \dfrac{LL - PL}{PI} \\
        &= \dfrac{PI}{PI} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This identity is a KTU board favourite — examiners ask students to "show that LI + CI = 1". Memorise the cancellation: the **W**-terms cancel, leaving **LL − PL = PI** in the numerator.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Dry Density from Proctor Compaction

In the **Standard Proctor Test (IS 2720 Part VII)**, the wet density of a compacted sample is:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}
$$

where $m$ = total wet mass, $V$ = mould volume (standard 944 cc).

The dry density is obtained by removing the water mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_d &= \dfrac{m}{1 + w} \\
\rho_d &= \dfrac{m_d}{V} = \dfrac{m}{V(1+w)} = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $w$ = water content (decimal).

**Worked numerical example:**

Given: $m = 1.95 \text{ kg}$, $V = 944 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.000944 \text{ m}^3$, $w = 0.12$ (12%).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{1.95}{0.000944} = 2065.7 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
\rho_d &= \dfrac{2065.7}{1 + 0.12} = \dfrac{2065.7}{1.12} = 1844.4 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\rho_d$ is always less than wet $\rho$ for any $w > 0$. The result 1844 kg/m³ lies in the typical range for compacted earthen walls (1700–2000 kg/m³).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mix-Design Decision Tree

| Observation in Lab Test | Diagnostic Meaning | Action in Mix Design |
|-------------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| PI > 20% | Excess clay | Add 20–40% sand by mass |
| LL > 50% | Highly plastic clay | Replace with low-PI soil |
| OMC variability > ±2% | Inconsistent gradation | Sieve and re-blend |
| Dry density < 1700 kg/m³ | Inadequate compaction | Increase tamping passes or lift thickness ↓ |
| VS > 8% | Shrinkage cracking | Add 1–2% straw fibre, lime 5% |
| Activity A > 1.25 | Montmorillonite present | NOT suitable — reject the soil |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flow of Earthen Materials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Earthen Construction Materials] --> B0[Monolithic]
    A0 --> C0[Block Masonry]
    A0 --> D0[Plastic Coherent]
    A0 --> E0[Surface Finishes]
    B0 --> B1[Rammed Earth RE]
    B0 --> B2[Poured Earth]
    C0 --> C1[Adobe]
    C0 --> C2[Compressed Earth Block CEB]
    C0 --> C3[Stabilized Earth Block SEB]
    D0 --> D1[Cob]
    D0 --> D2[Wattle and Daub]
    E0 --> E1[Mud Plaster]
    E0 --> E2[Earthen Floor]
    E0 --> E3[Thatch with Mud Daub]
    B1 --> F1[Optimum 8 to 12 percent clay]
    C2 --> F2[Press 2 to 5 MPa]
    C3 --> F3[Add 5 to 10 percent cement or lime]
    D1 --> F4[Hand shaped clay sand straw]
```

## 4.2 Rammed Earth Wall Construction Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Site Soil Selection] --> S1[Sieve to 20 mm]
    S1 --> S2[Moisture Conditioning to OMC]
    S2 --> S3[Add Stabilizer if needed]
    S3 --> S4[Erect Formwork Panel 1.2 m High]
    S4 --> S5[Pour Loose Earth 100 mm Lift]
    S5 --> S6[Pneumatic Tamping 4 to 6 Passes]
    S6 --> S7[Repeat Lifts up to Full Height]
    S7 --> S8[Strip Formwork after 24 hr]
    S8 --> S9[Cure 28 Days]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Earth Plaster 12 to 25 mm]
    S10 --> S11[Finished Earthen Wall]
```

## 4.3 Compressed Earth Block (CEB) Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Pulverized Soil Sieved] --> P1[Weighed Batch]
    P1 --> P2[Stabilizer Added 5 to 10 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Manual or Mechanical Mixing]
    P3 --> P4[Feed into Press]
    P4 --> P5[Press at 2 to 5 MPa]
    P5 --> P6[Eject Block]
    P6 --> P7[Air Cure 28 Days]
    P7 --> P8[Stack with Spacers]
    P8 --> P9[Test Compressive Strength 2 to 7 MPa]
```

## 4.4 Stabilization Decision Block (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0[Unstabilized Earth Problem] --> U1{High Clay Content PI greater than 20}
    U1 -- Yes --> U2[Mechanical Stabilization Add Sand]
    U1 -- No --> U3{Exposure to Rain}
    U3 -- Yes --> U4[Chemical Stabilization 5 to 10 percent Cement]
    U3 -- No --> U5[Use Lime if Soil is Silty]
    U4 --> U6[Improved Wet Strength and Durability]
    U5 --> U6
    U2 --> U7[Reduced Shrinkage Cracks]
    U6 --> U8[Earthen Material Ready for Wall Construction]
    U7 --> U8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **Q1.** Define *Plasticity Index* and *Liquidity Index*. State the identity that relates them and indicate the range of values in which a soil behaves **plastic** in nature. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Plasticity Index (PI):** the numerical range of water content over which a soil remains in a plastic state. $PI = LL - PL$.
- **Liquidity Index (LI):** the ratio of the difference between in-situ water content and plastic limit to the plasticity index. $LI = \dfrac{W - PL}{PI}$.
- **Identity:** $LI + CI = 1$.
- **Plastic range:** water content between **PL and LL**, i.e. $0 \le LI \le 1$ (with W between PL and LL).

---

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Q2.** List any **four types of earthen construction systems** with one characteristic application of each. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Rammed Earth** — load-bearing walls of public/government buildings (Auroville, Matrimandir approach).
2. **Adobe** — rural housing in arid regions of Rajasthan, Ladakh.
3. **Compressed Earth Blocks (CEB)** — institutional and eco-resort construction (Auroville Earth Institute, Hunnarshila Foundation).
4. **Cob** — vernacular cottages in the West and South-West United Kingdom, adapted to Kerala-style huts.
5. (Bonus) **Mud Plaster** — protective surface coating for both earthen and modern walls in tropical climates.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Choose either Q1 or Q2.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction procedure of Rammed Earth walls** and list the four essential properties of soil suitable for rammed earth. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Procedure** (step-wise):

1. **Soil selection and testing** — Sieve analysis, Atterberg limits, Proctor compaction; ensure PI = 5–15%, sand fraction 50–70%, OMC ± 2% tolerance.
2. **Formwork erection** — Use rigid steel/plywood panels, braced against each other; standard lift height 100–150 mm; pre-treat formwork with release oil.
3. **Moisture conditioning** — Pre-mix soil to OMC (8–12% by mass) and let it cure in a heap for 24 hours to ensure uniform moisture distribution.
4. **Pouring loose earth** — Fill formwork in layers (lifts) of 100–150 mm.
5. **Compaction** — Use a pneumatic tamper at 4–6 passes per lift, achieving dry density of **1700–2000 kg/m³**.
6. **Lift repetition** — Repeat steps 4–5 until full formwork height is reached.
7. **Curing** — Strip formwork after 24 h; cure by sprinkling water for 7 days; allow 28 days for full strength.
8. **Surface finish** — Apply earth plaster (12–25 mm) or breathable lime-wash for weather protection.

**Four essential properties of soil for RE:**

1. **Plasticity Index (PI) between 5 and 15%** (clay content sufficient for binding, low enough to avoid shrinkage cracks).
2. **Well-graded sand–silt–clay** distribution (gap-graded or with sand fraction 50–70%).
3. **Low activity clay minerals** (kaolinite, illite) — Skempton's activity $A < 1.25$.
4. **Compaction compatibility** — OMC between 8% and 12% with Proctor dry density > 1750 kg/m³.

**(b)** A **liquid limit test by Casagrande apparatus** on a soil sample gave the following data:

| No. of blows (N) | Water content w (%) |
|------------------|---------------------|
| 12 | 52 |
| 18 | 49 |
| 25 | 46 |
| 32 | 43 |
| 40 | 41 |

Determine the **Liquid Limit** and the **Flow Index**. If the **Plastic Limit** of the same soil is **20%**, calculate the **Plasticity Index** and comment on the **classification** of the soil. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plot semi-log flow curve** with w (Y-axis) vs log N (X-axis).

- $\log 12 = 1.079$, w = 52%
- $\log 18 = 1.255$, w = 49%
- $\log 25 = 1.398$, w = 46%
- $\log 32 = 1.505$, w = 43%
- $\log 40 = 1.602$, w = 41%

**Step 2 — LL is the water content at N = 25 blows** (IS convention).

$$
\boxed{LL = 46\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Flow Index ($I_f$):** the slope of the flow curve.

Using two extreme points: $(N_1 = 12, w_1 = 52)$ and $(N_2 = 40, w_2 = 41)$.

$$
I_f = \dfrac{w_1 - w_2}{\log N_2 - \log N_1} = \dfrac{52 - 41}{\log 40 - \log 12} = \dfrac{11}{1.602 - 1.079} = \dfrac{11}{0.523}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_f \approx 21.03\% \text{ per cycle}}
$$

**Step 4 — Plasticity Index:**

$$
PI = LL - PL = 46 - 20 = \boxed{26\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification (IS 1498, Plasticity Chart):**

- $LL = 46\%$ (above A-line, since $A$-line at $LL = 46$ gives $PI_A = 0.73(46 - 20) = 18.98$).
- $PI = 26\% > 18.98\%$, so the soil plots **above the A-line**.
- Soil is classified as **CI — Clay of Intermediate Plasticity**.

> **Engineering comment:** This clay is borderline acceptable for rammed earth (PI 5–15% preferred) but excellent for stabilized earth blocks (where 5–10% cement is added).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **chemical and mechanical stabilization of earthen materials** with suitable examples. State **two advantages** of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Chemical Stabilization** — addition of a binder that reacts with clay minerals to form cementitious / pozzolanic compounds, increasing strength and water resistance.

| Stabilizer | Dosage (% by mass of dry soil) | Reaction | Use |
|------------|--------------------------------|----------|-----|
| Ordinary Portland Cement | 5 – 10% | C₃S, C₂S hydrate → C-S-H gel binds clay | CEB, rammed earth in wet climates |
| Hydrated Lime | 3 – 8% | Pozzolanic reaction with silica/alumina of clay | Silt-clay soils (pH ≥ 12) |
| Bitumen Emulsion | 2 – 6% | Hydrophobic film around particles | Roads, plinth protection |
| Sugarcane / Molasses | 1 – 3% | Microbial + chelation | Traditional rural plasters |

**Two advantages of chemical stabilization:**
1. **Increased wet compressive strength** (3×–5× improvement, e.g. 2 MPa → 8–10 MPa with 8% cement).
2. **Reduced water absorption** (10–15% → 3–5% by mass), critical for monsoon regions like Kerala.

**Mechanical Stabilization** — modification of the soil's particle-size distribution by blending with another soil or by compaction alone.

**Methods:**
- **Blending** clay-rich and sand-rich soils to achieve the optimum gradation (Faber-Fawcett ideal: 25% gravel, 30% sand, 25% silt, 20% clay).
- **Compaction** to higher density (Modified Proctor) reducing voids.
- **Fibre reinforcement** — sisal, coir, straw (0.5–2% by mass) improving tensile strength by 30–60%.

**Two advantages of mechanical stabilization:**
1. **No chemical cost**; uses only locally available soil.
2. **Eliminates shrinkage cracking** by lowering PI through dilution with sand (PI drops from 25% to ~8%).

**(b)** During a **Standard Proctor Compaction Test**, the following data were recorded for one of the trials:

- Mass of compacted soil in mould = **1.85 kg**
- Volume of mould = **944 cm³**
- Water content = **10.5%**

Compute the **wet density**, **dry density**, and the **air voids** if the specific gravity of soil solids is **2.65**. Assume the soil is **saturated** for the air-voids part. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Wet density (ρ):**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{1.85 \text{ kg}}{944 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3} = 1959.7 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Dry density (ρ_d):**

$$
\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w} = \dfrac{1959.7}{1 + 0.105} = \dfrac{1959.7}{1.105} = 1773.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Void ratio (e):** using the phase-relation $\rho_d = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{1 + e}$.

$$
e = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1 = \dfrac{2.65 \times 1000}{1773.5} - 1 = 1.4944 - 1 = 0.494
$$

**Step 4 — Air voids for saturated soil (S = 1):** When soil is saturated, $S = 1$, hence:

$$
A_v = n \cdot (1 - S) = 0
$$

> **Engineering note:** Although the question *assumes* saturation, the dry density achieved (1773 kg/m³) indicates an air-void content of approximately 5–6% in the actual unsoaked field condition. The student should explicitly state the **assumption of saturation** ($S = 1$) to justify $A_v = 0$.

**Classification check:** $\rho_d = 1773 \text{ kg/m}^3 > 1700 \text{ kg/m}^3$ → the compaction is **acceptable for rammed earth walls**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing wet density with dry density** in Proctor problems — always divide by $(1+w)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to take log of blows** in the flow-curve question — Flow Index is defined on a *semi-log* plot.
> 3. **Confusing consistency index (CI) with liquidity index (LI)** — remember: $CI + LI = 1$, and CI is used in *drier* states while LI is used in *wetter* states.
> 4. **Writing "rammed earth" without mentioning formwork lifts** — a 14-mark answer without a sketch of formwork + lift sequence will lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Citing invented IS codes** — only **IS 2110, IS 13827, IS 1498, IS 2720 (Parts VII, VIII)** are valid for this topic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 1, Earthen Materials)**

- [x] Earthen materials = **unfired soil** (clay + silt + sand + gravel); main binder is **clay**.
- [x] **Atterberg limits**: $LL > PL > SL$ always; $PI = LL - PL$.
- [x] **$LI + CI = 1$** — derivable identity; KTU favourite.
- [x] **PI for rammed earth**: ideal 5–15%; PI > 20% → high shrinkage → add sand.
- [x] **Activity of clay (Skempton)**: $A = PI / (\% \text{ clay})$; reject if $A > 1.25$.
- [x] **Proctor compaction**: $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho}{1+w}$; OMC at peak dry density.
- [x] **Stabilization types**: *mechanical* (sanding, fibre) and *chemical* (cement 5–10%, lime 3–8%, bitumen 2–6%).
- [x] **Cement-stabilized** blocks for wet climates; **lime-stabilized** for silty soils.
- [x] **Rammed earth** walls: lift 100–150 mm, 4–6 tamper passes, density 1700–2000 kg/m³.
- [x] **Adobe**: sun-dried; **CEB**: pressed at 2–5 MPa; **Cob**: hand-shaped; **Mud plaster**: 12–25 mm thick.
- [x] **Codes**: IS 2110 (stabilized soils), IS 13827 (earthquake-resistant earthen buildings), IS 1498 (classification), IS 2720 Part VII & VIII (tests).
- [x] **Shrinkage limit calculation**: $SR = \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{m_d}$; volumetric shrinkage < 6% is the design target.
- [x] **Typical compressive strengths**: Adobe 1–3 MPa; CEB 2–7 MPa; Rammed Earth 2.5–5 MPa; Stabilized 5–10 MPa.
- [x] **Environmental dividend**: ~22 kg CO₂/m³ vs ~500 kg CO₂/m³ for burnt bricks — a 95% reduction in embodied carbon.
- [x] **Thermal mass**: 8–12 hours lag for 300–600 mm walls — Kerala-passive cooling relevance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycled Aggregates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recycled Aggregates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECET522 – Modern Construction Technology**, **Recycled Aggregates (RA)** are granular inorganic materials derived from the processing (crushing, screening, washing, and grading) of inorganic waste materials previously used in construction, demolition, or civil engineering infrastructure. The processed material is subsequently re-deployed as a partial or full substitute for natural aggregates (NA) in fresh concrete or other construction applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:**
> *Recycled Aggregate is the aggregate produced by the mechanical processing (or a combination of mechanical and chemical processing) of inorganic material previously used in construction and demolition (C&D) waste.*

The principal input material stream is **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste**, which is generated during:
- Structural demolition of buildings and bridges
- Renovation and remodelling works
- Road reconstruction activities
- Disaster debris (e.g., post-flood, post-earthquake clearance)

## 1.2 Classification of Recycled Aggregates

Recycled aggregates are officially classified based on the parent material composition. This classification is universally adopted in KTU-endorsed reference texts and is treated as a high-yield topic.

| Category | Full Form | Parent Material | Typical Colour |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **RCA** | Recycled Concrete Aggregate | Old hardened concrete only | Grey |
| **RMA** | Recycled Masonry Aggregate | Bricks, mortar, tiles, masonry | Reddish-brown |
| **MRA** | Mixed Recycled Aggregate | Concrete + Masonry (blended) | Mixed grey-red |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Note:** The Indian standard governing the use of these aggregates is **IS 383:2016 (Coarse and Fine Aggregates for Concrete – Specification)**, which permits a maximum of **20% replacement** of natural coarse aggregate with recycled coarse aggregate (RCA) for plain cement concrete (PCC) and a maximum of **20%** for reinforced cement concrete (RCC) up to **M25 grade** without special approval.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Stone That Lived Twice"**
> Imagine a building as a giant Lego sculpture. When the sculpture is dismantled, the individual Lego bricks are still perfectly usable — they are just slightly chipped, scratched, or dusty. If we collect, clean, and re-shape these old bricks, we can build a brand-new sculpture that may not be as strong as the original, but is fully functional for non-critical uses.
> In the same way, **recycled aggregates are the "old Lego bricks" of the construction industry** — salvaged from demolished structures, cleaned of impurities (rebar, wood, plastic), crushed, and graded. The resulting particles still possess the chemical essence of stone (silicates, calcium) but are wrapped in a thin layer of old cement mortar, which is why they absorb more water and are slightly weaker than virgin quarried stone.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics

The following physical metrics are the **bedrock numerical values** for any KTU-level problem on recycled aggregates:

- **Specific Gravity (SSD basis):** Natural Aggregate ≈ **2.6 – 2.7**; RCA ≈ **2.2 – 2.4**
- **Water Absorption (% by mass):** NA ≤ **1.0%**; RCA ≈ **4.0% – 9.0%**
- **Loose Bulk Density:** NA ≈ **1.45 – 1.55 g/cc**; RCA ≈ **1.20 – 1.35 g/cc**
- **Crushing Value (IS 2386 Part IV):** NA ≈ **14% – 22%**; RCA ≈ **20% – 30%**
- **Impact Value (IS 2386 Part IV):** NA ≈ **10% – 18%**; RCA ≈ **18% – 32%**
- **Los Angeles Abrasion Value:** NA ≈ **15% – 25%**; RCA ≈ **25% – 40%**
- **Nominal Size Range:** Coarse RA — 4.75 mm to 20 mm; Fine RA — 75 µm to 4.75 mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RCA is "weaker" than NA:** A typical RCA particle consists of a natural stone nucleus encased in a porous layer of old cement mortar (typically 25% – 65% of the particle volume). This old mortar is the primary cause of the lower density, higher water absorption, and reduced mechanical strength observed in RCA.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Five Pillars" of Recycled Aggregate Characterisation

For KTU 2024 evaluation, every problem on RA ultimately tests one or more of these five properties. Master these, and the topic becomes a guaranteed high-score area.

### Pillar 1: Density and Specific Gravity
Because the adhered mortar is porous, the **apparent (oven-dry) specific gravity** of RCA is always lower than NA. The **SSD (saturated surface-dry) specific gravity** is used in concrete mix design calculations.

### Pillar 2: Water Absorption
The adhered mortar contains micro-capillary pores, which act as sponges. **Water absorption is the single most critical property** governing mix design adjustments.

### Pillar 3: Mechanical Strength Indicators
- **Aggregate Crushing Value (ACV)** = Resistance to gradually applied compressive load.
- **Aggregate Impact Value (AIV)** = Resistance to sudden impact load.
- **Los Angeles (LA) Abrasion Value** = Resistance to combined abrasion and impact in a steel drum.

### Pillar 4: Shape & Surface Texture
RCA particles are more **angular, flaky, and rough-textured** than NA due to the crushing process and the brittle nature of the residual mortar. This improves the **bond between aggregate and new cement paste** (a positive effect), but reduces **workability** (a negative effect).

### Pillar 5: Chemical & Durability Aspects
RCA may carry residual chlorides (from marine exposure) and sulphates (from gypsiferous mortar). These can accelerate rebar corrosion and sulphate attack in the new concrete.

## 2.2 Production Process (Engineering Insight)

The transformation of bulky C&D waste into graded recycled aggregate follows a controlled sequence of unit operations:

1. **Pre-demolition survey** – Identify hazardous materials (asbestos, lead paint).
2. **Selective demolition** – Hand-stripping of finishes, separation of contaminants.
3. **Primary crushing** – Jaw crusher reduces debris to 100–200 mm fragments.
4. **Magnetic separation** – Recovery of reinforcing steel.
5. **Secondary crushing** – Cone or impact crusher reduces size to 20–40 mm.
6. **Air/water separation** – Removal of lightweight contaminants (wood, plastic, paper).
7. **Screening & grading** – Classified into desired size fractions.
8. **Washing** – Removes fines (<75 µm) and soluble impurities.
9. **Stockpiling** – Stored in covered bays to maintain SSD condition.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following markdown table is your **exam-day rapid-revision tool**. Every formula below is mathematically self-contained and is directly testable in KTU 2024 ESE Part A and Part B questions.

| Property | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard | Typical Unit |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Water Absorption | $W_a = \dfrac{(W_{sat} - W_{dry})}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | $W_{sat}$ = saturated mass, $W_{dry}$ = oven-dry mass | IS 2386 Part III | % |
| Specific Gravity (OD) | $G_{od} = \dfrac{W_{dry}}{W_{dry} - W_{sub}}$ | $W_{sub}$ = submerged mass in water | IS 2386 Part III | dimensionless |
| Specific Gravity (SSD) | $G_{ssd} = \dfrac{W_{sat}}{W_{sat} - W_{sub}}$ | SSD basis used in mix design | IS 2386 Part III | dimensionless |
| Aggregate Crushing Value | $ACV = \dfrac{W_{crushed}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | $W_{crushed}$ = mass passing 2.36 mm after load | IS 2386 Part IV | % |
| Aggregate Impact Value | $AIV = \dfrac{W_{fines}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | $W_{fines}$ = mass passing 2.36 mm after 15 blows | IS 2386 Part IV | % |
| LA Abrasion Value | $LAA = \dfrac{W_{abraded}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | After 500 revolutions, 30–33 rpm | IS 2386 Part IV | % |
| Replacement Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_{RA}}{V_{NA} + V_{RA}} \times 100$ | $V$ = volume of aggregate | – | % |
| Effective Water Adjustment | $W_{eff} = W_{mix} + (r \times W_{a,RA} \times M_{RA}) - (r \times W_{a,NA} \times M_{NA})$ | Compensates for RA absorption | – | kg/m³ |
| Density Index (Porosity) | $n = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{particle}}$ | Ratio of voids to total volume | – | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook — "WAS" Formula Trio:**
> W (Weight after immersion) – A (Absorbed water) – S (Surface water)
> For SSD condition: $W_{sat} = W_{dry} + A$ and $W_{ssd} = W_{dry} + (A/2)$ approximately.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sustainable Construction:** Diverts 30% – 40% of total municipal solid waste stream from landfills.
- **Carbon Footprint Reduction:** Reduces CO₂ emissions by approximately **15% – 20%** per cubic metre of concrete when 30% NA is replaced with RCA.
- **Urban Mining:** Particularly valuable in dense urban areas like **Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, and Kozhikode** where quarries are distant and landfill capacity is scarce.
- **Cost Reduction:** In Kerala's 2023–2024 C&D waste policy context, processed RCA is available at **40% – 60%** the cost of crushed granite aggregate in metro-adjacent projects.
- **LEED & GRIHA Certification:** Use of RA contributes directly to *Materials & Resources* credits under green building rating systems.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU answers, always mention "sustainable development" and "circular economy" in your conclusions.** Examiners reward socio-environmental awareness with 1–2 bonus marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Derivation — Water Absorption of Recycled Coarse Aggregate

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark Part B sub-question):**
> A laboratory test on Recycled Coarse Aggregate (RCA) yielded the following data:
> - Mass of oven-dry sample: $W_{dry} = 1500 \text{ g}$
> - Mass of saturated surface-dry (SSD) sample: $W_{sat} = 1620 \text{ g}$
> - Mass of sample submerged in water: $W_{sub} = 905 \text{ g}$
>
> Determine: (i) Water absorption (ii) Apparent specific gravity (iii) SSD specific gravity (iv) Bulk density in SSD condition, given loose volume of the SSD sample = 720 cm³.

### Step 1 — Compute Water Absorption

The governing equation per IS 2386 Part III is:

$$
W_a = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
W_a = \frac{1620 - 1500}{1500} \times 100
$$

$$
W_a = \frac{120}{1500} \times 100
$$

$$
W_a = 0.08 \times 100 = 8.0\%
$$

**Interpretation:** An $8.0\%$ water absorption is characteristic of RCA with significant adhered mortar content. This value is **8 times higher** than the typical NA limit of $1.0\%$ under IS 383.

### Step 2 — Compute Apparent (Oven-Dry) Specific Gravity

$$
G_{od} = \frac{W_{dry}}{W_{dry} - W_{sub}}
$$

$$
G_{od} = \frac{1500}{1500 - 905} = \frac{1500}{595}
$$

$$
G_{od} = 2.521
$$

### Step 3 — Compute SSD Specific Gravity

$$
G_{ssd} = \frac{W_{sat}}{W_{sat} - W_{sub}}
$$

$$
G_{ssd} = \frac{1620}{1620 - 905} = \frac{1620}{715}
$$

$$
G_{ssd} = 2.266
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why SSD specific gravity is the critical one for mix design:**
> In concrete mix design (IS 10262), the volume of aggregate is calculated assuming it is in the SSD condition — neither absorbing water from the mix (OD) nor contributing water (wet). Therefore, $G_{ssd}$ directly enters the absolute volume equation.

### Step 4 — Compute Bulk Density (Loose, SSD basis)

$$
\rho_{bulk} = \frac{W_{sat}}{V_{loose}}
$$

$$
\rho_{bulk} = \frac{1620 \text{ g}}{720 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

$$
\rho_{bulk} = 2.25 \text{ g/cm}^3 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 2250 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

This falls within the KTU 2024 expected range of **2200 – 2400 kg/m³** for RCA.

---

## 3.2 Solved Derivation — Effective Mix Water Adjustment

This is the **most frequently asked calculation** in KTU Part B questions on RA. It is the cornerstone of any successful RA mix design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (14-mark Part B question):**
> A concrete mix is to be designed for M25 grade with a **30% volumetric replacement** of natural coarse aggregate (NCA) by recycled coarse aggregate (RCA). Given:
> - Total coarse aggregate content (combined) = $1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ of concrete
> - Water absorption of NCA: $W_{a,NCA} = 0.8\%$
> - Water absorption of RCA: $W_{a,RCA} = 6.5\%$
> - Free mixing water required (for NCA at 0.8% absorption): $W_{mix} = 180 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Total aggregate volume remains constant (volumetric replacement).
>
> Calculate: (a) The mass of NCA and RCA in the mix. (b) The effective water to be added to compensate for RCA absorption.

### Step 1 — Resolve the 30% Volumetric Replacement

The 30% is **by volume**, not mass. Since we know the *combined* mass is 1200 kg/m³, we need to convert volumes.

**Volume of NCA (per m³ of concrete):**

$$
V_{NCA} = \frac{M_{NCA}}{G_{ssd,NCA} \times \rho_w}
$$

**Volume of RCA (per m³ of concrete):**

$$
V_{RCA} = \frac{M_{RCA}}{G_{ssd,RCA} \times \rho_w}
$$

Let the *total* volume of coarse aggregate be $V_{total}$. By volumetric proportion:

$$
V_{RCA} = 0.30 \times V_{total}
$$

$$
V_{NCA} = 0.70 \times V_{total}
$$

We use the standard SSD specific gravities: $G_{ssd,NCA} = 2.70$ and $G_{ssd,RCA} = 2.30$.

**Compute the combined volume per kg:**

$$
v_{NCA} = \frac{1}{2.70 \times 1000} = 3.704 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
v_{RCA} = \frac{1}{2.30 \times 1000} = 4.348 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Formulate a Single Equation for Mass Split

Let $M_{NCA} = x$ kg and $M_{RCA} = y$ kg. We have two simultaneous constraints:

**Constraint 1 — Total mass:**

$$
x + y = 1200
$$

**Constraint 2 — Volume ratio (RCA is 30% of total aggregate volume):**

$$
\frac{y \cdot v_{RCA}}{x \cdot v_{NCA} + y \cdot v_{RCA}} = 0.30
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
y \cdot v_{RCA} = 0.30 \cdot (x \cdot v_{NCA} + y \cdot v_{RCA})
$$

$$
0.70 \cdot y \cdot v_{RCA} = 0.30 \cdot x \cdot v_{NCA}
$$

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \frac{0.30 \times v_{NCA}}{0.70 \times v_{RCA}} = \frac{0.30 \times 3.704 \times 10^{-4}}{0.70 \times 4.348 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \frac{1.1112 \times 10^{-4}}{3.0436 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.3651
$$

Therefore: $y = 0.3651 x$

### Step 3 — Solve the Linear System

$$
x + 0.3651 x = 1200
$$

$$
1.3651 x = 1200
$$

$$
x = M_{NCA} = 878.99 \approx 879 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
y = M_{RCA} = 1200 - 879 = 321 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Effective Water Adjustment

The general effective water formula:

$$
W_{eff} = W_{mix} + (W_{a,RCA} \times M_{RCA}) - (W_{a,NCA} \times M_{NCA})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** We add the *extra* water that RCA will absorb (which would otherwise be stolen from the mix), and we subtract the *normal* water that would have been absorbed by NCA (which was already accounted for in $W_{mix}$).

Plugging in:

$$
W_{eff} = 180 + (0.065 \times 321) - (0.008 \times 879)
$$

$$
W_{eff} = 180 + 20.865 - 7.032
$$

$$
W_{eff} = 193.83 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** We must add approximately **13.83 kg of extra water per cubic metre of concrete** to prevent the RCA from desiccating the surrounding cement paste. In practice, this extra water is introduced either as **pre-soaking** (24 h water immersion of RCA) or as a **dosage adjustment** at the batching plant.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RCA vs NCA

| Parameter | NCA (Granite/Basalt) | RCA (Typical) | Engineering Consequence |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Specific Gravity (SSD) | **2.6 – 2.7** | **2.1 – 2.4** | Mix design volume calculations change |
| Water Absorption | **0.5 – 1.0%** | **4.0 – 9.0%** | Effective water/cement ratio must be re-computed |
| 28-day Compressive Strength | 100% (reference) | 80 – 90% at 30% replacement | Strength reduction factor = 0.85–0.90 |
| Drying Shrinkage | Lower | **30 – 60% higher** | Risk of cracking in restrained elements |
| Elastic Modulus | Higher | 10 – 25% lower | Increased deflection in beams/slabs |
| Workability (Slump) | Higher | Lower (more angular) | Need plasticiser for same slump |
| Carbon Footprint | High (quarrying) | Low (re-use) | Sustainability benefit |
| Cost (Kerala, 2024) | ₹1500 – ₹2200 / m³ | ₹700 – ₹1100 / m³ | Direct economic benefit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 C&D Waste Recycling Process — Functional Flow Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the engineering control flow of a typical stationary C&D waste recycling plant (capacity 100–500 tonnes/day), the standard infrastructure referenced in KTU 2024 case-study questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CD Waste Intake] --> B[Pre-Sort Platform]
    B --> C[Manual Contaminant Removal]
    C --> D[Primary Jaw Crusher]
    D --> E[Magnetic Separator Fe Overband]
    E --> F[Secondary Impact Crusher]
    F --> G[Air Classifier Light Fraction Removal]
    G --> H[Vibrating Screen 20mm]
    H --> I{Size Check}
    I --> J[Oversize Recycle Loop to F]
    I --> K[Undersize 4.75mm Fines Removal]
    K --> L[Water Washing Trommel]
    L --> M[Final Graded RCA Stockpile]
    L --> N[Sludge Dewatering Unit]
    N --> O[Water Recycle Reservoir]
    M --> P[Quality Control Lab IS 2386 Tests]
    P --> Q[Dispatch to Concrete Batching Plant]
    O --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each node represents a *unit operation*. The arrows are *material flow lines*. The diamond (`{Size Check}`) is a *decision node* — material not meeting 4.75–20 mm grading is diverted. The final RCA output is checked against IS 2386 specifications before dispatch.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Material Classification Tree

This block-level architecture diagram presents the **taxonomy of recycled aggregates** as a hierarchical functional decomposition — a topic structure commonly tested in KTU 2024 Part A (3-mark) questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Recycled Aggregates RA]
    Root --> Cat1[Recycled Coarse Aggregate RCA Size 4.75 to 20mm]
    Root --> Cat2[Recycled Fine Aggregate RFA Size 75 micron to 4.75mm]
    Root --> Cat3[Recycled Fines RF Size below 75 micron]
    Cat1 --> Sub1[RCA Type I Pure Concrete Origin]
    Cat1 --> Sub2[RCA Type II Mixed Concrete and Masonry]
    Cat1 --> Sub3[RCA Type III Predominantly Masonry]
    Cat2 --> Sub4[RFA Type A Concrete Derived]
    Cat2 --> Sub5[RFA Type B Masonry Derived]
    Cat3 --> Sub6[Recycled Powder RP Used as SCM Supplement]
    Sub1 --> App1[Application Structural Concrete M25 Limit 20 percent]
    Sub2 --> App2[Application Non Structural PCC Lean Concrete]
    Sub3 --> App3[Application Subbase Road Base Layer]
    Sub6 --> App4[Application Partial Cement Replacement up to 10 percent]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: RA Mix Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Mix Design] --> Q1{Is replacement percent above 30 percent}
    Q1 -- No --> P1[Use Standard IS 10262 Procedure]
    Q1 -- Yes --> P2[Pre Soak RCA for 24 hours]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Effective Water W eff]
    P3 --> P4[Adjust Plasticiser Dosage by plus 15 to 25 percent]
    P4 --> P5{Target Strength Reduction Factor below 0.85}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Limit Replacement to 20 percent]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Conduct Trial Mix Test]
    P7 --> P8[Validate Slump Compaction Strength]
    P8 --> P9[Finalise Mix Proportions]
    P1 --> P9
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Recycled Aggregate (RA). How is it classified based on the parent material composition?**

**Model Answer:**
Recycled Aggregate is a granular material obtained by the mechanical processing of construction and demolition (C&D) waste, used as a substitute for natural aggregate in concrete and other construction applications. It is classified into three main categories based on parent material:

1. **Recycled Concrete Aggregate (RCA):** Derived from crushed old concrete only; grey in appearance.
2. **Recycled Masonry Aggregate (RMA):** Derived from crushed bricks, mortar, and masonry; reddish in appearance.
3. **Mixed Recycled Aggregate (MRA):** A blend of both concrete and masonry sources.

Per **IS 383:2016**, the use of RA is restricted to a maximum of 20% replacement for plain and reinforced concrete up to M25 grade.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: List any four physical properties of Recycled Coarse Aggregate (RCA) and state how each differs from Natural Coarse Aggregate (NCA).**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Property | RCA Value | NCA Value | Difference |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Specific Gravity (SSD) | 2.1 – 2.4 | 2.6 – 2.7 | Lower (porous mortar) |
| 2 | Water Absorption (%) | 4.0 – 9.0 | 0.5 – 1.0 | Higher (capillary pores) |
| 3 | Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 1200 – 1350 | 1450 – 1550 | Lower |
| 4 | Los Angeles Abrasion (%) | 25 – 40 | 15 – 25 | Higher (weaker) |

The inferior properties of RCA arise primarily from the **adhered old cement mortar** that constitutes 25–65% of the particle volume.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice (Question A OR Question B). Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Each sub-part must be answered in approximately 6–8 sentences with at least one supporting diagram/table/derivation.*

---

### PART B – Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step process of producing recycled coarse aggregate** from construction and demolition waste, with the help of a flow diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **influence of recycled coarse aggregate (RCA) on the fresh and hardened properties of concrete**, with specific reference to workability, compressive strength, and durability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The production of RCA from C&D waste involves a controlled sequence of unit operations:

1. **Pre-demolition survey & hazardous material removal:** Identification of asbestos, lead paint, and other toxic substances; selective dismantling to separate salvageable materials.
2. **Primary crushing:** A jaw crusher reduces large concrete chunks (up to 1 m) into fragments of 100–200 mm.
3. **Magnetic separation:** Overband magnets extract ferrous reinforcement (rebar, binding wire) for recycling.
4. **Secondary crushing:** An impact or cone crusher further reduces fragments to 20–40 mm size.
5. **Air classification / wind sifting:** Removes lightweight contaminants (wood, plastic, paper, insulation).
6. **Screening:** Vibrating screens classify the material into desired size fractions (e.g., 20 mm, 12.5 mm, 10 mm).
7. **Washing:** A water spray removes fine dust (< 75 µm) and soluble salts/chlorides.
8. **Stockpiling:** Graded RCA is stored in covered bays to maintain SSD condition and prevent contamination.

**Flow Diagram:**
```
CD Waste → Pre-sort → Primary Crusher → Magnet → Secondary Crusher
       → Air Classifier → Vibrating Screen → Washing → Stockpile → QC Lab → Dispatch
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**1. Workability:** RCA has more angular, rough-textured particles with porous adhered mortar. This increases internal friction and water demand, leading to a **slump reduction of 20–50 mm** at constant water content. To restore workability, the effective water must be increased or a superplasticiser dosage raised by 15–25%.

**2. Compressive Strength:** At 30% RCA replacement, the 28-day compressive strength is typically 85–90% of the control mix. At 100% replacement, strength may drop to 70–80%. The reduction is due to the weaker ITZ (interfacial transition zone) between old mortar and new cement paste.

**3. Durability:** RCA concrete exhibits:
- **Higher drying shrinkage (30–60% greater)** due to porous mortar.
- **Lower resistance to chloride penetration** (residual chloride content).
- **Higher creep** under sustained loads.
- **Carbonation depth** may increase by 10–20% if water/cement ratio is not adjusted.

**Mitigation:** Pre-soaking of RCA, use of mineral admixtures (fly ash, GGBS, silica fume), and limiting replacement to 20% for structural applications (per IS 383:2016).

---

### PART B – Question B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: (a)** With neat sketches/tables, compare the **physical and mechanical properties of Natural Coarse Aggregate (NCA) and Recycled Coarse Aggregate (RCA)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concrete mix is to be designed with **25% volumetric replacement** of NCA by RCA. Given:
- Total coarse aggregate content = $1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- SSD specific gravity of NCA = $2.70$
- SSD specific gravity of RCA = $2.30$
- Water absorption of NCA = $0.8\%$
- Water absorption of RCA = $5.5\%$
- Net mixing water (without adjustment) = $185 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Compute: (i) The mass of NCA and RCA per m³. (ii) The **effective mixing water** to be added. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

| Property | NCA | RCA | Reason for Difference |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Specific Gravity (SSD) | 2.6 – 2.7 | 2.1 – 2.4 | Porous mortar coating |
| Water Absorption | 0.5 – 1.0% | 4.0 – 9.0% | Capillary pores in mortar |
| Bulk Density | 1.45 – 1.55 g/cc | 1.20 – 1.35 g/cc | Lighter mortar fraction |
| Aggregate Crushing Value | 14 – 22% | 20 – 30% | Weaker mortar |
| Aggregate Impact Value | 10 – 18% | 18 – 32% | Brittle mortar |
| LA Abrasion | 15 – 25% | 25 – 40% | Combined effect |
| Shape | Rounded, sub-rounded | Angular, flaky | Crushing process |
| Surface Texture | Smooth | Rough | Improves paste bond |

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Volumetric proportioning**

Let $M_{NCA} = x$ and $M_{RCA} = y$ in kg/m³.

$$
x + y = 1100
$$

Volume of NCA per kg = $\dfrac{1}{2.70} = 0.3704$ L/kg
Volume of RCA per kg = $\dfrac{1}{2.30} = 0.4348$ L/kg

Volumetric constraint: RCA accounts for 25% of the **total coarse aggregate volume**.

$$
\frac{0.4348 \, y}{0.3704 \, x + 0.4348 \, y} = 0.25
$$

Rearranging:

$$
0.4348 \, y = 0.25 \times (0.3704 \, x + 0.4348 \, y)
$$

$$
0.3261 \, y = 0.0926 \, x
$$

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \frac{0.0926}{0.3261} = 0.2840
$$

Therefore: $y = 0.2840 \, x$

**Step 2 — Solve linear system**

$$
x + 0.2840 \, x = 1100
$$

$$
1.2840 \, x = 1100
$$

$$
x = M_{NCA} = 856.7 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
y = M_{RCA} = 1100 - 856.7 = 243.3 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Effective mixing water**

$$
W_{eff} = W_{mix} + (W_{a,RCA} \times M_{RCA}) - (W_{a,NCA} \times M_{NCA})
$$

$$
W_{eff} = 185 + (0.055 \times 243.3) - (0.008 \times 856.7)
$$

$$
W_{eff} = 185 + 13.38 - 6.85
$$

$$
W_{eff} = 191.53 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Add approximately **6.53 kg of extra water per m³** (over the 185 kg baseline) to compensate for the higher water absorption of the RCA fraction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing volumetric vs. mass replacement:** The problem almost always states volumetric replacement. Solving as a mass split will give wrong numerical answers.
> 2. **Using wrong specific gravity basis:** Use SSD specific gravity, not oven-dry (OD) or apparent specific gravity, when computing aggregate volumes in mix design.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the effective water formula:** Always *add* the RCA absorption and *subtract* the NCA absorption. Reversing the signs is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Marks are deducted for final answers without "kg/m³" or "%" units.
> 5. **Forgetting IS code references:** Mentioning **IS 383:2016** and **IS 2386** explicitly in descriptive answers adds 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the flow diagram:** In production-process questions, a clean flow diagram is worth 1.5–2 marks. Even a text-based ASCII flow is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- [x] **Definition:** Recycled Aggregate = processed C&D waste used as substitute for NA.
- [x] **Three Categories:** RCA, RMA, MRA — based on parent material.
- [x] **Key Reason for Inferior Properties:** Adhered old cement mortar (25–65% of particle).
- [x] **Critical Property:** Water absorption of RCA is **4–9%** vs ≤ 1% for NA.
- [x] **Mix Design Adjustment:** Effective water formula must include the **(+ RCA absorption, – NCA absorption)** correction term.
- [x] **IS Code Limit:** Maximum **20% replacement** for structural concrete up to M25 grade (IS 383:2016).
- [x] **Test Standards:** IS 2386 Part III (specific gravity, absorption), Part IV (ACV, AIV, LAA).
- [x] **Production Sequence:** Demolition → Pre-sort → Primary crusher → Magnet → Secondary crusher → Air classifier → Screen → Wash → Stockpile.
- [x] **Mitigation Strategies:** Pre-soaking, plasticisers, mineral admixtures (fly ash, GGBS, silica fume), and limiting replacement %.
- [x] **Sustainability Hook:** Use of RCA = reduced landfill burden, lower carbon footprint (15–20% reduction), conservation of natural quarry resources.
- [x] **Examiner Buzzwords:** Circular economy, urban mining, LEED credits, GRIHA rating, embodied carbon.
- [x] **Common Numerical Trap:** Volumetric vs. mass replacement — **always volumetric** unless specified otherwise.
- [x] **Formula Triad to Memorise:** $W_a$, $G_{ssd}$, $W_{eff}$ — these three cover 80% of KTU numerical questions.
- [x] **Expected Kerala-Specific Data:** RCA availability 40–60% cheaper than NCA in metro projects (Kochi/TVM/Calicut).
- [x] **Green Building Connection:** Mandatory mention of "sustainable development" and "circular economy" in conclusion paragraphs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycled Plastic Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recycled Plastic Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recycled Plastic Products (RPP)** in the context of modern construction technology refer to **engineered building components, structural elements, and finishing materials manufactured by reprocessing post-consumer or post-industrial plastic waste** (PET, HDPE, LDPE, PP, PVC, PS, and mixed polymer streams) through mechanical, thermal, or chemical recycling routes, converting them into value-added construction commodities such as bricks, blocks, lumber, roofing sheets, paver tiles, fencing posts, door/window frames, and geotextiles.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECET522)**, the scope of Recycled Plastic Products in Module 1 covers:
- Identification and segregation of waste plastics.
- Recycling codes (Resin Identification Codes 1 to 7).
- Processing techniques: **extrusion, injection moulding, compression moulding, and rotational moulding**.
- Engineered products substituting conventional materials (wood, brick, concrete).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standard:** The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) does not yet prescribe a single unified code for recycled plastic bricks; however, **IS 16700:2017**, **IS 14443:1997**, and **ASTM D638 / D695 / D790** are widely referenced for property verification. The **Swachh Bharat Mission** and **Smart Cities Mission** actively promote RPP in public infrastructure.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a mountain of empty water bottles, biscuit wrappers, milk pouches, and broken buckets discarded in a landfill. Individually, each item is worthless "trash" that persists for **450 to 1,000 years** without degrading. Now imagine feeding this entire mountain into a giant industrial shredder, melting the chips at **200 °C to 280 °C**, and reshaping the molten slurry into the profile of a **brick, a door frame, a fence post, or a park bench**. The "trash mountain" is reborn as durable, weather-resistant construction material.

In essence, **Recycled Plastic Products = Urban Waste Repurposed as Building Material**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of RPP as a **"second life"** of plastic. The polymer chains (long spaghetti-like molecules) are broken down but not destroyed; they are simply **re-melted and re-aligned** to serve a new structural or aesthetic purpose.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value for RPP | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | **0.90 – 1.40** | g/cm³ |
| Water Absorption | **< 1.0** | % |
| Compressive Strength $\sigma_c$ | **30 – 60** | MPa |
| Flexural Strength $\sigma_f$ | **20 – 45** | MPa |
| Service Life | **50 – 100** | years |
| Operating Temperature | **-40 to +80** | °C |
| Thermal Conductivity $k$ | **0.13 – 0.25** | W/m·K |
| Recyclability Index | **Infinite (closed-loop)** | cycles |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Recycled Plastics (Resin Identification Codes)

The **Resin Identification Code (RIC)** system, established by the **Society of the Plastics Industry (SPI)** in 1988, is the universal sorting key for plastic waste recovery.

| RIC | Polymer | Common Waste Source | Construction Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) | Water/soda bottles | Fiber reinforcement, roof sheathing |
| **2** | HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene) | Detergent cans, milk bottles | Pipes, lumber, marine structures |
| **3** | PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) | Pipes, window frames (recycled) | Conduit pipes, flooring, false ceiling |
| **4** | LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene) | Plastic bags, squeezable bottles | Vapor barriers, insulation sheets |
| **5** | PP (Polypropylene) | Bottle caps, straws | Geotextiles, paving tiles |
| **6** | PS (Polystyrene) | Foam cups, packaging | Lightweight wall panels, insulation |
| **7** | Others (Mixed, PC, ABS) | Mixed waste, electronics | Composite lumber, agglomerated bricks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RIC matters:** Sorting accuracy directly dictates the **mechanical homogeneity** of the final RPP. Mixed plastic streams (RIC 7) are typically used for **agglomerated bricks**, where uniformity is moderated by compression and additive binders.

## 2.2 Recycling Pathways

### A. Mechanical Recycling (Primary route for RPP)
1. **Collection** – from municipal solid waste (MSW), landfills, oceans.
2. **Sorting** – manual or AI-driven (by RIC).
3. **Washing** – removes organic contaminants, labels, adhesives.
4. **Shredding** – reduces to flakes of **5–10 mm**.
5. **Melting & Extrusion** – flakes pass through a heated barrel (Twin-Screw Extruder) at **180–280 °C**.
6. **Pelletization** – molten polymer is cooled and cut into uniform pellets.
7. **Re-moulding** – pellets are fed into injection/compression moulds.

### B. Chemical Recycling
- **Depolymerization** (hydrolysis, glycolysis, methanolysis) – breaks polymer back to monomers.
- Used to regenerate virgin-grade PET for high-purity applications.

### C. Thermal Recycling (Energy Recovery)
- **Pyrolysis** – converts plastic to fuel (not preferred for RPP).

## 2.3 Engineering Properties of Major RPP

### 2.3.1 Recycled Plastic Bricks
- Made from a mix of shredded plastic (60–80%) + sand/fly ash (20–40%) by mass.
- Compression-moulded at **120–180 °C** and **20–30 MPa** pressure.
- **Density ≈ 1.7–2.0 g/cm³** (similar to clay brick).
- **Compressive strength 5.2–25 MPa** (some reports up to **60 MPa**).

### 2.3.2 Recycled Plastic Lumber (RPL)
- Manufactured by extrusion of HDPE/PP waste.
- **Density 0.65–0.95 g/cm³** (lighter than timber).
- **Modulus of Elasticity E = 1.5–4.0 GPa** (timber: 8–12 GPa; concrete: 25–30 GPa).
- **Bending strength 15–35 MPa**.

### 2.3.3 Plastic Paver Tiles
- Mixture of shredded plastic + quarry dust + pigments.
- **Water absorption < 0.5%** (vs. 10–15% for concrete tiles).
- **Skid Resistance (μ) > 0.6** (IS requirement).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested formulas** in KTU University Examinations for this topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Bulk density of RPP | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Compressive strength (uniaxial load) | MPa, where $P$ = load (N), $A$ = area (mm²) |
| 3 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^{2}}$ | Flexural strength (3-point bend) | MPa, ASTM D790 |
| 4 | $W_{abs} = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | Water absorption percentage | IS 3495 (Part 2) |
| 5 | $\text{Porosity } n = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{V \cdot \rho_{water}} \times 100$ | Open porosity | % |
| 6 | $\text{Mix Ratio} = \dfrac{M_{plastic}}{M_{aggregate}}$ | Mass proportion in brick | ratio (e.g., 70:30) |
| 7 | $\text{Waste Reduction} = \dfrac{M_{plastic,recycled}}{M_{plastic,landfilled}} \times 100$ | Environmental metric | % |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | Thermal conductivity (Fourier's Law) | W/m·K |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus | GPa |
| 10 | $\text{Deflection } \delta = \dfrac{5wL^{4}}{384EI}$ | Beam deflection (UDL on RPL beam) | mm |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rural Housing**: Plastic bricks have been deployed in **50,000+ houses** across states like Kerala, Tamil Nadu, and Maharashtra under CSR-funded pilot projects.
- **Coastal Defense**: RPL is used in **docks, jetties, and seawalls** due to its zero corrosion rate in saline environments.
- **Urban Infrastructure**: Smart City projects use recycled plastic benches, fencing, and signage.
- **Road Sub-base**: Shredded plastic is mixed with bitumen (modified bitumen) for flexible pavement construction.
- **Disaster-Resilient Shelters**: Lightweight RPP panels reduce seismic mass and transport cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Compressive Strength Verification of a Plastic Brick

> A recycled plastic brick of dimensions **$230 \text{ mm} \times 110 \text{ mm} \times 75 \text{ mm}$** was tested in a Compression Testing Machine (CTM). The failure load recorded was **$485 \text{ kN}$**. Calculate the compressive strength and check against the acceptance criterion of IS 3495 (Part 1).

**Given Data**

$$L = 230 \text{ mm}, \quad B = 110 \text{ mm}, \quad H = 75 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_{failure} = 485 \text{ kN} = 485{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 1 – Calculate the cross-sectional area**

$$A = L \times B = 230 \times 110 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$A = 25{,}300 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate compressive strength using the governing formula**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A}$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{485{,}000}{25{,}300} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_c = 19.17 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 – Verification against IS 3495 acceptance criterion**

The **minimum characteristic compressive strength** for a Class-1 burnt clay brick (the most common comparator) is **10 MPa** (for a load-bearing brick). For plastic bricks, the BIS draft typically mandates a minimum of **7 MPa** for non-load-bearing use.

$$\sigma_{c,RPP} = 19.17 \text{ MPa} \gg 7 \text{ MPa} \;\; \text{(IS 16700:2017 draft)}$$

**Conclusion:** The brick **passes** the IS criterion with a safety factor of approximately **2.74×**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always show the unit conversion (kN → N, mm² → N/mm² = MPa). Losing **1 mark** for skipped unit conversion is a frequent KTU evaluation error.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Water Absorption Test (IS 3495 Part 2)

> A recycled plastic brick of mass **$W_{dry} = 1.825 \text{ kg}$** is fully immersed in water for **24 hours** at ambient temperature. The saturated mass is **$W_{sat} = 1.842 \text{ kg}$**. Calculate the water absorption percentage.

**Given Data**

$$W_{dry} = 1.825 \text{ kg}, \quad W_{sat} = 1.842 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 1 – Identify the governing equation**

$$W_{abs} = \frac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 – Substitute numerical values**

$$W_{abs} = \frac{1.842 - 1.825}{1.825} \times 100$$

$$W_{abs} = \frac{0.017}{1.825} \times 100$$

$$W_{abs} = 0.9315 \%$$

**Step 3 – Acceptance check**

As per **IS 3495 (Part 2)**, for Class-1 bricks, water absorption shall not exceed **15%**. For RPP, the standard is typically **< 5%**.

$$W_{abs} = 0.93\% \;\; < \;\; 5\% \;\; \text{(PASS)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exceptionally low water absorption of RPP (sub-1%) is a **major durability advantage** over burnt clay bricks, which typically exhibit **10–20%** absorption.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Mix Design of Plastic-Composite Brick

> Design a **1 kg** mix batch for a plastic-aggregate composite brick using **70% plastic, 25% quarry sand, 5% fly ash (by mass)**. If 5% binder additive is required as a percentage of the total dry mass, compute the mass of each component.

**Given Data**

$$M_{total} = 1 \text{ kg} = 1000 \text{ g}$$

$$\% \text{ Plastic} = 70\%, \quad \% \text{ Sand} = 25\%, \quad \% \text{ Fly Ash} = 5\%$$

$$\% \text{ Binder (additive)} = 5\% \text{ of total dry mass}$$

**Step 1 – Compute component masses (without binder)**

$$M_{plastic} = \frac{70}{100} \times 1000 = 700 \text{ g}$$

$$M_{sand} = \frac{25}{100} \times 1000 = 250 \text{ g}$$

$$M_{flyash} = \frac{5}{100} \times 1000 = 50 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 – Compute binder mass**

$$M_{binder} = \frac{5}{100} \times 1000 = 50 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 – Final mass distribution**

$$M_{plastic} = 700 \text{ g}, \quad M_{sand} = 250 \text{ g}, \quad M_{flyash} = 50 \text{ g}, \quad M_{binder} = 50 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Cumulative mass} = 700 + 250 + 50 + 50 = 1050 \text{ g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In production-scale mix design, the **5% binder** is typically an industrial grade **Maleic Anhydride Grafted Polypropylene (MAPP)** or **Epoxy-based coupling agent** to improve the interfacial bond between hydrophobic plastic and hydrophilic aggregate.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Deflection of a Recycled Plastic Lumber Beam

> A simply-supported RPL beam of length **$L = 3 \text{ m}$** with cross-section **$b = 80 \text{ mm}$**, **$d = 150 \text{ mm}$** carries a uniformly distributed load **$w = 4 \text{ kN/m}$**. The flexural rigidity is **$EI = 9.0 \text{ kN·m}^{2}$**. Compute the maximum deflection.

**Given Data**

$$L = 3 \text{ m}, \quad b = 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.150 \text{ m}$$

$$w = 4 \text{ kN/m}, \quad EI = 9.0 \text{ kN·m}^{2}$$

**Step 1 – Apply the standard UDL deflection formula**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 w L^{4}}{384 \, E I}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute the values**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 4 \times (3)^{4}}{384 \times 9.0}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 4 \times 81}{3456}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{1620}{3456}$$

$$\delta_{max} = 0.4688 \text{ m} = 468.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 – Compare with IS 800 / IS 456 serviceability limit**

Allowable deflection for beams is generally **$L/250$** to **$L/360$**.

$$\frac{L}{360} = \frac{3000}{360} = 8.33 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_{max} = 468.75 \text{ mm} \;\; \gg \;\; 8.33 \text{ mm} \;\; \text{(FAIL)}$$

**Conclusion:** The RPL beam as designed is **unsuitable for structural load-bearing use** under this load. Either (a) the section depth must be increased, (b) higher-modulus material (glass-fiber reinforced RPL) must be used, or (c) the span must be reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> RPL is **NOT a direct replacement for steel or reinforced concrete** in primary structural elements. Its low Young's modulus restricts use to **non-structural and semi-structural applications** (handrails, fencing, bench slats, facades).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: RPP vs. Conventional Materials

| Property | Recycled Plastic Brick | Clay Brick | Concrete Block | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.7 – 2.0 | 1.8 – 2.0 | 1.9 – 2.3 | Comparable |
| Compressive Strength (MPa) | 5 – 25 | 3.5 – 10 | 4 – 12 | RPP superior |
| Water Absorption (%) | < 1.0 | 10 – 20 | 6 – 15 | RPP vastly superior |
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.60 – 0.80 | 0.70 – 1.30 | RPP is a better insulator |
| Embodied Energy (MJ/kg) | 15 – 30 | 4 – 6 | 1.5 – 3.0 | RPP higher, but offset by waste avoidance |
| Carbon Footprint (kg CO₂/kg) | 0.3 – 0.8 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.1 – 0.2 | RPP higher in production, lower in waste management |
| Service Life (years) | 50 – 100 | 100+ | 80 – 100 | Acceptable for non-critical use |
| Cost (INR/unit, 2024) | ₹ 18 – 30 | ₹ 8 – 15 | ₹ 25 – 45 | RPP is mid-range |
| Recyclability | Yes (closed-loop) | No (downcycled) | Partial (crushed aggregate) | RPP = closed-loop circular economy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A: Process Flow — From Plastic Waste to Recycled Construction Product

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Plastic Waste Collection] --> B[Step2: Manual and Automated Sorting by RIC Code]
    B --> C[Step3: Washing and Contaminant Removal]
    C --> D[Step4: Shredding into 5-10 mm Flakes]
    D --> E[Step5: Melting in Twin-Screw Extruder at 200-280 °C]
    E --> F[Step6: Filtration and Degassing]
    F --> G[Step7: Pelletization into Uniform Granules]
    G --> H{Step8: End Product Selection}
    H --> I[Step8a: Compression Moulding into Plastic Bricks]
    H --> J[Step8b: Profile Extrusion into Plastic Lumber]
    H --> K[Step8c: Injection Moulding into Paver Tiles]
    H --> L[Step8d: Sheet Extrusion into Roofing Panels]
    I --> M[Step9: Quality Testing per IS and ASTM Standards]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Step10: Approved Recycled Plastic Product for Construction]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Construction Application Hierarchy of RPP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plastic Bricks] --> P1A[Non-load-bearing Wall Construction]
    P1 --> P1B[Partition Walls and Infill Panels]
    P1 --> P1C[Pavements and Garden Edging]
    P2[Plastic Lumber RPL] --> P2A[Outdoor Furniture and Benches]
    P2 --> P2B[Park Fencing and Signage Posts]
    P2 --> P2C[Dock and Jetty Decking]
    P3[Plastic Paver Tiles] --> P3A[Walkways and Footpaths]
    P3 --> P3B[Parking Lots and Driveways]
    P3 --> P3C[Terrace and Balcony Flooring]
    P4[Plastic Roofing Sheets] --> P4A[Shed Roofs and Canopies]
    P4 --> P4B[Low-cost Rural Housing]
    P4 --> P4C[Greenhouse Structures]
    P5[Plastic Pipes] --> P5A[Non-pressure Sewerage]
    P5 --> P5B[Drainage and Sub-soil Systems]
    P5 --> P5C[Cable Conduits]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram C: Quality Control Workflow for RPP Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QC1[Raw Material Inspection] --> QC2[RIC Verification by FTIR Spectroscopy]
    QC2 --> QC3[Moisture Content Analysis less than 0.2 percent]
    QC3 --> QC4[Process Monitoring: Temperature, Pressure, Cycle Time]
    QC4 --> QC5[In-Process Density Check via Archimedes Method]
    QC5 --> QC6[Finished Product Sampling]
    QC6 --> QC7[Compressive Strength Test per ASTM D695]
    QC6 --> QC8[Water Absorption Test per IS 3495 Part 2]
    QC6 --> QC9[Flexural Test per ASTM D790]
    QC6 --> QC10[UV Exposure and Weathering Test per ASTM G155]
    QC7 --> QC11[Statistical Quality Control and Lot Release]
    QC8 --> QC11
    QC9 --> QC11
    QC10 --> QC11
    QC11 --> QC12[Approved Lot Dispatched to Construction Site]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram D: Circular Economy Loop for Plastic in Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR1[Virgin Plastic Production] --> CR2[Plastic Product Usage by Consumer]
    CR2 --> CR3[End-of-Life Plastic Waste]

    CR5 --> CR6[Manufacture of Recycled Plastic Construction Product]
    CR6 --> CR7[Construction Site Deployment]

```
