# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1.b]`

**Define recycled plastic products. List any four construction applications of recycled plastics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Recycled plastic products are construction materials manufactured by reprocessing post-consumer and post-industrial plastic waste through mechanical, thermal, or chemical recycling methods, transforming them into value-added building components.

> **Four Construction Applications (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Plastic bricks** for non-load-bearing wall construction.
> 2. **Recycled plastic lumber (RPL)** for fencing, park benches, and marine decking.
> 3. **Plastic paver tiles** for walkways, driveways, and terrace flooring.
> 4. **Recycled HDPE/PVC pipes** for non-pressure sewerage, drainage, and cable conduits.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q2.a]`

**Explain the difference between mechanical recycling and chemical recycling of plastic waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Recycling | Chemical Recycling |
|---|---|---|
| **Process** | Physical shredding, melting, and re-moulding | Depolymerization to monomers (hydrolysis, glycolysis, methanolysis) |
| **Output Quality** | Slightly degraded properties | Virgin-grade polymer recovery |
| **Energy Demand** | Low to moderate | High |
| **Applicability** | Homogeneous single-polymer waste (PET, HDPE) | Mixed or contaminated plastic streams |
| **Cost (INR/tonne)** | ₹ 8,000 – 15,000 | ₹ 25,000 – 60,000 |

> **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Mechanical recycling dominates the **construction RPP** market due to its low cost and operational simplicity, while chemical recycling is reserved for high-purity applications like food-grade packaging.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, marks are awarded as: **Theory 5 + Diagram/Flowchart 3 + Standards/Data 2 + Comparison/Application 2 + Conclusion 2**. Students typically lose marks for **missing neat diagrams** and **failing to cite specific IS/ASTM standards** by number.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q4]`

**(a)** Explain the various types of recycled plastic products used in modern construction. Describe the resin identification code system in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Recycled plastic products (RPPs) are categorized based on the source polymer, identified by the **Resin Identification Code (RIC)** developed by the Society of the Plastics Industry (SPI) in 1988.

**Types of RPPs (5 marks):**

1. **Plastic Bricks (1 mark):** Compression-moulded from a mix of shredded plastic (PET, HDPE, PP) and fine aggregate (sand, quarry dust, fly ash). Standard brick size: $230 \times 110 \times 75$ mm. Compressive strength: **5–25 MPa**. Water absorption: **< 1%**. Used for non-load-bearing walls and infill panels.

2. **Recycled Plastic Lumber (1 mark):** Extruded profile of HDPE/PP waste. Density: **0.65–0.95 g/cm³**. Used for park benches, fencing, sign posts, and marine decking due to its **zero corrosion** in saline environments.

3. **Plastic Paver Tiles (1 mark):** Compression-moulded tiles with plastic-aggregate mix. Available in $200 \times 200$ mm, $300 \times 300$ mm, and $400 \times 400$ mm. Skid resistance $\mu > 0.6$.

4. **Plastic Roofing Sheets (1 mark):** Corrugated or flat sheets extruded from recycled HDPE. Light weight (1.5–3.0 kg/m²), UV-stabilized. Used for sheds, low-cost housing, and greenhouses.

5. **Plastic Pipes and Conduits (1 mark):** Recycled PVC and HDPE pipes for non-pressure applications. Service life: 50+ years.

**RIC System (1 mark):** Codes 1 (PET), 2 (HDPE), 3 (PVC), 4 (LDPE), 5 (PP), 6 (PS), 7 (Others/Mixed). RIC enables sorting accuracy that determines final RPP quality.

---

**(b)** Discuss the manufacturing process of plastic bricks with a neat flowchart. List their advantages over conventional burnt clay bricks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Manufacturing Process (4 marks):**

| Stage | Description | Temperature/Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Collection | Waste plastic from MSW, landfills | Ambient |
| 2. Sorting | Manual sorting by RIC; magnetic separation of metal contaminants | Ambient |
| 3. Shredding | Granulator reduces to 5–10 mm flakes | Ambient |
| 4. Washing | Caustic soda wash (NaOH 1–2%) to remove grease/labels | 60–80 °C |
| 5. Drying | Centrifugal + hot air dryer | 80–100 °C |
| 6. Mixing | Plastic flakes + sand + fly ash + binder (MAPP) in sigma mixer | 180 °C |
| 7. Moulding | Hydraulic compression press | 120–180 °C, 20–30 MPa |
| 8. Cooling | Air-cooled for 30–60 min | Ambient |
| 9. Demoulding & Curing | Stack in open yard for 24 h | Ambient |

**Flowchart (1 mark):** *Reference Section 4.1 Diagram A above.*

**Advantages over Clay Bricks (2 marks — 0.25 each):**
1. **Lower water absorption** (< 1% vs. 10–20% for clay bricks).
2. **Higher compressive strength** (up to 25 MPa vs. 3.5–10 MPa).
3. **No firing required** → saves fuel and reduces emissions.
4. **Faster production cycle** (hours vs. days for kiln-fired bricks).
5. **Consistent dimensions** (no warping).
6. **Utilizes urban waste** → supports Swachh Bharat Mission.
7. **Reusable/recyclable** at end-of-life.
8. **Lighter weight** → reduces structural dead load.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q5]`

**(a)** Describe the properties and engineering characteristics of recycled plastic lumber. Compare it with conventional timber for at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Recycled Plastic Lumber (RPL) is a wood-substitute lumber manufactured by extruding or compression-moulding post-consumer and post-industrial thermoplastic waste (primarily HDPE, PP, and mixed polymer streams) into rectangular or profiled cross-sections.

**Properties of RPL (3 marks):**

| Property | RPL (HDPE-based) | Conventional Timber (Teak) |
|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 0.65 – 0.95 | 0.55 – 0.85 |
| Water Absorption (%) | < 0.5 | 8 – 25 |
| Termite Resistance | Excellent (immune) | Poor (vulnerable) |
| Fungal Decay | Resistant | High susceptibility |
| Modulus of Elasticity (GPa) | 1.5 – 4.0 | 8 – 12 |
| Bending Strength (MPa) | 15 – 35 | 50 – 100 |
| UV Resistance | Moderate (needs stabilizer) | Good with coating |
| Service Life (years) | 50+ (no maintenance) | 15 – 25 (with treatment) |
| Recyclability | Closed-loop | Downcycled / burned |

**Comparison (3 marks):**
1. **Moisture Behaviour:** RPL absorbs < 0.5% water, while timber absorbs 8–25% → RPL does not warp, swell, or rot.
2. **Biological Resistance:** RPL is immune to termites, borers, and fungal decay. Timber requires periodic chemical treatment (CCA, creosote).
3. **Maintenance:** RPL needs zero painting/sealing; timber requires annual maintenance.
4. **Dimensional Stability:** RPL retains its dimensions over decades; timber expands/contracts with humidity.
5. **Environmental Impact:** RPL diverts plastic from landfills; timber harvesting contributes to deforestation.
6. **Structural Capacity:** Timber has higher bending strength and stiffness; RPL is restricted to **non-structural** applications.

---

**(b)** Explain the testing procedures and quality control measures for recycled plastic products as per relevant IS and ASTM standards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Importance of Quality Control (0.5 mark):** RPPs are heterogeneous due to variable feedstock; rigorous QC ensures structural and durability performance.

**Standard Test Methods (6 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Compressive Strength (ASTM D695 / IS 3495 Part 1):**
   Specimen: $50 \times 50 \times 50$ mm cube or full brick.
   Loading rate: **1.3 mm/min** until failure.
   Report: $\sigma_c = P/A$ in MPa.

2. **Water Absorption (IS 3495 Part 2 / ASTM D570):**
   Specimen oven-dried at 100–110 °C for 24 h, weighed ($W_{dry}$), immersed in water for 24 h, re-weighed ($W_{sat}$).
   Formula: $W_{abs} = (W_{sat} - W_{dry})/W_{dry} \times 100\%$.
   Acceptance: < 5% for RPP.

3. **Flexural Strength (ASTM D790, 3-point bend):**
   Specimen: rectangular prism, span-to-depth ratio = 16:1.
   Formula: $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bd^2)$.
   Acceptance: ≥ 20 MPa for RPL.

4. **Tensile Strength (ASTM D638):**
   Dumbbell-shaped specimen, pulled at 5 mm/min.
   Report: Ultimate tensile strength (MPa) and elongation (%).

5. **Density (ASTM D792, Archimedes Method):**
   Specimen weighed in air and water.
   Formula: $\rho = W_{air}/(W_{air} - W_{water}) \times \rho_{water}$.

6. **Accelerated Weathering / UV Resistance (ASTM G155):**
   Specimen exposed to xenon-arc lamp for 1,000 h simulating 5 years of outdoor exposure.
   Acceptance: < 10% loss in strength or surface chalking.

7. **Impact Resistance (ASTM D256, Izod):**
   Pendulum impact test; report energy absorbed (J/m).

**Statistical Quality Control (0.5 mark):** Lot sampling as per **IS 2500** (sampling inspection tables), with AQL of 4% for major defects and 6.5% for minor defects.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-hour revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** RPP = post-consumer plastic waste reprocessed into construction commodities.
- ✅ **RIC Codes 1–7:** PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS, Mixed.
- ✅ **Primary Recycling Route:** Mechanical (shred → wash → melt → mould).
- ✅ **Top 3 Construction Products:** Plastic bricks, RPL, paver tiles.
- ✅ **Density of RPP:** 0.9 – 1.4 g/cm³ (lightweight).
- ✅ **Compressive strength of RPP bricks:** 5 – 25 MPa.
- ✅ **Water absorption:** < 1% (vs. 10–20% for clay bricks).
- ✅ **Service life:** 50 – 100 years.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** IS 3495, IS 16700, ASTM D695, D790, D638, D570.
- ✅ **Mix design ratio for bricks:** 70% plastic : 30% sand/fly ash.
- ✅ **Compression moulding parameters:** 120–180 °C, 20–30 MPa.
- ✅ **Major Advantage:** Waste diversion, durability, low water absorption.
- ✅ **Major Limitation:** Low Young's modulus → **non-structural** use only.
- ✅ **Safety criterion:** $\sigma_c \geq 7$ MPa for non-load-bearing RPP bricks.
- ✅ **Recyclability:** Closed-loop; RPP can be re-recycled at end-of-life.
- ✅ **Government Initiatives:** Swachh Bharat Mission, Smart Cities Mission, GOBAR-DHAN scheme.
- ✅ **Formula to remember:** $W_{abs} = (W_{sat} - W_{dry})/W_{dry} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Formula to remember:** $\sigma_c = P/A$.
- ✅ **Application areas:** Walls, pavements, fencing, marine decking, drainage pipes, roofing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always cite a specific **IS code number** (e.g., IS 3495) and a specific **ASTM standard** (e.g., ASTM D695) when answering KTU questions. Citing standards is a **2-mark value-addition** that examiners explicitly look for.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustainable Concretes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sustainable Concretes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sustainable Concrete** (also termed *Green Concrete*) is defined as a class of engineered cementitious composites manufactured by partially or fully replacing conventional Portland cement and natural aggregates with industrial by-products, recycled materials, or waste streams, in order to reduce the embodied carbon, energy consumption, and depletion of natural resources, while maintaining or enhancing the structural and durability performance of the hardened product over its entire service life.

The hierarchy of sustainability in concrete technology follows a three-tier framework recognized by **ASTM C595**, **EN 197-1**, and **IS 16714:2018**:

1. **Material Substitution Level** — Replacement of cement with Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs).
2. **Structural Efficiency Level** — High-strength, high-durability mixes that reduce member cross-section.
3. **Life-Cycle Level** — Carbonation uptake, recyclability, and end-of-life recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embodied Carbon of Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC):**
> Manufacturing **1 tonne of OPC releases approximately 0.83 – 0.95 tonnes of CO₂** into the atmosphere. The cement industry alone contributes **7 % – 8 %** of global anthropogenic CO₂ emissions (Source: *Chatham House Report, 2018*; *Global Cement and Concrete Association, 2021*).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of conventional concrete as a **"single-use plastic bottle"** — it works perfectly, but its raw material extraction (limestone mining for clinker) and manufacturing process leave a heavy carbon residue on the planet.

**Sustainable concrete is the "reusable, recycled water bottle"** — it accomplishes the same job (carrying loads, providing shelter), but it is fabricated by:
- Reusing industrial "leftovers" (fly ash from thermal power plants, slag from steel mills, silica fume from silicon industries).
- Reincarnating demolition debris (crushed concrete as recycled coarse aggregate).
- Eliminating the cement kiln altogether (geopolymer binders activated by alkaline solutions).

In essence, sustainable concrete converts **waste liabilities** (industrial by-products) into **structural assets** (durable, high-performance building materials).

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Drivers for Sustainable Concrete Adoption (KTU 2024 PEO Alignment):**
> - **Environmental:** Reduction of CO₂ footprint, landfill diversion, conservation of virgin aggregates.
> - **Economic:** Lower material cost (waste SCMs are cheaper than OPC), longer service life reducing maintenance.
> - **Social:** Improved occupational health (less quarry dust), compliance with **UN-SDG 9, 11, and 13**.
> - **Regulatory:** Compliance with **IGBC / GRIHA / LEED** rating systems mandated in Indian public infrastructure tenders.

## 1.3 Classification of Sustainable Concretes

| **Category** | **Key Material Innovation** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|
| High-Volume Fly Ash Concrete (HVFAC) | 50 % – 60 % OPC replaced with Class-F fly ash | Mass concrete, dams, raft foundations |
| Ground Granulated Blast-Furnace Slag (GGBFS) Concrete | 30 % – 70 % slag replacement | Marine structures, sulfate-rich environments |
| Silica Fume Concrete | 5 % – 12 % silica fume | High-strength columns, bridges |
| Geopolymer Concrete | 100 % OPC eliminated; alkali-activated fly ash / slag | Precast elements, industrial floors |
| Recycled Aggregate Concrete (RAC) | 20 % – 100 % Recycled Coarse Aggregate (RCA) | Non-structural to medium-grade structural members |
| Pervious / Porous Concrete | Single-sized aggregate, minimal fines | Pavements, parking lots, drainage layers |
| Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC) | High powder content + superplasticizers | Congested reinforcement, architectural concrete |
| Lightweight Concrete (LWC) | Lightweight aggregates (LWA), foamed mortar | Partition walls, long-span decks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Triple Bottom Line of Sustainable Concrete

Sustainable concrete must simultaneously satisfy the **3P Paradigm**:

- **Planet (Environmental):** Lower embodied carbon, reduced virgin resource extraction, minimized landfill burden.
- **Profit (Economic):** Equivalent or lower life-cycle cost (LCC) when durability and carbon-tax penalties are accounted for.
- **People (Social):** Safer work environment, enhanced occupant comfort, equitable resource distribution.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanism of Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs)

SCMs function via two complementary physico-chemical pathways:

### (a) Pozzolanic Reaction
Latent siliceous/alumino-siliceous materials (fly ash, silica fume, metakaolin) react with **calcium hydroxide [Ca(OH)₂]** liberated during cement hydration to form additional **Calcium-Silicate-Hydrate (C–S–H)** gel.

$$
\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C–S–H gel (secondary)}
$$

### (b) Latent Hydraulic Reaction
Slag-based SCMs (GGBFS) self-hydrate in the presence of an alkaline activator (e.g., Ca(OH)₂ from OPC) to form C–S–H and Calcium-Aluminate-Hydrate (C–A–H) directly, without requiring external pozzolan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SCMs reduce CO₂:**
> - They dilute the clinker factor (tonnes of CO₂ per tonne of binder drops from ~0.93 for OPC to ~0.15 – 0.30 for blended binders).
> - They consume Ca(OH)₂, which is a greenhouse-neutral by-product of cement hydration, converting it into load-bearing C–S–H.
> - They refine the pore structure, reducing chloride/sulfate ingress and extending service life.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula (LaTeX)** | **Symbol Glossary** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| $f_c \;=\; \dfrac{A}{B^{\,(w/c)}}$ | $f_c$ = compressive strength (MPa); $A, B$ = empirical constants (typically $A \approx 96$ MPa, $B \approx 27$ for OPC) | **Abrams' Law** — predicts compressive strength from water-to-cement ratio |
| $SAI \;=\; \dfrac{f_{SCM}}{f_{ctrl}} \times 100$ | $SAI$ = Strength Activity Index (%, per ASTM C311); $f_{SCM}$ = strength of mortar with 20 % SCM replacement; $f_{ctrl}$ = control OPC mortar strength (same w/cm) | Quantifies efficiency of fly ash, slag, or silica fume replacement |
| $CO_{2,\,saved} \;=\; W_c \cdot r \cdot e_c$ | $W_c$ = OPC mass (kg); $r$ = replacement fraction; $e_c$ = CO₂ emission factor of OPC ($\approx 0.93$ kg CO₂ / kg OPC) | Direct computation of carbon reduction for a given mix |
| $f_t \;=\; 0.55 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | $f_t$ = split tensile strength (MPa); $f_{ck}$ = characteristic cube strength (MPa) | Used for mix optimization and durability checks |
| $E_c \;=\; 5000 \cdot \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | $E_c$ = Young's Modulus of concrete (MPa); $f_{ck}$ in MPa | **IS 456:2000** elastic stiffness estimation |
| $\rho_{pack} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_i \cdot \phi_i$ | $\rho_{pack}$ = packing density; $\beta_i$ = volume fraction of phase $i$; $\phi_i$ = packing factor of phase $i$ | Optimizes aggregate grading for sustainable mixes |
| $D_{carbonation} \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | $D$ = carbonation depth (mm); $k$ = carbonation coefficient (mm/√year); $t$ = exposure time (years) | **Carbonation uptake (carbon sink) estimation** — natural CO₂ absorption in service |
| $\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\mu$ = plastic viscosity; $\tau$ = shear stress; $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate | Rheological design of SCC and HVFAC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Abrams' Law:**
> - Valid for $0.40 \leq w/c \leq 0.80$ under standard curing (27 °C, 95 % RH).
> - Constants $A$ and $B$ are **mix-specific** and must be re-calibrated for blended cements, mineral admixtures, or chemical admixtures.
> - For HVFAC mixes, modified form $f_c = A/(B^{(w/b)})$ is used, where $w/b$ = water-to-binder ratio.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carbon-tax Compliance (India):** Under the proposed **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) 2023**, every tonne of CO₂ abated earns tradable credits (~$5 – 30 / tCO₂).
- **Infrastructure Tendering:** NHAI and MoHUA mandate **at least 20 % – 30 % fly ash blending** in all central-government-funded road and housing projects (per **CPWD specifications 2019**).
- **Smart City Mission:** LEED-rated buildings require 10 % – 20 % recycled content, achievable only via sustainable concrete technology.
- **Marine & Coastal Engineering:** GGBFS-blended concrete extends service life in chloride-rich environments by **2× – 4×** compared to OPC, reducing life-cycle cost.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Embodied CO₂ Reduction for a Blended Cement Mix

### Problem Statement
A structural concrete mix uses **400 kg of binder per m³**, comprising **30 % fly ash + 70 % OPC** (Grade 43). Compute:
1. The embodied CO₂ per m³ of this mix.
2. The CO₂ savings compared to a 100 % OPC mix.
3. The percentage reduction in carbon footprint.

### Given Data
- OPC CO₂ emission factor, $e_c = 0.93 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{kg OPC}$ (industry-accepted)
- Fly ash CO₂ emission factor, $e_f = 0.027 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{kg FA}$ (negligible — a waste product)
- Total binder, $W_b = 400 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Fly ash replacement fraction, $r = 0.30$
- OPC fraction, $(1 - r) = 0.70$

### Step 1 — Quantify the mass of each binder component

$$
W_{OPC} = W_b \times (1 - r) = 400 \times 0.70 = 280 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

$$
W_{FA} = W_b \times r = 400 \times 0.30 = 120 \;\text{kg/m}^3
$$

*Logic:* A 30 % replacement by mass of OPC means 30 % of the 400 kg binder is fly ash.

### Step 2 — Compute CO₂ emissions of each component

$$
CO_{2,\,OPC} = W_{OPC} \times e_c = 280 \times 0.93 = 260.4 \;\text{kg CO}_2
$$

$$
CO_{2,\,FA} = W_{FA} \times e_f = 120 \times 0.027 = 3.24 \;\text{kg CO}_2
$$

*Logic:* Multiply each component's mass by its specific CO₂ emission factor. Fly ash is treated as a diverted waste, hence its emission factor is minimal (only grinding, transport).

### Step 3 — Total embodied CO₂ of the blended mix

$$
CO_{2,\,blend} = CO_{2,\,OPC} + CO_{2,\,FA} = 260.4 + 3.24 = 263.64 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{m}^3
$$

*Logic:* Sum the partial CO₂ contributions of each constituent. Aggregates and water have negligible CO₂ (~0.005 kg/kg) and are usually neglected in tier-1 calculations.

### Step 4 — CO₂ of equivalent 100 % OPC mix (baseline)

$$
CO_{2,\,OPC\,mix} = W_b \times e_c = 400 \times 0.93 = 372.0 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{m}^3
$$

*Logic:* Replace the fly ash mass with OPC of the same quantity and recompute.

### Step 5 — Absolute CO₂ savings

$$
CO_{2,\,saved} = CO_{2,\,OPC\,mix} - CO_{2,\,blend} = 372.0 - 263.64 = 108.36 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{m}^3
$$

*Logic:* The difference between the baseline and blended mixes is the abatement.

### Step 6 — Percentage reduction

$$
\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{CO_{2,\,saved}}{CO_{2,\,OPC\,mix}} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{108.36}{372.0} \times 100 = 29.13 \;\%
$$

### Verification
The result aligns with the industry heuristic: **each 10 % fly ash replacement reduces CO₂ by ~9 % – 10 %**. Hence 30 % replacement → ~29 % reduction ✓.

### Final Result Summary

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| OPC mass | **280 kg / m³** |
| Fly ash mass | **120 kg / m³** |
| Blended mix CO₂ | **263.64 kg CO₂ / m³** |
| OPC baseline CO₂ | **372.0 kg CO₂ / m³** |
| CO₂ saved | **108.36 kg CO₂ / m³** |
| Reduction | **≈ 29.13 %** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Service-Life Extension via Carbonation (C–S–H Conversion)

### Step 1 — Carbonation front kinetics

Atmospheric CO₂ diffuses into concrete and reacts with C–S–H and Ca(OH)₂:

$$
\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 2 — Depth of carbonation vs time

$$
D_{carbonation} = k \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

### Step 3 — CO₂ absorbed per m² of exposed surface

$$
\text{CO}_2^{absorbed} = D_{carbonation} \cdot \rho_c \cdot \eta \cdot M_{CO_2 / CaO}
$$

where
- $\rho_c$ = concrete density (≈ 2400 kg/m³)
- $\eta$ = degree of carbonation efficiency (≈ 0.3 – 0.5)
- $M_{CO_2 / CaO}$ = molar mass ratio $= 44 / 56 = 0.7857$

### Step 4 — Numerical example

For a 50-year-old structure with $k = 1.5$ mm/√year:

$$
D = 1.5 \times \sqrt{50} = 10.61 \;\text{mm}
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2^{absorbed} = 0.01061 \times 2400 \times 0.40 \times 0.7857 = 8.0 \;\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{m}^2
$$

*Logic:* This represents the **carbon sink** effect — concrete re-absorbs ~8 kg of CO₂ per m² of exposed surface over 50 years, partially offsetting initial embodied emissions.

---

| **Selection Criterion** | **HVFAC** | **GGBFS Concrete** | **Geopolymer** | **Recycled Aggregate** | **Pervious** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ reduction | 25 % – 35 % | 30 % – 50 % | 60 % – 80 % | 15 % – 25 % | 20 % – 30 % |
| 28-day strength | Moderate | High | High | Moderate | Low – Moderate |
| Long-term strength gain | High | Very high | High | Moderate | Negligible |
| Cost premium | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Low |
| Durability in marine | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate | Poor |
| Carbonation resistance | Low | High | High | Moderate | Very Low |
| Recommended application | Mass concrete | Marine / sulfate | Industrial precast | Non-structural | Pavement |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Exam Heuristic (KTU 2024):**
> - *Dam / mass concrete* → **HVFAC**
> - *Coastal bridge* → **GGBFS concrete**
> - *Carbon-neutral target* → **Geopolymer**
> - *Sustainable urban pavement* → **Pervious concrete**
> - *Demolition reuse* → **Recycled Aggregate Concrete**
