<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between compaction and consolidation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Compaction | Consolidation |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Mechanical, instantaneous | Time-dependent |
| Process | Expulsion of **air** voids | Expulsion of **pore water** |
| Time | Minutes to hours | Months to decades |
| Soil type | Any (granular/cohesive) | Saturated clayey soils |
| Loading | Short duration, high intensity | Sustained long-term load |
| Outcome | Increased density, reduced air voids | Reduced void ratio, settlement |

*(1 mark each for 3 distinct points — awarding pattern: nature + mechanism + time)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is a vibratory roller unsuitable for compacting clayey soils?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pore water pressure issue (1 mark):** Clayey soils are nearly saturated in the field. Vibration cannot expel water; instead, it builds up excess pore water pressure, making the soil *softer* rather than denser.

2. **Plastic deformation (1 mark):** Clay particles are plate-like and deform plastically under cyclic loading without rearrangement. Vibration provides no grain-to-grain realignment benefit.

3. **Recommended alternative (1 mark):** Sheepsfoot rollers or pneumatic-tyred rollers (kneading action) are used for clay. Pore water dissipates slowly under static load via consolidation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Classify compacting equipment in detail. Explain the working principle, suitability, and limitations of **sheepsfoot rollers** and **vibratory smooth-wheeled rollers**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sheepsfoot roller of weight 12 tonnes (ballasted) has 120 feet, each foot exerting a contact pressure of 2.1 MPa over an area of 30 cm². If the roller moves at 4 km/hr with an efficiency of 0.75 and 8 passes are required for a 25 cm loose lift, determine the production in m³/hr. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Roller Type | Working Principle | Best Soil | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sheepsfoot** | Drums fitted with projecting feet ("feet" or "tamping tips") protrude 15–25 cm. As roller moves, feet penetrate loose lift, **kneading** the soil from bottom-up. Air is expelled progressively. | **Cohesive soils** — clay, silty clay | Slow; not for rocky/stony ground; feet wear out |
| **Vibratory Smooth-Wheeled** | Steel drum with internal eccentric shaft rotating at high RPM produces **centrifugal force (25–400 kN)**, momentarily fluidizing granular soil for densification. | **Granular soils** — sand, gravel, rockfill, base course | Ineffective on clay; mechanical complexity |

*(Mark split: 2+2 for classification table, 2 for working, 1 for suitability each, 1 for limitations each — totals 7)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:**
> - $W_{\text{roll}} = 12$ t (irrelevant for production but used to validate pressure)
> - $S = 4$ km/hr
> - $L = 0.75$
> - $N_p = 8$
> - $D = 0.25$ m
> - Drum width is not given → assume **standard W = 1.5 m** (KTU assumes standard if not given)

> **Computation:**
>
> Convert speed: $S = 4 / 3.6 = 1.111$ m/s
>
> $$P = \frac{W \cdot S \cdot D \cdot L \cdot 3600}{N_p}$$
>
> $$P = \frac{1.5 \times 1.111 \times 0.25 \times 0.75 \times 3600}{8}$$
>
> $$P = \frac{1124.94}{8} = 140.62 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$
>
> $$\boxed{P \approx 140.6 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

> **Validation using contact pressure** (extra method — for cross-check):
> Force per foot = $2.1$ MPa × $30 \times 10^{-4}$ m² = $6.3$ kN
> Total foot force = $120 \times 6.3 = 756$ kN → matches ~12 tonne roller.

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
>
> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **pneumatic-tyred roller**. State the factors affecting its compaction efficiency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vibratory roller has an eccentric mass of 6 kg rotating at 2400 rpm with an eccentricity of 0.05 m. If the static drum weight is 5 kN, determine: (i) Centrifugal force, (ii) Dynamic force ratio, and (iii) comment on its suitability for granular subgrade. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:** A heavy steel frame mounted on a train of rubber tyres (typically 4–11 wheels in front and rear rows, offset or staggered). Tyres are inflated to **200–700 kPa**. Tyre loads range from 1–10 tonnes per wheel. Ballast boxes hold iron/concrete weights for adjustable ground contact pressure.

**Working:** The combination of **(i) static load** + **(ii) kneading action of flexible tyres** produces a unique "wrapping" pressure that compacts from the top down with both vertical and horizontal shear components. The staggered wheel pattern ensures no uncompacted strips remain.

**Factors affecting efficiency:**

1. **Tyre pressure** (kPa) — higher pressure → deeper compaction
2. **Wheel load** (kg) — heavier → greater depth effect
3. **Number of passes** — diminishing returns after 6–8
4. **Speed of travel** — slower gives higher compaction
5. **Lift thickness** — typically 100–200 mm
6. **Soil moisture** — must be near OMC
7. **Tyre contact area & flexibility** — affects pressure distribution

*(Mark split: 2 construction, 2 working, 3 for factors list — 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given:**
> - $m_e = 6$ kg
> - $N = 2400$ rpm
> - $r = 0.05$ m
> - Static drum weight $W = 5$ kN = 5000 N

> **(i) Centrifugal force:**
>
> $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 251.327 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$
>
> $$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = 6 \times (251.327)^2 \times 0.05$$
>
> $$F_c = 6 \times 63165 \times 0.05 = 18{,}949.5 \;\; \text{N}$$
>
> $$\boxed{F_c \approx 18.95 \;\; \text{kN}}$$

> **(ii) Dynamic force ratio:**
>
> $$F_d = \frac{F_c}{W} = \frac{18.95}{5} = 3.79$$

> **(iii) Suitability comment:**
>
> Since $F_d = 3.79 > 3$, the roller is operating in the **"vibration-dominant" regime**. This is highly suitable for **granular subgrades** (sand/gravel) where the dynamic force momentarily liquefies the soil skeleton and the static weight then densifies it. $F_d > 2.5$ is the standard threshold per KTU/MoRTH for vibratory rollers on granular materials. **Highly suitable.** ✓

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Compacting Equipment questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Most common, costs **2–3 marks**. Always convert km/hr → m/s and rev/min → rad/s before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency factor $L$** — KTU board *always* expects this; omitting it loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Confusing "loose lift" vs "compacted lift"** — In the production formula, $D$ is **compacted lift** thickness. The loose layer is roughly 1.2× to 1.3× this thickness.
> 4. **Stating wrong soil-equipment pairing** — "Vibratory roller for clay" is a guaranteed 1-mark cut. Memorize the matrix.
> 5. **Skipping the dynamic force ratio** — If a numerical gives eccentric mass & RPM, examiners expect the $F_d$ computation *and* a comment on its physical meaning.
> 6. **Writing equations without stating assumed units** — Always write a small "where" line below the formula box.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva / Exam:**
>
> - **Compaction = air removal** (mechanical, fast). **Consolidation = water removal** (time-dependent, slow).
> - **Proctor test** gives MDD & OMC. **Modified Proctor** uses heavier hammer (4.9 kg vs 2.5 kg) and 5 layers (vs 3) → higher compactive effort → higher MDD.
> - **Sheepsfoot → Clay** | **Smooth static → Granular** | **Vibratory → Granular (best)** | **Pneumatic → Mixed/sandy clay** | **Rammer → Confined, cohesive** | **Plate → Patch, granular**.
> - **Lift thickness rule of thumb:** Granular = 300–1500 mm (vibratory) | Cohesive = 150–250 mm.
> - **Number of passes:** Typically 4–8; beyond this, marginal density gain is negligible.
> - **Centrifugal force formula:** $F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ — the *single most important* equation.
> - **Dynamic force ratio $F_d$:** $F_d < 1$ = pure static; $F_d \approx 1$–3 = mixed; $F_d > 3$ = full vibration mode (granular soils).
> - **Frequency for vibratory rollers:** 25–80 Hz (1500–4800 rpm typical). Higher frequencies = shallower depth of influence.
> - **Amplitude** (mm) controls depth of compaction; **Frequency** (Hz) controls intensity.
> - **Relative compaction target:** 95% (embankment body), 98% (subgrade top 500 mm), 100% (airfield sub-base).
> - **Production formula units trap:** If $S$ is in km/hr, **do not** multiply by 3600; if in m/s, **do** multiply by 3600. Use whichever is consistent.
> - **Rule of thumb for selection by area:** Smooth static for surface finish; Sheepsfoot for clay embankment; Pneumatic for base course; Vibratory for high production on granular subgrade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finishing Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finishing Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Finishing Equipment** in modern construction technology refers to a specialized category of mechanized tools, machines, and plant machinery used to perform the final surface treatment, texturing, smoothing, polishing, coating, or aesthetic enhancement operations on structural and architectural elements of a building or civil engineering project after the primary structural construction activities (such as concreting, masonry, or framing) are completed.

The term "finishing" in construction encompasses all operations that transform a raw structural surface into a finished, functional, durable, and aesthetically acceptable surface. These operations include:
- **Surface leveling and smoothing** of concrete (e.g., screeding, troweling, floating)
- **Surface texturing and polishing** (e.g., grinding, honing, polishing of concrete/marble)
- **Application of coatings and paints** (e.g., spray painting, putty application)
- **Plastering and rendering** (e.g., mechanical plastering)
- **Floor treatment** (e.g., epoxy application, polishing)
- **Joint treatment and sealing**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of finishing equipment as the "makeup and grooming kit" of a building. Just as a person might first get a haircut (structural work), then shave and trim (rough finishing), and finally apply cologne and style (fine finishing) to look presentable for a special event, a building too needs multiple stages of cosmetic and functional surface treatment to be ready for occupancy.

Consider a freshly cast concrete slab:
- **Structural phase** → concrete is poured and compacted (the "skeleton")
- **Rough finishing phase** → screeding removes excess concrete (the "rough grooming")
- **Intermediate phase** → floating smoothens the surface (the "intermediate cleanup")
- **Fine finishing phase** → troweling creates a hard, polished surface (the "final makeup")

Without these finishing operations, the slab would be rough, porous, uneven, and unfit for use — much like a person without grooming before a public appearance.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

Finishing equipment is typically classified by the following key parameters:
- **Power source** (electric, pneumatic, hydraulic, diesel)
- **Operating mode** (manual, walk-behind, ride-on, self-propelled)
- **Application surface** (horizontal floors, vertical walls, ceilings)
- **Output capacity** (measured in **m²/hour** or **m³/hour**)
- **Rotational/oscillation speed** (measured in **rpm** for rotary equipment)

### 1.4 KTU Syllabus Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PECET522, this topic is tested under **CO2: Understand modern construction equipment and machinery** at the cognitive level of **Apply (Level 3)** and **Analyze (Level 4)**. Students are expected to identify, classify, and recommend appropriate finishing equipment for given construction scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Finishing Equipment

Finishing equipment can be systematically classified as follows:

#### A. **Concrete Finishing Equipment**

1. **Power Screeds (Vibratory Screeds)**
   - Used to strike off, level, and consolidate fresh concrete
   - Operates on the principle of vibration combined with a strike-off blade
   - Types: Roller screeds, truss screeds, laser screeds, hand-held vibratory screeds

2. **Power Trowels (Float Machines)**
   - Used for final smoothing and polishing of concrete surfaces
   - **Walk-behind trowels** (diameter 600–1200 mm) — for small to medium slabs
   - **Ride-on trowels** (diameter 900–1500 mm) — for large industrial floors
   - Operate at **speeds of 50–180 rpm** (low speed for floating, high speed for troweling)

3. **Concrete Grinders and Polishers**
   - Used to grind, hone, and polish hardened concrete surfaces
   - Use diamond-embedded or abrasive discs
   - **Stages**: Coarse grinding (16–30 grit) → Intermediate (60–120 grit) → Fine polishing (200–3000 grit)

4. **Concrete Vibrators (for finishing)**
   - Immersion (needle) vibrators — for compacting
   - Surface vibrators (screed boards) — for slabs
   - Form vibrators — for precast elements

#### B. **Plastering and Rendering Equipment**

1. **Mechanical Plastering Machines (Spray Plasterers)**
   - Apply plaster, mortar, or stucco through a spraying nozzle
   - Capacity: **1–4 m³/hour** depending on model
   - Used for large-area wall and ceiling plastering

2. **Putty Sprayers**
   - Apply wall putty for smooth finishing
   - Use airless spray technology

#### C. **Painting and Coating Equipment**

1. **Airless Sprayers**
   - Atomize paint without compressed air
   - Pressure range: **1500–3000 psi** (100–200 bar)
   - Ideal for large surface areas, viscous coatings

2. **HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure) Sprayers**
   - Provide fine finish with low overspray
   - Pressure: **0.5–3 bar**

3. **Air-assisted Airless Sprayers**
   - Combine benefits of airless and HVLP

4. **Roller and Brush Systems (mechanized)**

#### D. **Floor Finishing Equipment**

1. **Floor Polishers / Buffers**
   - For marble, granite, terrazzo, vinyl floors
   - Rotational speed: **150–400 rpm**

2. **Floor Scarifiers / Planers**
   - Remove coatings, level concrete

3. **Shot Blasters / Blastrac Machines**
   - Surface preparation through abrasive blasting

#### E. **Joint Treatment and Sealing Equipment**

1. **Joint Fillers and Sealant Applicators**
2. **Groove Cutters and Saws**

### 2.2 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

**Why finishing is needed:**
- To achieve dimensional accuracy of the surface
- To provide durability and resistance to wear
- To enable aesthetic appeal
- To provide functional properties (anti-slip, water-resistance, cleanability)
- To seal pores and reduce permeability

**How each equipment type works (mechanism summary):**

| Equipment | Working Mechanism | Primary Action |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Power Screed | Vibration + Strike-off | Leveling |
| Power Trowel | Rotating blades | Smoothing/Polishing |
| Concrete Grinder | Abrasive friction | Grinding/Polishing |
| Plastering Machine | Pumping + Atomization | Coating Application |
| Airless Sprayer | Hydraulic atomization | Paint Application |
| Floor Polisher | Rotating pads | Buffing/Shining |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **key formulas and metrics** that are commonly tested in KTU examinations for finishing equipment.

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------|-------------|
| Productivity of Power Trowel | $A = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot N \cdot k$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$ | Area coverage rate |
| Effective coverage (overlapping passes) | $A_{eff} = A \cdot (1 - O_f)$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{hr}$ | Real output |
| Sprayer flow rate | $Q = \frac{V}{t}$ | $\text{liters}/\text{min}$ | Paint consumption |
| Coverage of coating | $C = \frac{S \cdot \rho}{d}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{liter}$ | Paint coverage |
| Nozzle pressure (airless) | $P_n = P_{in} \cdot \left(\frac{d_{in}}{d_{n}}\right)^{2}$ | $\text{psi or bar}$ | Spray pressure |
| Vibration frequency (screed) | $f = \frac{N_{rpm}}{60}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Vibration rate |
| Concrete setting time vs. troweling | $t_{trowel} = t_{initial\,set} + \Delta t$ | hours | Timing of operation |
| Surface roughness | $R_a = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | Surface quality |
| Polishing efficiency | $\eta_p = \frac{A_{polished}}{P \cdot t}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{kWh}$ | Energy efficiency |
| Floor flatness (F-number) | $F_N = \frac{2.66 \cdot q}{12}$ | dimensionless | Quality check |

> **Note on notation:** $r$ = trowel radius, $N$ = pass number, $k$ = efficiency factor (typically 0.6–0.85), $O_f$ = overlap factor, $S$ = solids content, $\rho$ = density, $d$ = dry film thickness, $q$ = measured deviation in inches, $P$ = power input, $t$ = time.

### 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Finishing equipment is **critical infrastructure** in:
> - **Industrial flooring projects** (warehouses, factories) where ride-on trowels achieve 5000+ m²/day
> - **High-rise construction** where mechanical plastering replaces manual labor by 5–10x productivity
> - **Smart city projects** requiring uniform, durable, aesthetic surfaces
> - **Airport runways and highways** requiring precise surface texturing for skid resistance
> - **Hospital and cleanroom construction** requiring seamless, polished epoxy floors
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Productivity of a Power Trowel

**Problem Statement:**
A walk-behind power trowel has a trowel diameter of 900 mm. The operator makes overlapping passes such that the effective width per pass is 750 mm. The forward speed is 4 m/min, and the operating efficiency is 75%. Calculate the productivity in **m²/hour**.

**Given Data:**
- Trowel diameter $D = 900 \text{ mm} = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- Effective pass width $w = 750 \text{ mm} = 0.75 \text{ m}$
- Forward speed $v = 4 \text{ m/min}$
- Efficiency factor $k = 0.75 = 75\%$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert the forward speed from m/min to m/hr.
$$
v = 4 \text{ m/min} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} = 240 \text{ m/hr}
$$
*Logic:* Since 1 hour = 60 minutes, multiplying by 60 converts the rate per minute to rate per hour.

**Step 2:** Write the basic productivity formula for a pass-based operation.
$$
A_{theoretical} = w \times v
$$
*Logic:* The area covered per unit time is the product of the effective sweep width and the forward travel speed.

**Step 3:** Substitute the known values into the theoretical productivity formula.
$$
A_{theoretical} = 0.75 \text{ m} \times 240 \text{ m/hr} = 180 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$
*Logic:* Multiplying the effective width of 0.75 m by the linear travel of 240 m/hr gives the theoretical area that can be covered if there were no operational losses.

**Step 4:** Apply the efficiency factor to obtain the actual productivity.
$$
A_{actual} = A_{theoretical} \times k
$$
*Logic:* Real machines do not operate at 100% efficiency due to turning, overlapping, blade change, rest periods, etc.

**Step 5:** Substitute and compute the final result.
$$
A_{actual} = 180 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr} \times 0.75 = 135 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$

**Final Answer:** The productivity of the power trowel is **$135 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}$**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Paint Coverage by Airless Sprayer

**Problem Statement:**
An airless paint sprayer is used to apply an epoxy coating with a dry film thickness (DFT) of 200 microns. The paint has 60% solids by volume and a wet density of 1.4 g/cm³. Calculate the theoretical coverage in **m²/liter** and the volume of paint required to cover 500 m² of surface area.

**Given Data:**
- Dry film thickness $d = 200 \text{ \mu m} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Solids content $S = 60\% = 0.60$
- Wet density $\rho = 1.4 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1400 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Area to cover $A = 500 \text{ m}^2$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the coverage formula for a coating.
$$
C = \frac{S \cdot \rho}{d}
$$
*Logic:* Coverage (m²/liter) relates the volume of solids deposited (a fraction S of total volume) to the dry film thickness.

**Step 2:** Convert density to consistent units (kg/liter).
$$
\rho = 1.4 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1.4 \text{ kg/liter}
$$
*Logic:* Since 1 g/cm³ = 1 kg/liter, the conversion is direct.

**Step 3:** Convert dry film thickness to a compatible form.
$$
d = 200 \text{ \mu m} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the coverage formula.
$$
C = \frac{0.60 \times 1.4}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \text{ m}^2/\text{liter}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerator computation.
$$
0.60 \times 1.4 = 0.84
$$

**Step 6:** Final division.
$$
C = \frac{0.84}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.84}{0.0002} = 4200 \text{ m}^2/\text{liter}
$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical coverage = **$4200 \text{ m}^2/\text{liter}$** (this is the maximum possible; actual coverage is typically 60–70% of theoretical due to losses).

**Step 7 (Extension):** Calculate volume required for 500 m² considering a 70% transfer efficiency.
$$
V_{paint} = \frac{A}{C \times \eta_t} = \frac{500}{4200 \times 0.70} = \frac{500}{2940} = 0.170 \text{ liters}
$$
*Logic:* Transfer efficiency $\eta_t$ accounts for overspray and wastage.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Selection of Plastering Machine

**Problem Statement:**
A construction project requires 800 m² of wall plastering to be completed in 5 working days of 8 hours each. Recommend the appropriate type of plastering machine and calculate the number of machines required if each machine has a productivity of 50 m²/hour and 80% utilization factor.

**Given Data:**
- Total area $A_{total} = 800 \text{ m}^2$
- Working days = 5
- Hours per day = 8
- Machine productivity $P_m = 50 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}$
- Utilization factor $u = 0.80$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate total available working hours.
$$
T = 5 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hr/day} = 40 \text{ hr}
$$
*Logic:* This is the total project timeline in machine-hours available per machine.

**Step 2:** Calculate the effective productivity per machine.
$$
P_{eff} = P_m \times u = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$
*Logic:* Multiplying by utilization factor accounts for setup, cleaning, maintenance, and operator breaks.

**Step 3:** Calculate area capacity of one machine over the project duration.
$$
A_{one} = P_{eff} \times T = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ m}^2
$$
*Logic:* One machine can cover 1600 m² in 40 hours at 80% utilization.

**Step 4:** Calculate number of machines required.
$$
N = \frac{A_{total}}{A_{one}} = \frac{800}{1600} = 0.5
$$
*Logic:* Since the result is less than 1, only one machine is needed.

**Step 5:** Round up to the next whole machine.
$$
N_{required} = \lceil 0.5 \rceil = 1 \text{ machine}
$$

**Final Answer:** **1 mechanical plastering machine** is sufficient for this project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** Although mathematically 0.5 machines suffice, on real KTU site problems and practical projects, always round **up** to the next whole number to account for breakdowns, maintenance, and contingency. Additionally, consider having a backup machine for critical timelines.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Selection Decision Tree

| If the surface is... | And the area is... | And finish quality needed is... | Recommended Equipment |
|----------------------|--------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------|
| Fresh concrete | < 100 m² | Smooth | Walk-behind power trowel |
| Fresh concrete | 100–1000 m² | Smooth | Walk-behind or small ride-on trowel |
| Fresh concrete | > 1000 m² | Smooth, level (F-number high) | Ride-on trowel or laser screed |
| Hardened concrete | Any | Polished, glossy | Diamond grinder/polisher |
| Wall | > 200 m² | Plaster | Mechanical plastering machine |
| Wall | Any | Paint | Airless sprayer |
| Floor (marble) | Any | Mirror finish | Floor polisher (150–400 rpm) |
| Old floor | Any | Coating removal | Shot blaster / scarifier |
| Joint | Any | Sealing | Sealant gun/applicator |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define "Finishing Equipment" in modern construction. List any four types of finishing equipment with one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Finishing equipment refers to a class of construction machinery used to perform the final surface treatment operations on structural elements after the primary construction activities are complete. These operations aim to provide the required level, smoothness, texture, durability, and aesthetic appearance to the surface.

**Four types and their applications:**

| S.No. | Equipment | Application |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Power Trowel | Final smoothing and polishing of concrete floors |
| 2 | Mechanical Plastering Machine | Spraying plaster on large wall areas |
| 3 | Airless Paint Sprayer | Applying high-viscosity coatings on walls and ceilings |
| 4 | Concrete Grinder/Polisher | Grinding and polishing hardened concrete to a mirror finish |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Definition = 1 mark, Four types with applications = 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the difference between a walk-behind power trowel and a ride-on power trowel. State two situations where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Walk-Behind Power Trowel | Ride-On Power Trowel |
|-----------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| Operator Position | Operator walks behind the machine | Operator sits and rides on the machine |
| Trowel Diameter | 600–1200 mm | 900–1500 mm |
| Productivity | Lower (100–300 m²/hr) | Higher (300–800 m²/hr) |
| Maneuverability | High (good for corners) | Lower (needs larger turning radius) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Preferred situations:**
- **Walk-behind:** Small to medium residential floors (< 200 m²) and areas with many corners/obstructions.
- **Ride-on:** Large industrial floors, warehouses, airport hangars, and commercial complexes (> 1000 m²) where high productivity is required.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3A.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified] — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Classify concrete finishing equipment in detail, explaining the working principle, components, and applications of a **power trowel**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A walk-behind power trowel with a trowel diameter of 1.2 m operates at a forward speed of 5 m/min. The effective overlap per pass is 0.85 m, and the machine operates at 70% efficiency. Calculate the productivity of the trowel in m²/hour. If 1500 m² of floor area needs to be troweled in 8 hours, how many such trowels are required? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Q3A:

#### Part (a) — Classification and Power Trowel Details

**Classification of Concrete Finishing Equipment:**

1. **Leveling/Screeding Equipment:** Power screeds (roller, truss, laser screeds)
2. **Compaction/Consolidation Equipment:** Immersion vibrators, surface vibrators
3. **Smoothing Equipment:** Hand floats, bull floats, power floats
4. **Troweling/Polishing Equipment:** Walk-behind trowels, ride-on trowels
5. **Grinding/Polishing Equipment:** Diamond grinders, planetary polishers
6. **Texturing Equipment:** Broom finishers, stamped concrete tools

**Working Principle of Power Trowel:**

A power trowel works on the principle of **rotational smoothing**. The gasoline engine or electric motor drives a spider assembly through a centrifugal clutch. The spider rotates multiple trowel blades at speeds between **50 and 180 rpm**. The blades, tilted at a slight angle, press against the partially set concrete, smoothing and compacting the surface through a combination of rotational friction and applied pressure.

**Main Components:**
- **Engine/Motor:** Power source (typically 5–25 HP)
- **Centrifugal Clutch:** Engages blades at engine start
- **Spider Assembly:** Rotating disc that holds the trowel blades
- **Trowel Blades:** 3–4 steel blades (finish or float blades) of size 150–450 mm
- **Handle/Pitch Control:** Allows operator to adjust blade pitch (0–30°)
- **Safety Stop Switch:** Emergency shutoff

**Applications:**
- Final finishing of concrete floors in residential, commercial, and industrial buildings
- Polishing warehouse floors, parking decks, factory floors
- Smoothing airport runways and highway concrete pavements

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Trowel diameter $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Forward speed $v = 5 \text{ m/min}$
- Effective pass width $w = 0.85 \text{ m}$
- Efficiency $k = 0.70 = 70\%$
- Total area $A = 1500 \text{ m}^2$
- Available time $T = 8 \text{ hr}$

**Step 1:** Convert forward speed to m/hr.
$$
v = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ m/hr}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate theoretical productivity.
$$
A_{theoretical} = w \times v = 0.85 \times 300 = 255 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate actual productivity.
$$
A_{actual} = 255 \times 0.70 = 178.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate area capacity per trowel in 8 hours.
$$
A_{per\,trowel} = 178.5 \times 8 = 1428 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate number of trowels required.
$$
N = \frac{1500}{1428} = 1.05
$$

**Step 6:** Round up to next whole number.
$$
N_{required} = 2 \text{ trowels}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Productivity = **$178.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}$**
- Number of trowels required = **2**

---

**Q3B.** [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified] — **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle, types, and applications of **mechanical plastering machines** in modern construction. Compare them with traditional manual plastering. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A construction firm has 1200 m² of wall plastering to be completed in 6 working days (8 hours/day). Each mechanical plastering machine has a capacity of 45 m²/hr at 75% utilization. Calculate:
   (i) The number of machines required.
   (ii) The total wet volume of plaster mortar needed if the plaster thickness is 12 mm and the wet density is 1900 kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Q3B:

#### Part (a) — Mechanical Plastering Machines

**Working Principle:**
A mechanical plastering machine (also called a spray plasterer) operates on the principle of **pumping and atomization**. Pre-mixed plaster or mortar is fed into a hopper, then pumped (using a screw or piston pump) through a hose to a spray nozzle. Compressed air atomizes the mortar into a fine spray, which is applied uniformly onto the wall surface. The operator controls the spray pattern, thickness, and flow rate through a trigger gun.

**Types of Mechanical Plastering Machines:**

1. **Screw-type Plastering Machine:** Uses an auger screw to pump mortar. Suitable for thin plaster coats.
2. **Piston-type Plastering Machine:** Uses reciprocating pistons for high-pressure delivery. Suitable for thick plaster and renders.
3. **Continuous Mix-and-Spray Machine:** Combines mixing, pumping, and spraying in a single unit.
4. **Putty/Finishing Sprayer:** Specialized for fine wall putty application.

**Applications:**
- Large-scale wall and ceiling plastering in high-rise buildings
- External rendering of facades
- Fireproofing sprays on structural steel
- Acoustic plaster application in auditoriums

**Comparison with Manual Plastering:**

| Parameter | Manual Plastering | Mechanical Plastering |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Productivity | 5–10 m²/day per mason | 150–400 m²/day per machine |
| Labor Required | 3–4 masons per team | 1–2 operators |
| Quality | Variable, depends on skill | Uniform, consistent |
| Material Wastage | 10–20% | 5–10% |
| Cost (Initial) | Low | High |
| Cost (Long-term) | High (labor costs) | Low (fewer man-hours) |
| Suitable for | Small, intricate areas | Large, uniform surfaces |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Total area $A = 1200 \text{ m}^2$
- Working days = 6
- Hours/day = 8
- Machine capacity = 45 m²/hr
- Utilization $u = 0.75$
- Plaster thickness $t = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- Wet density $\rho = 1900 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1:** Total available working hours per machine.
$$
T = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ hr}
$$

**Step 2:** Effective productivity per machine.
$$
P_{eff} = 45 \times 0.75 = 33.75 \text{ m}^2/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3:** Total area capacity per machine.
$$
A_{per\,machine} = 33.75 \times 48 = 1620 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Number of machines required.
$$
N = \frac{1200}{1620} = 0.74
$$
Round up:
$$
N_{required} = 1 \text{ machine}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate volume of plaster mortar.
$$
V_{mortar} = A \times t = 1200 \times 0.012 = 14.4 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate mass of plaster mortar.
$$
M = V \times \rho = 14.4 \times 1900 = 27360 \text{ kg} = 27.36 \text{ tons}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Number of machines required = **1 mechanical plastering machine**
- (ii) Wet volume of plaster = **14.4 m³** (Mass = 27,360 kg)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse "forward speed" units (m/min vs m/hr) and lose 1–2 marks. Always explicitly state the unit conversion step.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Rounding down instead of rounding up the number of machines. Always round **up** (use ceiling function) for procurement decisions — this is a standard KTU examiner expectation.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to apply the **utilization/efficiency factor** in numerical problems. The 70–80% factor is **mandatory** unless explicitly stated as 100%.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing "wet volume" vs. "dry volume" of mortar. The dry volume is approximately **1.33 times** the wet volume (to account for voids in sand). For KTU numerical problems, always clarify which volume is being asked.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** In Part (a) descriptive questions, students often list equipment names without stating their **working principle**. KTU examiners specifically look for the *principle* — merely naming equipment fetches only 50% marks for that sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Finishing Equipment**

- ✅ **Definition:** Finishing equipment = machines for final surface treatment (leveling, smoothing, polishing, coating, sealing) after structural work.
- ✅ **Five main categories:** (1) Concrete finishing, (2) Plastering, (3) Painting/Coating, (4) Floor treatment, (5) Joint treatment.
- ✅ **Power trowel rpm range:** 50–180 rpm (low for floating, high for troweling).
- ✅ **Productivity formula:** $A_{actual} = w \times v \times k$ (where $w$ = effective width, $v$ = forward speed, $k$ = efficiency).
- ✅ **Always round up** the number of machines required (ceiling function).
- ✅ **Sprayer pressure:** Airless = 1500–3000 psi; HVLP = 0.5–3 bar.
- ✅ **Concrete finishing sequence:** Screeding → Bull floating → Wait for bleed water → Power floating → Power troweling → (Optional) Polishing.
- ✅ **Plastering machine types:** Screw-type, Piston-type, Continuous mix-and-spray, Putty sprayer.
- ✅ **Concrete grinding stages:** Coarse (16–30 grit) → Intermediate (60–120) → Fine (200–3000).
- ✅ **Productivity comparison:** Mechanical plastering is **5–10x faster** than manual.
- ✅ **Units to remember:** m²/hr (productivity), rpm (rotation), bar/psi (pressure), m³ (volume), microns (film thickness).
- ✅ **Always convert units consistently** before calculation — a common KTU trick is mixing m/min with m²/hr.
- ✅ **Mention safety features** (emergency stop, guard, operator training) — KTU values safety awareness.
- ✅ **Selection criteria:** Area size, finish quality, project timeline, budget, surface type, and labor availability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Erection Equipment: Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Erection Equipment: Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crane (As per KTU 2024 PECET522 Syllabus):** A *crane* is a machine equipped with a **boom**, **jib**, **hoist mechanism**, and **counterweights**, designed to lift, lower, and horizontally transport heavy construction materials, structural members, and prefabricated components within a specified **working radius** and **lift height** under controlled speed and safety parameters.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a crane is classified as a **Category-I Erection Equipment** under *Modern Construction Technology*, used for vertical and horizontal transportation of loads in high-rise, industrial, and infrastructure projects.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Rated Capacity** | Maximum load a crane can safely lift at a specified working radius under defined outrigger/track conditions. |
| **Working Radius** | Horizontal distance from the centre of rotation to the centre of the load hook. |
| **Tip Height** | Maximum vertical reach from ground level to the tip of the jib. |
| **Pick Point** | The exact location on a structural element where the crane sling/hook is attached. |
| **Outrigger Pads** | Load-distribution plates placed beneath outrigger jacks to prevent ground failure. |
| **Free-on-Wheels (FOW)** | Crane capacity without outriggers, on tyres/tracks only. |
| **Duty Cycle** | One complete lift-and-place operation constituting a working cycle. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Comparison)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a crane as a "giant robotic human arm" attached to a stable base.** Just like your arm pivots at the shoulder (the crane's **swing circle**), extends from the elbow (the **boom**), and lifts at the wrist (the **hook block**), a crane performs the same three motions: **swinging**, **elevating (luffing)**, and **hoisting**. The base (your feet) is the crane's **outriggers or crawler tracks** — without a stable base, the arm cannot lift heavy weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key insight:** A crane is essentially a **first-class lever** of the third kind where the load is at one end, the fulcrum is the pivot, and the lifting force is applied via the hydraulic ram or wire rope. The longer the **boom radius**, the **lower the lifting capacity** — this is the most critical concept for KTU numerical problems.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Constants (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Standard hook block weight (typical 50 T crane):** **≈ 0.5 T to 1.2 T**
> - **Wire rope weight per metre (22 mm dia):** **≈ 1.65 kg/m**
> - **Design wind pressure (as per IS 875 Part 3):** **Basic wind speed for Kerala = 39 m/s (V<sub>b</sub>)**
> - **Standard counterweight (50 T mobile crane):** **≈ 8 T to 12 T**
> - **Ground bearing pressure (typical outrigger pad):** **75 kN/m² to 200 kN/m²**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Alert:** Questions frequently test the relationship between **working radius vs. capacity** and **boom length vs. tip height**. Always remember: *"As radius increases, capacity decreases — this is the inverse-square law of moment balancing."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cranes (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Construction cranes are classified based on **mobility**, **structural configuration**, and **power source**. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups them into the following primary categories:

### A. By Mobility / Mounting Type

1. **Mobile Cranes (Truck-Mounted / All-Terrain)**
   - Mounted on rubber-tyred carrier
   - High mobility between job sites
   - Used for short-duration lifts (1–4 hours)
2. **Crawler Cranes**
   - Mounted on crawler tracks (caterpillar)
   - Self-propelled, no outriggers required
   - Ideal for soft ground and continuous operation
3. **Rough Terrain Cranes**
   - Single-engine, 4-wheel drive, 4-wheel steer
   - Compact, used on unprepared sites
4. **Tower Cranes**
   - Fixed vertical mast with horizontal jib
   - Used for high-rise buildings (heights > 30 m)
5. **Telescopic Cranes (Hydraulic)**
   - Multi-section boom that telescopes hydraulically
   - Variable reach without dismantling
6. **Self-Erecting Cranes**
   - Foldable mast and jib, rapid setup
   - Used in confined urban sites

### B. By Structural Configuration

| Crane Type | Boom Type | Typical Capacity | Reach | Site Suitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Truck-mounted** | Telescopic | 20–250 T | 30–90 m | Highway projects |
| **Crawler** | Lattice/Telescopic | 50–3500 T | 40–150 m | Heavy industrial |
| **Tower** | Horizontal jib | 6–80 T | 50–80 m | High-rise buildings |
| **Luffing Jib Tower** | Articulated jib | 8–100 T | 40–60 m | Congested urban |
| **Rough Terrain** | Telescopic | 20–80 T | 25–50 m | Unpaved sites |
| **All-Terrain** | Telescopic | 30–1000 T | 40–120 m | Mixed terrain |
| **Crawler Telescopic** | Telescopic | 60–750 T | 50–140 m | Heavy lift / wind energy |

## 2.2 Operational Logic: How a Crane Lifts — Step-by-Step Theory

> [!NOTE]
> The "WHY and HOW" of crane operation must be understood in terms of **statics** (load balancing) and **kinematics** (movement).

**Step 1 — Load Attachment**
- The load is attached at the **pick point** using slings, shackles, or lifting beams.
- Load is suspended vertically (gravity acts downward through the centre of mass).

**Step 2 — Hook Block Ascent (Hoisting)**
- The **hoist drum** rotates, winding the wire rope.
- The hook block rises via a **multiple-part reeving** system (typically 2, 4, 6, or 8 parts).
- Mechanical advantage: $N = \text{Number of reeving parts}$
- Effective line pull reduces by factor of $N$.

**Step 3 — Luffing (Boom Elevation Change)**
- The boom angle $\theta$ is changed by hydraulic cylinder or pendant cables.
- Working radius changes as: $R = L \cos\theta$ where $L$ = boom length.

**Step 4 — Slewing (Rotation)**
- The upper structure rotates on a **swing circle** (slewing ring/ball bearing).
- Controlled by a hydraulic motor with planetary gearbox.

**Step 5 — Trolley Travel (Tower Cranes)**
- The trolley moves radially along the jib, changing the load position.
- For tower cranes: $R = \text{Trolley distance from mast centre}$

**Step 6 — Lowering & Placement**
- Controlled descent via load brake (automatic hydraulic brake).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet is the most tested content in KTU 2024 ESE for PECET522. Memorize all entries.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $R = L \cos\theta$ | $R$ = working radius, $L$ = boom length, $\theta$ = boom angle | Radius calculation |
| 2 | $H = L \sin\theta + h_b$ | $H$ = tip height, $h_b$ = boom pivot height | Height-of-lift |
| 3 | $M = W \times R$ | $M$ = overturning moment, $W$ = load weight, $R$ = radius | Stability check |
| 4 | $W_{net} = W_{gross} - W_{hook} - W_{rope} - W_{rigging}$ | Net vs. gross capacity | Capacity deduction |
| 5 | $W_{rope} = w \times L \times n$ | $w$ = rope weight/m, $L$ = length, $n$ = parts of line | Rope weight |
| 6 | $F_{line} = \dfrac{W_{load}}{N \times \eta}$ | $F_{line}$ = line pull, $N$ = reeving parts, $\eta$ = efficiency | Hoist drum |
| 7 | $P_{stability} = \dfrac{\sum M_{resisting}}{\sum M_{overturning}} \geq 1.5$ | Stability factor (must be ≥1.5 per IS 4573) | Anti-tip check |
| 8 | $T_{cycle} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5$ | Hoist + swing + lower + return + setup | Productivity |
| 9 | $Q_{hourly} = \dfrac{3600 \times \eta_{time}}{T_{cycle}} \times Q_{lift}$ | Hourly production rate | Cycle planning |
| 10 | $V_{wind\ allowable} = \sqrt{\dfrac{q_z \times A}{C_d \times \rho}}$ | Wind velocity on boom | Out-of-service wind |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Important:** For the **Stability Factor** of a crane on outriggers, the minimum allowed value as per **IS 4573:1982 (Reaffirmed 2014)** is **1.5** for in-service condition and **1.25** for out-of-service condition.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Crane engineering directly impacts:
> - **High-rise construction** (tower crane cycle time = critical path)
> - **Bridge segment launching** (crawler crane on barges for marine projects)
> - **Wind turbine erection** (crawler telescopic with 150 m boom)
> - **Modular construction** (precision lifts within ±25 mm tolerance)
> - **Disaster response** (mobile cranes for collapsed structure salvage)

The selection of the correct crane is the **single most important decision** in pre-construction planning, as it directly affects **cost (₹), time (days), and safety (zero-incident goal)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Working Radius and Tip Height from Boom Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU 2024 Module 2. Understand every line.**

### Given:
- Boom length $L$ (in metres)
- Boom angle $\theta$ (in degrees, measured from horizontal)
- Boom pivot (slew centre) height above ground $= h_p$

### Derivation:

Let the boom be represented as a straight rigid member pivoted at the slew centre. The hook is suspended vertically from the boom tip (assume negligible jib offset for simplicity).

**Step 1 — Horizontal Projection of Boom**

The horizontal component of the boom, when elevated at angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, is:
$$R = L \cos\theta$$

where:
- $R$ = horizontal working radius (metres)
- $L$ = total boom length (metres)
- $\theta$ = boom elevation angle (degrees)

**Step 2 — Vertical Projection of Boom**

The vertical rise of the boom tip above the slew centre pivot is:
$$h_{tip} = L \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Total Tip Height Above Ground**

Adding the pivot height (boom foot above ground level):
$$H = h_p + L \sin\theta$$

where $h_p$ = height of the boom pivot from ground level.

**Step 4 — Expressing in Terms of Radius (Inverse Relationship)**

From Step 1, we have $\cos\theta = \dfrac{R}{L}$.

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:
$$\sin\theta = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{R^2}{L^2}}$$

Therefore, the tip height can be re-expressed purely as a function of $R$ and $L$:
$$H = h_p + L \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{R^2}{L^2}}$$

$$H = h_p + \sqrt{L^2 - R^2}$$

**Step 5 — Verification Limiting Cases**

- If $\theta = 90°$ (vertical boom): $R = 0$, $H = h_p + L$ ✓
- If $\theta = 0°$ (horizontal boom): $R = L$, $H = h_p$ ✓
- As $R$ increases, $H$ decreases monotonically. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Lifting Capacity (Load Chart Deduction Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Problem Pattern:** "A crane has a gross capacity of X T at radius R. Calculate the net load that can be lifted, given the hook, rope, and rigging weights."

### Given:
- Gross capacity from load chart: $W_{gross}$ (T)
- Working radius: $R$ (m)
- Boom length: $L$ (m)
- Weight of hook block: $W_{hook}$ (T)
- Weight of wire rope per metre: $w$ (kg/m)
- Number of parts of line: $n$
- Weight of rigging (slings, shackles, spreader beam): $W_{rig}$ (T)

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Weight of Wire Rope**

The total suspended length of rope is approximately equal to the vertical projection of the boom (assuming vertical hook drop):
$$L_{rope} = L \sin\theta + h_{hook}$$

For the conservative case (maximum deduction), use:
$$L_{rope} \approx L$$

Total weight of rope:
$$W_{rope} = \dfrac{w \times L \times n}{1000} \text{ T}$$

where the factor 1000 converts kg → tonnes.

**Step 2 — Total Deductions**

The gross capacity must be reduced by all "dead" weights not contributing to productive load:
$$W_{deduction} = W_{hook} + W_{rope} + W_{rig}$$

**Step 3 — Net Lifting Capacity**

$$\boxed{W_{net} = W_{gross} - \left( W_{hook} + W_{rope} + W_{rig} \right)}$$

**Step 4 — Critical Check**

The value $W_{net}$ must be **greater than the actual load weight** for safe lifting:
$$W_{actual\ load} \leq 0.75 \times W_{net}$$

The **0.75 factor** is the KTU/IS code safety margin (75% utilization rule for routine lifts).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stability Against Overturning (IS 4573 Method)

> [!NOTE]
> A crane on outriggers can tip over if the overturning moment exceeds the resisting moment. This derivation is critical for site safety questions.

### Given:
- Load weight: $W_L$ (T)
- Working radius: $R$ (m)
- Crane weight: $W_C$ (T)
- Distance of crane centre of gravity from outrigger tip: $d_C$ (m)
- Outrigger spread (base dimension): $B$ (m)

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Overturning Moment**

The load creates an overturning moment about the tipping edge (outer outrigger):
$$M_{OT} = W_L \times R$$

**Step 2 — Resisting Moment**

The crane's self-weight creates a stabilizing moment:
$$M_R = W_C \times d_C$$

**Step 3 — Stability Factor (IS 4573 Formula)**

$$S.F. = \dfrac{M_R}{M_{OT}} \geq 1.5$$

Substituting:
$$\dfrac{W_C \times d_C}{W_L \times R} \geq 1.5$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Maximum Allowable Radius**

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{W_C \times d_C}{1.5 \times W_L}$$

This gives the **maximum safe working radius** for a given load — a directly useful site-engineering result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cycle Time and Hourly Production

### Given:
- Lift height: $H$ (m)
- Hoist speed: $V_h$ (m/min)
- Slewing angle: $\alpha$ (degrees)
- Slew speed: $\omega$ (rpm)
- Lowering speed: $V_l$ (m/min)
- Set-up time per cycle: $t_s$ (min)

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Hoisting Time**
$$t_1 = \dfrac{H}{V_h}$$

**Step 2 — Slewing Time (Loaded)**
$$t_2 = \dfrac{\alpha}{360 \times \omega}$$

**Step 3 — Lowering Time**
$$t_3 = \dfrac{H}{V_l}$$

**Step 4 — Return Slew (Empty)**
$$t_4 = \dfrac{\alpha}{360 \times \omega}$$

**Step 5 — Total Cycle Time**
$$T_{cycle} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_s$$

**Step 6 — Hourly Production**
$$Q_{hourly} = \dfrac{60}{T_{cycle}} \times Q_{lift} \times \eta_{time}$$

where $\eta_{time}$ = time efficiency (typically **0.75 to 0.83** for normal site conditions).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solve this carefully — it represents the exact style of KTU 2024 14-mark questions.**

**Problem:** A truck-mounted telescopic crane has a **gross capacity of 50 T** at a working radius of **12 m** with boom length **30 m** and boom angle **60°**. The hook block weighs **1.2 T**, the wire rope weighs **1.65 kg/m**, the number of parts of line is **4**, and the rigging weight is **0.3 T**. Calculate:
(a) The **net lifting capacity** (7 marks)
(b) The **stability factor** if the crane weight is 38 T and CG is 4 m from tipping edge (7 marks)

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Net Capacity**

Step 1: Weight of wire rope
$$W_{rope} = \dfrac{1.65 \times 30 \times 4}{1000} = \dfrac{198}{1000} = 0.198 \text{ T}$$

Step 2: Total deductions
$$W_{ded} = 1.2 + 0.198 + 0.3 = 1.698 \text{ T}$$

Step 3: Net capacity
$$W_{net} = 50 - 1.698 = 48.302 \text{ T}$$

Step 4: Safe allowable load (75% rule)
$$W_{safe} = 0.75 \times 48.302 = 36.227 \text{ T}$$

**Part (b) — Stability Factor**

Step 1: Overturning moment
$$M_{OT} = 50 \times 12 = 600 \text{ T·m}$$

Step 2: Resisting moment
$$M_R = 38 \times 4 = 152 \text{ T·m}$$

Step 3: Stability Factor
$$S.F. = \dfrac{152}{600} = 0.253$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**
Since $S.F. = 0.253 < 1.5$, the crane is **UNSAFE** at the rated capacity. The actual safe load must be reduced:

$$W_L^{max} = \dfrac{M_R}{1.5 \times R} = \dfrac{152}{1.5 \times 12} = 8.44 \text{ T}$$

**Conclusion:** The crane cannot lift the full 50 T at 12 m radius without additional counterweight or closer outrigger positioning.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "working radius" of a crane. How does it affect the lifting capacity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **working radius** of a crane is defined as the **horizontal distance measured from the centre of rotation (slew centre) of the crane to the centre of the vertical line passing through the hook block and the load**.

It directly affects the lifting capacity through the **overturning moment principle**. The overturning moment is given by $M = W \times R$. As the working radius increases, the moment arm increases, thereby reducing the safe lifting capacity of the crane. This inverse relationship between radius and capacity is fundamental to load chart interpretation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**List any four types of cranes commonly used in modern construction with one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Tower Crane** — Used for vertical transportation in high-rise building construction (heights > 30 m).
2. **Crawler Crane** — Used for heavy lifts on soft/unprepared ground, e.g., bridge girder erection.
3. **Truck-Mounted Mobile Crane** — Used for short-duration lifts and quick mobilization across multiple sites.
4. **Luffing Jib Tower Crane** — Used in congested urban sites where horizontal sweep is restricted.
**(3 marks — 0.75 each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - 14 Marks with Internal Choice]

**Question A (a):** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **hydraulic telescopic crane**. List its advantages over a lattice boom crane. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **hydraulic telescopic crane** consists of a multi-section boom (typically 3 to 6 sections) that can be extended or retracted using **hydraulic cylinders** mounted within the boom structure. The boom is mounted on a rotating upper structure (turret), which in turn is supported by a carrier vehicle with outriggers.

**Working Principle:**
1. The diesel engine drives a **hydraulic pump**, which generates high-pressure hydraulic oil.
2. The oil is directed to **telescope cylinders** that push the inner boom sections out sequentially (or simultaneously with sequential booms).
3. The **luffing cylinder** raises or lowers the entire boom assembly about the pivot.
4. The **hoist motor** rotates the hoist drum, winding the wire rope to lift the load.
5. The **slewing motor** with planetary gearbox rotates the upper structure on the swing circle.
6. **Outriggers** are extended and padded to provide a stable base.

**Advantages over Lattice Boom Crane (any 4 of the following):**
1. **Variable length** — Can be extended/retracted in seconds without dismantling auxiliary sections.
2. **Faster setup and operation** — No assembly of pin-connected lattice sections required.
3. **Lower transport height** — Retracted boom fits within standard road transport dimensions.
4. **Higher manoeuvrability** — Single cab control, joystick operations.
5. **Better positioning accuracy** — Smooth hydraulic control enables precision placement.
6. **Suitable for multiple-radius lifts** — Can adjust reach on the fly. **(7 marks: 3 for working + 4 for advantages)**

---

**Question A (b):** A crawler crane has a **gross capacity of 80 T** at a **working radius of 8 m** with **boom length 40 m** at **boom angle 70°**. Hook block weight = **1.5 T**, wire rope weight = **2.0 kg/m**, parts of line = **6**, rigging weight = **0.5 T**. Calculate the **net lifting capacity** and the **safe allowable load**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Gross capacity: $W_{gross} = 80$ T
- Working radius: $R = 8$ m
- Boom length: $L = 40$ m
- Boom angle: $\theta = 70°$
- Hook block: $W_{hook} = 1.5$ T
- Wire rope: $w = 2.0$ kg/m
- Parts of line: $n = 6$
- Rigging: $W_{rig} = 0.5$ T

**Step 1: Weight of Wire Rope**
$$W_{rope} = \dfrac{w \times L \times n}{1000} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 40 \times 6}{1000} = \dfrac{480}{1000} = 0.48 \text{ T}$$

**Step 2: Total Deductions**
$$W_{ded} = W_{hook} + W_{rope} + W_{rig} = 1.5 + 0.48 + 0.5 = 2.48 \text{ T}$$

**Step 3: Net Lifting Capacity**
$$W_{net} = W_{gross} - W_{ded} = 80 - 2.48 = 77.52 \text{ T}$$

**Step 4: Safe Allowable Load (75% rule per IS 4573)**
$$W_{safe} = 0.75 \times 77.52 = 58.14 \text{ T}$$

**Result:** The crane can safely lift up to **58.14 T** at 8 m radius. **(7 marks: 2+2+2+1)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

### [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - 14 Marks]

**Question B (a):** Compare **tower cranes** and **mobile cranes** based on the following criteria: (i) site mobility, (ii) lifting height, (iii) lifting capacity, (iv) setup time, (v) cost, and (vi) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Tower Crane | Mobile Crane |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Site Mobility** | Fixed once erected; requires dismantling for relocation | Highly mobile; drives on highways to multiple sites |
| **Lifting Height** | 30 m to 200 m+ (excellent for high-rise) | 20 m to 150 m (limited by boom + jib geometry) |
| **Lifting Capacity** | 6 T to 80 T typical (jib-end low capacity) | 20 T to 3500 T (highest single-crane capacity) |
| **Setup Time** | 1 to 3 days for erection; complex process | 1 to 3 hours for outrigger setup; quick |
| **Cost** | High initial + high erection cost (₹15–50 lakh/month rental) | Lower rental; pay per lift (₹2–10 lakh/day) |
| **Typical Applications** | High-rise buildings, power plants, tall chimneys | Bridges, industrial plants, infrastructure, one-off lifts |
**(7 marks — 1 mark per criterion with 0.5 marks for each correct sub-point)**

---

**Question B (b):** A tower crane is to be used for a high-rise project. The jib length is **60 m** and the trolley is positioned at a **radius of 45 m** from the mast. The hook is suspended at a **height of 50 m** above ground. Calculate the **load moment** for a precast element weighing **8 T** and verify whether the **rated moment capacity of 400 T·m** is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Jib length: $L_{jib} = 60$ m
- Trolley radius: $R = 45$ m
- Hook height: $H = 50$ m
- Load weight: $W = 8$ T
- Rated moment capacity: $M_{rated} = 400$ T·m

**Step 1: Load Moment Calculation**
$$M_{load} = W \times R = 8 \times 45 = 360 \text{ T·m}$$

**Step 2: Compare with Rated Capacity**
$$M_{load} = 360 \text{ T·m} < M_{rated} = 400 \text{ T·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Utilization Ratio**
$$U = \dfrac{M_{load}}{M_{rated}} = \dfrac{360}{400} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

**Step 4: Verification per IS 4573**
For routine lifts, the utilization should not exceed 75%:
$$0.75 \times 400 = 300 \text{ T·m}$$
Since $M_{load} = 360$ T·m > 300 T·m, the load is **WITHIN capacity** (90% utilization) but is **NOT within the 75% safe utilization rule**.

**Conclusion:** The crane's rated capacity (400 T·m) is **sufficient** to lift the 8 T load at 45 m radius (360 T·m). However, for **routine operations**, the lift should be **approved with engineering certification** since utilization is 90%, exceeding the conservative 75% rule. **Use of a load moment indicator (LMI) is mandatory.** **(7 marks: 2+2+2+1)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 75% safety rule** — Students calculate net capacity correctly but forget to apply the IS-mandated 75% factor for routine lifts. Marks deducted: **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using total boom length instead of vertical projection** for rope weight — Rope weight calculation must use the **vertical suspended length**, not the boom length. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing gross and net capacity** — The load chart gives **gross** capacity; deductions for hook + rope + rigging must always be subtracted. Marks deducted: **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify stability factor ≥ 1.5** — Numerical problems must always include a stability check using IS 4573. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kg and tonnes in rope weight calculation. Always convert kg/m × m × parts ÷ 1000 = tonnes. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-Day KTU Revision — Bullet-Point Summary for Module 2: Cranes**

**🔹 Core Concepts:**
- A crane is a **lifting + slewing + luffing** machine mounted on a stable base.
- Three primary motions: **hoisting (up/down)**, **slewing (rotation)**, **luffing (boom elevation)**.
- Working radius $R$ is **horizontal distance** from slew centre to load.
- Tip height $H$ is **vertical distance** from ground to boom tip.

**🔹 Key Formulas (Memorize Verbatim):**
- $R = L \cos\theta$
- $H = h_p + L \sin\theta$
- $W_{net} = W_{gross} - (W_{hook} + W_{rope} + W_{rig})$
- $W_{rope} = \dfrac{w \times L \times n}{1000}$ T
- $S.F. = \dfrac{M_{resisting}}{M_{overturning}} \geq 1.5$
- $M_{OT} = W_L \times R$
- $W_{safe} = 0.75 \times W_{net}$

**🔹 Crane Types (Know at least 6):**
- Tower (hammerhead, luffing jib, self-erecting)
- Crawler
- Truck-mounted / All-terrain
- Rough terrain
- Telescopic hydraulic
- Lattice boom crawler

**🔹 Codes & Standards:**
- **IS 4573:1982** — Stability of cranes
- **IS 3177:1999** — Code of practice for cranes
- **75% utilization rule** for routine lifts
- **Stability Factor ≥ 1.5** for in-service condition

**🔹 Site Selection Criteria:**
- **Capacity** required (with deductions)
- **Reach** (radius + height)
- **Site access** (road width, ground bearing pressure)
- **Mobility** (crawler vs. wheeled)
- **Cycle time** (number of lifts per day)
- **Cost** (rental + erection + dismantling)

**🔹 Numerical Problem Solving Order:**
1. Identify gross capacity from load chart
2. Calculate rope weight using boom length
3. Apply all deductions → get $W_{net}$
4. Apply 75% rule → get $W_{safe}$
5. Check stability factor → must be ≥ 1.5
6. State final decision: **Approved / Reject / Re-plan**

**🔹 Common Numerical Values to Remember:**
- Hook block: 0.5–1.5 T
- Wire rope: 1.5–2.0 kg/m
- Rigging: 0.2–0.5 T
- 75% safety factor
- 1.5 stability factor

**🔹 Examiner Hacks:**
- Always draw a **neat boom geometry diagram** with all dimensions marked.
- Always **state the formula before substituting** values.
- Always include **units** in every step.
- Always **interpret the result** (e.g., "The crane is unsafe because S.F. < 1.5").
- Always **mention IS Code references** to demonstrate applied knowledge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derrick Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derrick Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derrick Crane (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *derrick crane* is a stationary or semi-stationary lifting appliance consisting of a vertical or near-vertical **mast** (king post) supported by guy ropes, a **boom (jib)** pivoted at the base of the mast, and a system of **hoisting ropes and slewing gear** used to lift, swing, and place heavy loads in construction, erection, and material-handling operations.

It is classified under the family of **jib cranes** but is distinguished by its reliance on **guy cables** (or rigid supports) for stability rather than a self-supporting structural frame. The design pre-dates modern mobile cranes and remains preferred for **vertical lifting in confined sites** such as high-rise cores, chimney erection, and steel-structure assembly.

## 1.2 Key Engineering Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mast / King Post** | The vertical structural column that supports the boom pivot and transmits loads to the base |
| **Boom / Jib** | The inclined lifting arm carrying the load block and pulley system |
| **Bull Wheel** | Large rope-drum wheel mounted on the mast that tensions the topping lift (boom hoist) |
| **Topping Lift** | Rope supporting the boom head; controlled by the bull wheel |
| **Guy Ropes / Cables** | Steel wire ropes anchored to the ground that stabilize the mast laterally |
| **Slewing Gear** | Mechanism (rack + pinion or chain) that rotates the boom in the horizontal plane |
| **Sill / Base Plate** | Heavy timber or steel base distributing mast reaction to the foundation |
| **Whip Line** | Auxiliary single-part hoist line for high-speed light loads |
| **Load Line** | Main hoist rope carrying the working load |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tripod Fishing Rod" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a giant fishing rod propped on a vertical pole in your backyard.** The pole is the **mast**, the fishing rod is the **boom**, and you have tied **four strong ropes from the top of the pole to the ground** so the pole does not topple. You lift a heavy bucket using a rope-and-pulley at the end of the rod (boom), and you can swing the rod around the pole (slewing) to drop the bucket at a different spot. That is exactly how a guy-derrick crane works — except the pole is 30 m tall, the ropes are made of 2-inch wire cable, and the bucket weighs 5 tonnes.

The intuition makes three engineering facts immediately clear:

1. The **mast is not self-supporting** — it *must* have the guy ropes, just like a tent pole needs guy lines.
2. The **boom carries the load** and uses the mast only as a *pivot point* and *anchor* for its topping lift.
3. **Slewing is rotation about a fixed vertical axis** — there is no travelling, so the crane's stability depends entirely on its anchorage and ballast.

## 1.4 Classification of Derrick Cranes (Syllabus-Mandated)

The KTU 2024 PECET522 syllabus requires explicit coverage of the three principal variants:

- **Guy Derrick (Gin Pole / Stiff-Leg variant):** Mast held by **flexible wire-rope guys** anchored to the ground; used for general lifting where ground anchorage is feasible.
- **Stiff-Leg Derrick:** Mast held by **two rigid inclined struts (stiff legs)** forming a triangular portal; used on sites where guys cannot be anchored (e.g., congested urban sites).
- **Breast Derrick:** A short-mast variant where the boom pivots from a wall-mounted bracket; used for **harbour-side cargo handling** and limited-radius lifting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Examiner loves to ask — *"Distinguish between a Guy Derrick and a Stiff-Leg Derrick."* The single-line answer: **Guy Derrick uses flexible wire-rope supports, Stiff-Leg Derrick uses rigid inclined structural members.** No further words required for full marks in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A derrick crane converts the **rotary input** of an electric or diesel winch into **vertical lift + horizontal slew + boom elevation**, transmitted through a coordinated rope-and-pulley system. The energy flow is:

1. **Power Source (Electric Motor / Diesel Engine)** drives the hoist winch and the bull-wheel winch through a gearbox.
2. **Hoist Winch** pulls the **load line** (which passes over a sheave at the boom tip and down to the load block) → produces **vertical lift**.
3. **Bull-Wheel Winch** pulls the **topping lift** (which runs from the bull wheel, over a sheave at the mast top, down to the boom tip) → raises or lowers the **boom angle**.
4. **Slewing Gear** rotates the entire boom assembly on a circular roller track at the base → produces **horizontal swing**.
5. **Guy Ropes (or Stiff Legs)** resist the horizontal component of the boom pull, keeping the mast in equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why":** The boom is essentially a *compression member* in pure bending. The mast is in *pure axial compression* plus *bending from the boom pull*. The guys carry the *lateral force* that would otherwise overturn the mast. This force distribution is what makes the derrick **structurally efficient for very tall, slender installations**.

## 2.2 Forces Acting on a Derrick Crane — The Free-Body Picture

For analytical purposes, consider a **Guy Derrick** with the boom inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal and the load $W$ hanging from the boom tip. Four force systems act on the mast-boom assembly:

- **Vertical Reaction** at the mast base (compression into the sill).
- **Horizontal Reaction** at the mast base (resisted by friction or a pin).
- **Tension $T_g$** in the guy ropes.
- **Internal Boom Force $F_b$** (compression along the boom).

### Step 1 — Resolve the Load at the Boom Tip

The load $W$ acts vertically downward. The boom takes this load in **axial compression combined with bending**; for the simple static analysis, the effective load on the boom tip is:

$$P_{boom} = W + w_b \cdot L$$

where $w_b$ is the **self-weight per unit length** of the boom and $L$ is the boom length. Often $w_b L$ is neglected in Part A problems; for Part B numericals it must be included.

### Step 2 — Resolve Forces at the Boom-Mast Pivot

At the pivot, two rope systems meet: the **load line** (vertical, with tension $W$) and the **topping lift** (running from the boom tip, over a mast-top sheave, to the bull wheel). Taking moments about the pivot and resolving gives the topping-lift tension $T_t$.

### Step 3 — Resolve Forces at the Mast Top

The mast top receives:
- The **vertical component** of the topping-lift tension.
- The **horizontal pull** from the boom (tension side) and the **guy tension** (anchorage side).

The **horizontal component of the boom pull** is the single most important quantity, because it dictates the **guy-rope tension** and the **mast bending moment**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every formula a KTU student must memorize** for Derrick Cranes. These are the equations that reappear in 14-mark Part B questions almost every cycle.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective load at boom tip | $P = W + w_b L$ | $W$ = load, $w_b$ = boom self-weight/length, $L$ = boom length |
| 2 | Horizontal pull at mast top | $H = P \cos\theta$ | $\theta$ = boom angle to horizontal |
| 3 | Vertical load on mast top | $V = P \sin\theta$ | Acts downward through the topping-lift sheave |
| 4 | Topping-lift tension (approx.) | $T_t \approx \dfrac{P L_t}{h}$ | $L_t$ = topping-lift span, $h$ = vertical rise |
| 5 | Guy-rope tension per rope (4 guys at angle $\phi$ to ground) | $T_g = \dfrac{H}{4 \cos\phi}$ | Force in each guy, assuming equal share |
| 6 | Mast base vertical reaction | $R_V = W + w_b L + w_m H_m + T_t \sin\beta$ | $\beta$ = topping-lift angle to horizontal |
| 7 | Mast base horizontal reaction | $R_H = H - \sum T_g \cos\phi$ | Should equal zero in equilibrium |
| 8 | Bending moment in mast at boom pivot | $M_{mast} = H \cdot y_{pivot}$ | $y_{pivot}$ = height of pivot above base |
| 9 | Required counterweight (stiff-leg variant) | $W_c = \dfrac{P \cos\theta \cdot h_2}{h_1}$ | $h_1$, $h_2$ = lever arms |
| 10 | Hoist power | $P_{kw} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{6120 \cdot \eta}$ | $W$ in kg, $v$ in m/min, $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 11 | Slewing speed | $N = \dfrac{v_s}{\pi D}$ rpm | $v_s$ = slewing velocity, $D$ = slew circle diameter |
| 12 | Safe working load (SWL) of derrick | $SWL = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow} \cdot A_{boom} - w_b L}{n}$ | $n$ = factor of safety (typically 3 to 4) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Pipeline Warning:** The table above uses the LaTeX vertical-bar-free convention ($\vert$ / $\mid$) only where needed. All other notations are bracket-free to keep the GFM renderer stable. Do **not** introduce absolute-value `|x|` directly into table cells — always wrap them as `$\vert x \vert$`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Derrick cranes are the **workhorses of vertical construction** in scenarios where mobile cranes cannot operate:

- **High-rise core construction:** Stiff-leg derricks are mounted on the completed floor slab and "climb" floor-by-floor as the building rises. The classic example is the **Empire State Building** (1930) and many Keralan high-rises today.
- **Chimney and tower erection:** A small guy derrick at the top of a partially-built chimney lifts each new ring of bricks.
- **Harbour cargo handling:** The **breast derrick** is still the dominant ship-side loader in Cochin Port.
- **Steel-structure erection:** Used for placing heavy trusses and girders on industrial sheds.
- **Power-plant construction:** Lifting turbine casings, generators, and boiler drums inside confined turbine halls.

The **economic advantage** is that the derrick is *cheaper per tonne-metre of lift* than a mobile crane once the project height exceeds ~40 m, because no counterweight truck or outrigger pad is needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Guy-Rope Tension for a Symmetrically Loaded Derrick

This is the **single most important derivation** in this module and is almost guaranteed to appear in a 14-mark Part B question. We work it out in full.

### Given
- Effective load at boom tip: $P$ (kN)
- Boom inclination to horizontal: $\theta$
- Mast height: $H_m$ (m)
- Boom length: $L$ (m)
- Number of guy ropes: $n_g = 4$ (typical KTU assumption)
- Angle of each guy rope to the horizontal ground: $\phi$
- Boom pivot height above base: $y_p$

### Step 1 — Resolve $P$ at the boom tip

The boom tip carries the load $P$ vertically downward. Resolving along and perpendicular to the boom:

$$P_{\text{axial}} = P \sin\theta \quad \text{(along boom, compression)}$$

$$P_{\perp} = P \cos\theta \quad \text{(perpendicular to boom, transferred to mast top)}$$

### Step 2 — Horizontal force at the mast top

The boom is hinged at the mast top, so the perpendicular component of $P$ is transmitted to the mast as a **horizontal pull**:

$$H_{mast} = P \cos\theta$$

This is the **force the guys must resist** (in the plane of the boom). Out-of-plane, the load is balanced by symmetric guy pairs.

### Step 3 — Force equilibrium in the guy plane

Consider the four guy ropes anchored symmetrically. In the **boom plane**, only **two guys are effective** — one on the load side (which goes slack and is often omitted) and one on the opposite side (which takes the full pull). The classical textbook simplification for a **4-guy symmetric derrick** is to assume all four share the load equally in the resultant horizontal sense, but the conservative engineering approach for Part B is to consider **two guys in the boom plane only**.

For 4 equally-pretensioned guys each making angle $\phi$ with the ground, the resultant horizontal holding force per guy is $T_g \cos\phi$. Summing the four guys (2 forward, 2 backward, symmetric):

$$H_{mast} = 2 T_g \cos\phi \quad \text{(effective horizontal component from the two rear guys)}$$

Therefore:

$$T_g = \dfrac{H_{mast}}{2 \cos\phi} = \dfrac{P \cos\theta}{2 \cos\phi}$$

### Step 4 — Vertical reaction at the mast base

The vertical reaction must support the weight of the load, the boom, the mast itself, plus the vertical components of the topping-lift tension. Neglecting topping-lift for a first-order estimate:

$$R_V = P + w_b L + w_m H_m$$

### Step 5 — Bending moment in the mast

The horizontal pull $H_{mast}$ acts at the boom pivot height $y_p$ above the base. The mast behaves as a cantilever fixed at the base. The maximum bending moment occurs at the base:

$$M_{base} = H_{mast} \cdot y_p = P \cos\theta \cdot y_p$$

### Step 6 — Combined stress check at mast base

The mast is a hollow circular section of outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$. The combined axial + bending stress at the extreme fibre is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{R_V}{A} + \dfrac{M_{base}}{Z}$$

where

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right) \quad \text{and} \quad Z = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(\dfrac{D_o^4 - D_i^4}{D_o}\right)$$

The safety condition for KTU board-level design:

$$\sigma_{max} \le \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{n}$$

with $n = 3$ to $4$ being the typical factor of safety for construction derricks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Hoist Power Requirement

The hoist motor must overcome the load weight plus friction in the sheaves. The standard formula is:

$$P_{hoist} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta}$$

where $W$ is in newtons, $v$ in m/s, and $\eta$ is the overall efficiency (sheaves + gearbox, typically 0.75 to 0.85). Converting to kW:

$$P_{hoist,\,kW} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta}$$

If $W$ is given in kgf and $v$ in m/min (very common in KTU problems):

$$P_{hoist,\,kW} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{6120 \cdot \eta}$$

This $6120$ constant comes from:

$$6120 = 60 \times 102 \quad \text{(minute-to-second, kgf-to-N conversion factors combined)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Load Case Summary

| Load Case | Load on Boom | Hoist Tension | Guy Tension | Mast Bending | Critical Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lifting rated load at max radius** | $W + w_b L$ | Max | Max (boom angle low) | Max | Guy rope + Mast base |
| **Lifting at mid radius** | $W + w_b L$ | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Boom compression |
| **Boom at high angle, light load** | Small $W$ | Low | Low | Low | Bull-wheel rope wear |
| **Slewing with full load** | $W + w_b L$ + centrifugal | Steady | Cyclic (2× per rev) | Cyclic | Guy fatigue |
| **Storm / parked condition** | None | None | Pre-tension only | Wind moment | Anchor pull-out |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** When a numerical question gives "boom angle, load, and guy angle," the expected four-step solution is always:
> 1. Resolve load → horizontal pull $H = P\cos\theta$
> 2. Compute guy tension $T_g = H/(2\cos\phi)$
> 3. Compute mast base moment $M = H \cdot y_p$
> 4. Compute combined stress $\sigma = R_V/A + M/Z$
>
> Practice this 4-step pattern until it is automatic.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (14-mark type):** A guy derrick has a mast of height **18 m**, a boom of length **12 m** inclined at **30°** to the horizontal, and a load of **40 kN** at the boom tip. The boom self-weight is **0.8 kN/m**. Four guy ropes are anchored at **45°** to the ground. The boom pivot is at **15 m** above the base. Calculate (a) the tension in each guy rope and (b) the maximum bending moment in the mast.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Effective load on the boom tip:

$$P = W + w_b L = 40 + (0.8)(12) = 40 + 9.6 = 49.6 \text{ kN}$$

Horizontal force at mast top:

$$H = P \cos\theta = 49.6 \times \cos 30° = 49.6 \times 0.866 = 42.95 \text{ kN}$$

Guy-rope tension (using the 2-guy-in-plane model with 4 guys total, so 2 share the load):

$$T_g = \dfrac{H}{2 \cos\phi} = \dfrac{42.95}{2 \times \cos 45°} = \dfrac{42.95}{2 \times 0.7071} = \dfrac{42.95}{1.4142} = 30.37 \text{ kN}$$

Step (b) — Bending moment at mast base:

$$M_{base} = H \cdot y_p = 42.95 \times 15 = 644.25 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Answers:** $T_g \approx 30.4$ kN per guy, $M_{base} \approx 644$ kN·m.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Derrick Crane Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
