```mermaid
    A[Power Pack - Diesel or Electric Motor] --> B[Main Gearbox]
    B --> C1[Hoist Winch Drum]
    B --> C2[Bull Wheel Drum]
    B --> C3[Slewing Pinion]
    C1 --> D1[Load Line Rope]
    C2 --> D2[Topping Lift Rope]
    C3 --> D3[Slew Ring and Roller Track]
    D1 --> E1[Boom Tip Sheave]
    D2 --> E2[Mast Top Sheave]
    E1 --> F[Load Block and Hook]
    D3 --> G[Boom Slewing Assembly]
    G --> H[Boom Jib]
    H --> I1[Guy Rope Anchor Set 1]
    H --> I2[Guy Rope Anchor Set 2]
    H --> I3[Mast Base Sill and Foundation]
    J[Operator Cabin with Controls] --> B
    K[Safety Limit Switches] --> B
```

## 4.2 Force-Transmission Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Power Source]
        M1[Engine or Motor]
    end
    subgraph TRX[Transmission]
        M1 --> GB1[Gearbox]
        GB1 --> W1[Hoist Winch]
        GB1 --> W2[Bull Wheel]
        GB1 --> SL1[Slewing Motor]
    end
    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Output]
        W1 --> R1[Load Line]
        W2 --> R2[Topping Lift]
        SL1 --> R3[Slew Drive]
    end
    subgraph STR[Structural Frame]
        R1 --> BT1[Boom Tip]
        R2 --> BT1
        R3 --> MAST1[Mast]
        BT1 --> MAST1
        MAST1 --> GR1[Guy Ropes]
        MAST1 --> BS1[Base Sill]
    end
    subgraph LD[Load Path]
        BT1 --> BLK1[Load Block]
        BLK1 --> LD1[Suspended Load]
    end
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Schematic of a Guy Derrick (Block-Level Functional Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ground Anchor Block A] -. Tension Tg .-> C[Mast Top]
    B[Ground Anchor Block B] -. Tension Tg .-> C
    F[Ground Anchor Block F] -. Tension Tg .-> C
    G[Ground Anchor Block G] -. Tension Tg .-> C
    C --> D[Boom Pivot Point]
    D --> E[Boom Tip Sheave]
    H[Hoist Drum] -->|Load Line| E
    E -->|Load Hook| L[Suspended Load W]
    I[Bull Wheel Drum] -->|Topping Lift| C
    C --> J[Mast Column]
    J --> K[Base Sill]
    K --> S[Foundation Pad]
    R[Reactions at Sill] --> K
```

## 4.4 Component Hierarchy Table

| Subsystem | Components | Function | Failure Mode if Omitted |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Pack | Motor, coupling, brake | Convert fuel/electricity to mechanical energy | No lift possible |
| Transmission | Gearbox, clutches, couplings | Match torque-speed to drum needs | Motor burnout |
| Hoist Train | Winch drum, load line, hook block, sheaves | Convert torque to vertical lift | Load cannot be raised |
| Topping-Lift Train | Bull wheel, topping-lift rope, mast-top sheave | Raise / lower boom angle | Fixed boom radius only |
| Slewing Train | Pinion, slew ring, rollers | Rotate boom horizontally | Cannot reposition load |
| Structural Frame | Mast, boom, sills, base | Carry all loads to ground | Catastrophic collapse |
| Stabilizing System | Guys or stiff legs | Resist horizontal pull from boom | Mast overturns |
| Control System | Cabin, levers, limit switches, anemometer | Operator interface and safety | Runaway load / over-travel |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define a derrick crane and list its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A derrick crane is a lifting appliance consisting of a vertical **mast** supported by **guy ropes** (or stiff legs), a pivoted **boom** carrying a hoisting rope, and a **slewing mechanism** at the base. Main components: mast, boom, guy ropes, topping lift, bull wheel, load line, slewing gear, base sill, and operator cabin. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming, 1 for short justification of one component.)*

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Distinguish between a Guy Derrick and a Stiff-Leg Derrick.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Guy Derrick | Stiff-Leg Derrick |
|---|---|---|
| Support | Flexible wire-rope guys anchored to ground | Rigid inclined struts forming a portal |
| Footprint | Large (guy spread) | Compact (within base rectangle) |
| Site requirement | Open ground for anchors | Congested / urban sites |
| Capacity | Higher (limited by guy tension) | Limited by strut buckling |
| Erection | Easier to dismantle and re-erect | Heavier to dismantle |

*(3 marks: 1 for support distinction, 1 for site/footprint, 1 for capacity.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In derrick-crane numericals, students **commonly lose 3 to 4 marks** for: (i) forgetting boom self-weight $w_b L$, (ii) using 4 guys in the denominator instead of the 2 effective guys in the boom plane, (iii) using boom length $L$ where the **pivot height $y_p$** was intended, and (iv) writing bending moment as $H \cdot L$ instead of $H \cdot y_p$. Memorize the four-step pattern in §3.3 to avoid all of these.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Guy Derrick Crane**. List its main components and state two advantages and two limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A guy derrick has a mast height of **20 m**, boom length **14 m** inclined at **45°** to the horizontal, and is lifting a load of **50 kN** at the boom tip. The boom weighs **1.0 kN/m**. Four guy ropes are anchored at **60°** to the ground. The boom pivot is **17 m** above the base. Calculate:
- (i) The effective load on the boom tip
- (ii) The horizontal pull at the mast top
- (iii) The tension in each guy rope
- (iv) The maximum bending moment at the base of the mast *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

A guy derrick consists of:
1. **Mast (king post):** A vertical structural member, typically a lattice steel column of 6 to 30 m height, mounted on a base sill.
2. **Boom:** An inclined jib of 5 to 20 m length, pivoted at the lower portion of the mast and supported at its head by a **topping lift** running over a sheave at the mast top.
3. **Guy ropes:** Four (or more) steel wire ropes anchored to the ground, providing lateral stability.
4. **Bull wheel:** Large drum on the mast that tensions the topping lift.
5. **Hoisting arrangement:** Winch, load line, sheaves at the boom tip, and load block with hook.
6. **Slewing gear:** Rack-and-pinion or chain-drive mechanism for rotating the boom.
7. **Base sill and foundation:** Heavy timber or steel base distributing loads to the ground.

**Working:** The hoist winch lifts the load via the load line passing over the boom-tip sheave. The bull wheel adjusts the boom angle via the topping lift. The slewing gear rotates the entire boom assembly on a roller track. The guy ropes resist the horizontal component of the boom pull.

**Advantages:** (i) High lifting capacity at low cost, (ii) can be erected at heights exceeding mobile-crane reach, (iii) stable in high winds once guyed.
**Limitations:** (i) Requires large clear ground area for guy anchors, (ii) non-mobile, (iii) slow slewing and lifting speeds.

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 2 for components, 1 for working, 1 each for advantages and limitations.)*

**Part (b):**

Step (i) — Effective load on boom tip:

$$P = W + w_b L = 50 + (1.0)(14) = 64 \text{ kN}$$

Step (ii) — Horizontal pull at mast top:

$$H = P \cos\theta = 64 \times \cos 45° = 64 \times 0.7071 = 45.25 \text{ kN}$$

Step (iii) — Guy-rope tension (2 effective guys in the boom plane):

$$T_g = \dfrac{H}{2 \cos\phi} = \dfrac{45.25}{2 \times \cos 60°} = \dfrac{45.25}{2 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{45.25}{1.0} = 45.25 \text{ kN}$$

Step (iv) — Maximum bending moment at mast base:

$$M_{base} = H \cdot y_p = 45.25 \times 17 = 769.3 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $P = 64$ kN
- (ii) $H = 45.25$ kN
- (iii) $T_g = 45.25$ kN per guy
- (iv) $M_{base} = 769.3$ kN·m

*(7 marks: 1 each for (i)–(iv) setup, 1 for correct final values, 1 for units, 1 for logical flow.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Compare **Stiff-Leg Derrick** and **Breast Derrick** with respect to (i) structural support, (ii) typical applications, (iii) load capacity, and (iv) site footprint. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A stiff-leg derrick has two rigid struts of length **8 m** each, hinged at the base sills **6 m apart**, and connected at the top of a **12 m mast**. A load of **30 kN** is suspended from a **10 m boom** inclined at **30°** to the horizontal. The boom self-weight is **0.6 kN/m**. Determine:
- (i) The horizontal pull on the mast top
- (ii) The compression in each stiff leg
- (iii) The vertical reaction at the mast base
- (iv) The hoist-motor power required if the lifting speed is **6 m/min** and the overall efficiency is **75%** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

| Parameter | Stiff-Leg Derrick | Breast Derrick |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Support | Two rigid inclined struts hinged at ground | Boom pivots from a wall or structure |
| (ii) Application | High-rise construction, tower erection | Harbour cargo, ship-side loading |
| (iii) Capacity | Medium to high (50–200 kN) | Low to medium (10–50 kN) |
| (iv) Footprint | Compact (within base rectangle) | Minimal (one side against wall) |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per row, 1 mark for overall clarity and tabular format.)*

**Part (b):**

Step (i) — Effective load on the boom:

$$P = W + w_b L = 30 + (0.6)(10) = 36 \text{ kN}$$

Horizontal pull at the mast top:

$$H = P \cos\theta = 36 \times \cos 30° = 36 \times 0.866 = 31.18 \text{ kN}$$

Step (ii) — Compression in each stiff leg:

The two stiff legs form an isosceles triangle with the mast. Each leg makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical such that:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{3}{12} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 14.04°$$

The horizontal component of the pull is shared equally by the two legs (assuming symmetry and the pull in the plane of the legs):

$$T_{leg} \sin\alpha = \dfrac{H}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{leg} = \dfrac{H}{2 \sin\alpha} = \dfrac{31.18}{2 \times \sin 14.04°} = \dfrac{31.18}{2 \times 0.2425} = \dfrac{31.18}{0.485} = 64.29 \text{ kN}$$

Step (iii) — Vertical reaction at the mast base:

The vertical reaction supports the load, the boom weight, the mast weight, and the vertical component of the leg forces. Using a simplified balance (neglecting mast and leg weights for clarity, the dominant term is the load):

$$R_V = W + w_b L = 30 + 6 = 36 \text{ kN}$$

(Including the small additional vertical components of the leg forces, $R_V \approx 36 + 2 T_{leg} \cos\alpha \approx 36 + 2(64.29)(0.970) \approx 36 + 124.7 = 160.7$ kN — **note:** the second term is the *downward* push of the legs on the mast cap transmitted to the base; in KTU problems, the simple form $R_V = W + w_b L$ is acceptable when leg geometry is not specified for vertical balance.)

Step (iv) — Hoist-motor power:

$$P_{kW} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{6120 \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{3000 \times 6}{6120 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{18000}{4590} = 3.92 \text{ kW}$$

(Note: $W = 30$ kN $\equiv 3000$ kgf for this formula.)

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $H = 31.18$ kN
- (ii) $T_{leg} = 64.3$ kN per leg
- (iii) $R_V = 36$ kN (simplified) or $\approx 160.7$ kN (including leg vertical components)
- (iv) $P_{motor} \approx 3.92$ kW

*(7 marks: 1.5 for (i), 2 for (ii) — leg geometry often loses marks, 1.5 for (iii), 2 for (iv).)*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition first, classification second, derivation third** — the KTU board examiner expects answers in this logical order in 14-mark questions.
- **Three principal types** of derricks: **Guy**, **Stiff-Leg**, and **Breast**. Always state the *support mechanism* first when distinguishing.
- **Five mandatory formulas** to memorize: (i) effective load $P = W + w_b L$, (ii) horizontal pull $H = P\cos\theta$, (iii) guy tension $T_g = H/(2\cos\phi)$, (iv) mast base moment $M = H \cdot y_p$, (v) hoist power $P_{kW} = Wv/(6120\eta)$.
- **Always include boom self-weight** in numericals unless the problem explicitly states "neglect boom weight."
- **Two effective guys** (not four) carry the horizontal pull in the boom plane; this is the most-missed point in 14-mark numericals.
- **Bending moment uses pivot height $y_p$**, not boom length $L$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
- **Combined stress check** at the mast base uses $\sigma_{max} = R_V/A + M/Z$ — Part B "design" sub-questions expect this full form.
- **Hoist-power formula uses $W$ in kgf and $v$ in m/min** with the constant 6120. Do not mix SI units with this constant.
- **Slewing produces cyclic guy loading** — fatigue is the dominant design consideration for cyclic operation, not static strength.
- **Counterweight / stiff legs** are required whenever the boom is at a low angle (large horizontal pull); the **classic Part C design question** asks the student to size the counterweight for a given horizontal pull.
- **Safety devices** — every derrick crane must have (i) hoist limit switch, (ii) boom-angle indicator, (iii) anemometer for wind, (iv) overload cutoff, (v) slewing limit. A 3-mark question on these is a recurring favourite.
- **The $6120$ constant** in the hoist-power formula comes from $60 \times 102$ — the **60** converts minutes to seconds and the **102** converts kgf to N. Students who derive this on paper earn bonus credit in the valuations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Cranes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mobile Cranes

# 🏗️ MOBILE CRANES — A KTU 2024 Premium Study Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Mobile Crane** is a cable-controlled crane mounted on crawlers, rubber tires, or specially designed truck carriers, capable of moving freely between job sites under their own power (self-propelled) or by being towed. They are designed for intermittent lifting operations and are widely employed in construction, infrastructure, and industrial erection works.

---

## 🧠 Intuitive Overview — "The Walking Arm of Construction"

Imagine a giant **mechanical arm on wheels** that can roll up to a construction site, extend a long telescoping limb, pick up a multi-tonne steel beam or concrete bucket, swing it 360°, and gently place it atop a building — all within minutes. That is the essence of a mobile crane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Analogy:**
> Think of a mobile crane as a **"human arm on a vehicle"**:
> - The **chassis (carrier)** = the legs that walk the arm to the site.
> - The **outriggers** = the feet that "plant" the crane firmly to prevent tipping.
> - The **boom** = the upper arm that reaches out and up.
> - The **jib (extension)** = the fingertip that fine-tunes reach.
> - The **hook + load** = the object held in the hand.
> - The **counterweight** = the muscle at the back of the shoulder that keeps the arm balanced.

The unique advantage of mobile cranes is their **mobility + setup speed**, making them the "go-anywhere, lift-anything" machines in modern construction projects.

---

## 📋 Classification of Mobile Cranes (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| S.No | Type of Mobile Crane | Mounting / Mobility | Typical Use |
|:----:|:--------------------|:--------------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Crawler Crane** | Mounted on caterpillar tracks | Heavy lifting, soft ground, long-duration jobs |
| 2 | **Truck-Mounted Crane** | Mounted on a standard truck chassis | Highway travel + medium-lift site work |
| 3 | **All-Terrain Crane** | Hybrid truck carrier (on/off road) | Versatile site movement |
| 4 | **Rough Terrain Crane** | 4×4 wheel carrier, single cab | Off-road, unprepared sites |
| 5 | **Pick-and-Carry Crane** | Pneumatic-tired; rear-wheel steer | Industrial yards, short-radius lifts |
| 6 | **Telescopic Boom Crane** | Hydraulic boom with multiple sections | Variable reach, fast setup |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "CTARP":** **C**rawler, **T**ruck-mounted, **A**ll-terrain, **R**ough-terrain, **P**ick-and-carry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — Components, Working & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Principal Components of a Mobile Crane

A mobile crane, irrespective of type, is built from the following subsystems:

- **Carrier (Chassis):** Provides mobility. Houses the engine, transmission, and travel mechanism.
- **Superstructure (Upperworks):** The rotating portion that mounts the cab, boom, and counterweight.
- **Operator's Cab:** Houses the control levers, gauges, Load Moment Indicator (LMI), and slewing controls.
- **Boom:** The primary lifting member — either **lattice (lattice boom)** or **telescopic (hydraulic)**.
- **Jib (Extension):** A secondary boom attached to the main boom to extend reach.
- **Counterweight:** Fixed or modular weights at the rear of the superstructure to balance the load.
- **Outriggers (Stabilizers):** Hydraulic beams with jacks that extend outward and downward to level and stabilize the crane.
- **Hook Block & Hook:** The attachment point for the load.
- **Hoist Drums & Wire Ropes:** Provide lifting and lowering motion.
- **Slewing Gear:** Allows 360° rotation of the superstructure.

---

## 2. Working Principle — The Load Moment Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:**
> A mobile crane works on the principle of **lever and fulcrum**. The slewing axis (center of rotation) acts as the **pivot (fulcrum)**, while the **load × its horizontal distance (radius)** creates a **tipping moment** that must always be balanced by the **counterweight moment** about the same pivot.

When a crane lifts a load, two opposing moments act on it:

$$
M_{load} = W \times R
$$

$$
M_{resist} = W_{cw} \times d
$$

For the crane to remain stable, the **resisting moment ≥ tipping moment × safety factor**, typically with a **Factor of Safety (FoS) of 1.25 (or 75% tipping load)** as per ASME B30.5 and Indian Standard **IS 4573 / IS 3177**.

---

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|:----:|:----------|:---------------------|:------|:------|
| 1 | **Load Moment (Tipping)** | $M_t = W \times R$ | tonne·m (or kN·m) | W = load, R = radius |
| 2 | **Resisting Moment** | $M_r = W_{cw} \times d_{cw}$ | tonne·m | d_cw = distance of CW from pivot |
| 3 | **Net Capacity** | $W_{net} = W_{gross} - W_{rigging} - W_{block}$ | tonnes | Subtract hook, slings, etc. |
| 4 | **Net Load Moment** | $LM_{net} = W_{net} \times R$ | tonne·m | Used for LMI calibration |
| 5 | **Percentage of Tipping Load (%)** | $\%T = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{tipping}} \times 100$ | % | Must be < 75% (FoS=1.33) or < 80% (FoS=1.25) |
| 6 | **Effective Radius** | $R_{eff} = R_{load} + R_{swing} + R_{deflection}$ | m | Includes boom deflection |
| 7 | **Height of Lift** | $H = h_1 + h_2 + h_3 + h_4 + S$ | m | See derivation below |
| 8 | **Ground Bearing Pressure (Outrigger)** | $p = \dfrac{Total\ Load}{n \times A_{pad}}$ | kN/m² | n = no. of outriggers |
| 9 | **Counterweight Required** | $W_{cw} = \dfrac{W \times R}{d_{cw} \times FS}$ | tonnes | For known load and radius |
| 10 | **Crawler Ground Pressure** | $p = \dfrac{Weight\ on\ Track}{2 \times L \times b}$ | kN/m² | L = track length in contact, b = shoe width |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Absolute Value Notation:**
> Never write `|x|` inside markdown tables — it **breaks the column delimiter**. In your **derivation and answer sheets**, use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` instead.

---

## 4. Real-World Engineering Utility

- Used for **erecting steel structures, precast panels, and heavy machinery** in high-rise and industrial projects.
- In **Kerala's post-flood reconstruction**, mobile cranes have been critical for bridge girder placement, high-rise residential towers, and metro rail projects (Kochi Metro).
- In **wind energy**, crawler cranes with **lattice booms** are used to erect 100+ m turbines.

---

## 5. Selection Criteria (KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight)

The selection of a mobile crane for a specific job is governed by:

1. **Maximum load weight (with rigging deductions).**
2. **Maximum lift radius required (boom tip to load centre).**
3. **Maximum hook height required.**
4. **Site accessibility (road width, slope, ground bearing capacity).**
5. **Travel distance (own power or transport by trailer).**
6. **Cycle time and number of lifts per shift.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## DERIVATION 1: Determination of Required Boom Length for a Given Lift

> **Problem Context (Classical KTU 2-mark):**
> Find the minimum boom length required to lift a load over an obstacle of given height, when the boom is anchored at a known radius from the obstacle.

### Given:
- Height of obstacle (wall/structure), $h_w$ = 6 m  
- Horizontal distance from crane centre to obstacle, $d$ = 8 m  
- Clearance above obstacle, $S$ = 2 m  
- Boom pivot height above ground, $h_p$ = 2 m  
- Hook block + sling height, $h_b$ = 1.5 m  
- Required lift height, $h_l$ = 0 (load placed at top of obstacle)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The boom must clear the obstacle and reach the load. The minimum boom length is the **straight-line distance from boom pivot to the load pick point** plus the **hook block height**.

**Step 2 — Compute the vertical height the boom tip must reach above the boom pivot.**

$$
H_{req} = h_w + S + h_b
$$

Substituting:

$$
H_{req} = 6 + 2 + 1.5 = 9.5 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the horizontal reach (radius) to the load.**

$$
R = d + r_{load}
$$

Assuming the load is placed at the centre of the obstacle, $r_{load} = 0$:

$$
R = 8 + 0 = 8 \ \text{m}
$$

> *(Note: For an off-centre load, $r_{load}$ is the horizontal distance from the obstacle face to the load's centre of gravity.)*

**Step 4 — Apply the Pythagorean Theorem for minimum boom length.**

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{H_{req}^{2} + R^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{(9.5)^{2} + (8)^{2}}
$$

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{90.25 + 64}
$$

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{154.25}
$$

$$
L_{boom} \approx 12.42 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Add a Safety Margin (typical 10–15% for boom deflection + rigging stretch).**

$$
L_{boom,design} = 1.15 \times 12.42 \approx 14.28 \ \text{m}
$$

> **∴ Minimum boom length required ≈ 14.3 m** (choose next standard boom length: 15 m).

---

## DERIVATION 2: Net Lifting Capacity After Rigging Deductions

### Given:
- Gross capacity from load chart at $R = 10$ m radius: $W_{gross} = 20$ tonnes  
- Weight of hook block: $W_{block} = 0.5$ t  
- Weight of slings + shackles: $W_{rigging} = 0.3$ t  
- Weight of load below the hook (e.g., bucket, spreader beam): $W_{aux} = 0.7$ t

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Total rigging deduction.**

$$
W_{ded} = W_{block} + W_{rigging} + W_{aux}
$$

$$
W_{ded} = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.7 = 1.5 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Net capacity available for the actual structural load.**

$$
W_{net} = W_{gross} - W_{ded}
$$

$$
W_{net} = 20 - 1.5 = 18.5 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

> **∴ Net lift = 18.5 tonnes** — this is the actual mass of the structural component the crane may safely pick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:**
> Marks are frequently lost when students **forget the hook block** in their deduction. Always quote: "**Hook block + slings + any in-line auxiliary equipment**."

---

## DERIVATION 3: Counterweight Required for Stability

### Given:
- Maximum load $W = 30$ t at radius $R = 12$ m  
- Distance of counterweight from slewing axis $d_{cw} = 4$ m  
- Factor of Safety $FS = 1.25$ (75% tipping rule)

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Tipping moment (load side).**

$$
M_{tip} = W \times R = 30 \times 12 = 360 \ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Required resisting moment (with safety factor).**

$$
M_{req} = M_{tip} \times FS = 360 \times 1.25 = 450 \ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Counterweight needed.**

$$
W_{cw} = \dfrac{M_{req}}{d_{cw}} = \dfrac{450}{4} = 112.5 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

> **∴ Counterweight required ≈ 112.5 t**

> [!WARNING]
> In modular counterweight systems, you must choose the **next higher standard module** (e.g., 115 t) — never interpolate below.

---

## DERIVATION 4: Outrigger Pad Bearing Pressure

### Given:
- Total load on outrigger (crane + load + counterweight): $T = 80$ tonnes = 800 kN  
- Number of outriggers: $n = 4$  
- Pad area: $A_{pad} = 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36 \ \text{m}^2$

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Maximum reaction on a single outrigger** (assuming symmetric loading, otherwise use worst case).

$$
R = \dfrac{T}{n} = \dfrac{800}{4} = 200 \ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing pressure.**

$$
p = \dfrac{R}{A_{pad}} = \dfrac{200}{0.36} \approx 555.56 \ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with safe ground bearing capacity.**

> If safe ground bearing capacity = 200 kN/m², then the pad is undersized. The required pad area is:

$$
A_{req} = \dfrac{R}{p_{safe}} = \dfrac{200}{200} = 1.0 \ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Required pad size} = \sqrt{1.0} = 1.0 \ \text{m} \times 1.0 \ \text{m}
$$

> **∴ Use 1.0 m × 1.0 m timber mats or steel plates under each outrigger.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural & Functional Schematics (Mermaid Diagrams)

## Diagram 1: Mobile Crane — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Cabin] --> B[Load Moment Indicator LMI]
    A --> C[Slewing Control Unit]
    A --> D[Hoist Drum Controller]
    A --> E[Boom Telescoping Valve]
    A --> F[Outrigger Control Panel]
    B --> G[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Hydraulic Cylinders]
    H --> I[Boom Raise / Lower]
    H --> J[Outrigger Extend / Retract]
    H --> K[Winch Rotation]
    L[Diesel Engine] --> M[Transmission]
    M --> N[Drive Axles]
    N --> O[Carrier Wheels or Crawler Tracks]
    P[Counterweight] --> Q[Superstructure Frame]
    Q --> R[Slewing Ring Bearing]
    R --> S[Carrier Chassis]
    T[Hook Block] --> U[Wire Rope from Drum]
```

---

## Diagram 2: Decision Flow for Crane Selection (KTU 2024 Skill Outcome)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Lift Requirements] --> Q1{Is site accessible by road?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is ground soft or hard?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need to move crane on site?}
    Q2 -- Soft/Uneven --> C1[Use Crawler Crane]
    Q2 -- Hard/Level --> C2[Use Truck-Mounted or All-Terrain Crane]
    Q3 -- Yes --> C3[Use Rough Terrain Crane]
    Q3 -- No / short distance --> C4[Use Pick and Carry Crane]
    C1 --> Q4{Required lift > 100 t?}
    C2 --> Q4
    C3 --> Q4
    C4 --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> L1[Use Lattice Boom Crane]
    Q4 -- No --> L2[Use Telescopic Boom Crane]
    L1 --> End[Final Selection: Verify Load Chart + Safety]
    L2 --> End
```

---

## Diagram 3: Mobile Crane — Stability & Tipping Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Load[Load W at Radius R] --> Moment1[Tipping Moment Mt = W x R]
    CounterW[Counterweight Wcw at distance dcw] --> Moment2[Resisting Moment Mr = Wcw x dcw]
    Moment1 --> Compare{Is Mt x FS <= Mr?}
    Moment2 --> Compare
    Compare -- Yes --> Safe[SAFE: Proceed with Lift]
    Compare -- No --> Unsafe[UNSAFE: Reduce Load or Increase CW]
    Unsafe --> Corrective[Add Counterweight Modules or Reduce Radius]
    Corrective --> Compare
```

---

## Diagram 4: Boom Geometry for Clearance Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boom Pivot] -->|Hp above ground| B[Boom Tip]
    B -->|Vertical reach Hreq| C[Load Pick Point]
    A -->|Horizontal R| D[Base of Wall]
    D -->|Wall Height hw| E[Top of Wall]
    E -->|Clearance S| C
    C -->|Hook Block hb| B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Differentiate between a Crawler Crane and a Truck-Mounted Crane. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Crawler Crane | Truck-Mounted Crane |
|:--------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| Mobility | Slow (5–6 km/h) on crawler tracks | Fast (40–60 km/h) on rubber tyres |
| Site setup | No outriggers needed (tracks spread load) | Outriggers essential |
| Ground suitability | Soft, uneven, muddy ground | Hard, prepared roads |
| Lift capacity | Higher (up to 3500 t) | Moderate (up to 1200 t) |
| Road travel | Must be transported on trailers | Self-driven on highways |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for distinction table – 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. What is a Load Moment Indicator (LMI)? State its significance. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** is an electronic safety device fitted in a mobile crane that continuously monitors the **load moment (W × R)** and compares it to the crane's permissible capacity at the prevailing radius and boom configuration. It gives **visual and audio warnings** when the operator approaches the safe limit and **automatically cuts off the lifting function** if exceeded.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- Prevents **overloading and structural failure** of the boom.
- Protects against **tipping accidents** — the most common cause of crane fatalities globally.
- Mandatory as per **IS 4573, ASME B30.5, and KTU safety regulations** for all hydraulic mobile cranes above 10 t capacity.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2 Choice]**

A construction project requires lifting a **precast concrete panel weighing 18 tonnes** to a height of **22 m** on the 4th floor of a building under construction. The crane will be positioned **10 m away from the building face**. Available crane has a **30-tonne rated capacity at 12 m radius with full outriggers**.

**(a)** Calculate the **minimum boom length** required for this lift. (7 Marks)  
**(b)** Determine whether the crane is **safe to perform the lift**, including all rigging deductions. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a) — Minimum Boom Length (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
- $h_w = 22 - 2 = 20$ m (wall/obstacle height, assuming 2 m above 4th floor slab)  
- $d = 10$ m (horizontal distance from crane to building)  
- $S = 1.5$ m (clearance above lift point)  
- $h_b = 1.0$ m (hook block + sling)  
- $r_{load} = 0$ (load directly at edge of building)

**Step 2 — Required vertical reach above boom pivot (2 marks).**

$$
H_{req} = h_w + S + h_b = 20 + 1.5 + 1.0 = 22.5 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal radius (1 mark).**

$$
R = d + r_{load} = 10 + 0 = 10 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum boom length (2 marks).**

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{H_{req}^{2} + R^{2}} = \sqrt{(22.5)^{2} + (10)^{2}}
$$

$$
L_{boom} = \sqrt{506.25 + 100} = \sqrt{606.25} \approx 24.62 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — With 10% safety margin (1 mark).**

$$
L_{boom,design} = 1.10 \times 24.62 \approx 27.08 \ \text{m}
$$

> **∴ Minimum boom length required ≈ 27.1 m.** (Select a crane with 28 m or longer boom.)

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b) — Safety Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply rigging deductions (2 marks).**
- Hook block: 0.4 t  
- Slings + shackles: 0.3 t  
- Spreader beam: 0.6 t  
- Total deduction: $W_{ded} = 1.3$ t

**Step 2 — Net load to be lifted (1 mark).**

$$
W_{net} = 18 + 1.3 = 19.3 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with crane capacity at operating radius (2 marks).**

Operating radius ≈ 10 m (with full outriggers)  
Rated capacity at 10 m = 30 t (given)  
Since 19.3 t < 30 t → **SAFE on capacity criterion**.

**Step 4 — Check the load moment against stability (1 mark).**

$$
M_{tip} = 19.3 \times 10 = 193 \ \text{t·m}
$$

For 75% tipping rule: $M_{allow} = 0.75 \times$ (Tipping Moment from chart) — assuming chart shows 280 t·m tipping:

$$
193 < 0.75 \times 280 = 210 \ \text{t·m} \rightarrow \text{SAFE}
$$

**Step 5 — Verdict (1 mark).**

> **∴ The crane IS safe** for this lift **only if** the boom can be extended to 27.1 m, the radius is held at ≤ 10 m, and the outriggers are fully extended on firm ground. The operator should also engage the LMI throughout the lift.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 2 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **components of a mobile crane**, clearly labelling the **boom, jib, counterweight, outriggers, hook block, and slewing ring**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A **truck-mounted crane** is required to lift a load of **15 t at 8 m radius**. The crane has a **gross chart capacity of 22 t at 8 m**. The crane is fitted with **hook block (0.5 t), 4-leg sling (0.4 t), and a small spreader (0.3 t)**. Determine the **net load** the crane can safely handle, and the **percentage of its tipping load** if the chart's tipping load at 8 m is 28 t. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a) — Components (7 marks)**

*(Use Mermaid Diagram 1 above as the reference. Each component carries 1 mark for naming + 0.5 mark for function.)*

1. **Boom** — Primary lifting arm; raises/lowers and telescopes. (1 mark)  
2. **Jib** — Secondary extension fitted to boom tip for additional reach. (1 mark)  
3. **Counterweight** — Rear-mounted mass to balance the load moment. (1 mark)  
4. **Outriggers** — Hydraulic stabilizers extended sideways and downward to level and stabilize the crane. (1 mark)  
5. **Hook Block** — Contains hook and sheaves; weight of block must be deducted from capacity. (1 mark)  
6. **Slewing Ring Bearing** — Large-diameter bearing that allows the entire superstructure to rotate 360° over the carrier. (1 mark)  
7. **Operator's Cab** — Houses all controls including LMI, hoist, slew, and boom levers. (1 mark)

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b) — Net Load & % Tipping (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Total rigging deduction (2 marks).**

$$
W_{ded} = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 1.2 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum allowable lift at 8 m radius (1 mark).**

$$
W_{allow} = W_{gross} - W_{ded} = 22 - 1.2 = 20.8 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Net actual load to be lifted (1 mark).**

$$
W_{actual} = 15 \ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of tipping load (2 marks).**

$$
\%T = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{tipping}} \times 100 = \dfrac{15}{28} \times 100 \approx 53.57\%
$$

**Step 5 — Safety verdict (1 mark).**

Since 53.57% < 75% → **WITHIN SAFE STABILITY LIMIT (75% rule).**

> **∴ The crane can safely lift 15 t; 20.8 t remains as reserve capacity; % of tipping load = 53.6%.**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Mobile Crane problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting hook block + slings** in rigging deductions — costs a full 2-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Using boom tip radius** instead of **load centre radius** — gives wrong moment and wrong verdict.
> 3. **Not applying the 75% tipping rule** — examiners expect this as a default safety check.
> 4. **Confusing truck-mounted with all-terrain cranes** — definitions carry 1 mark each.
> 5. **Skipping a labelled sketch** for "components" question — at least **3 marks reserved** for a clean, labelled diagram in part-(a) of long answers.
> 6. **Not adding safety margin** (10–15%) in boom length derivations.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ A **mobile crane** is a self-propelled, cable/hydraulically controlled lifting machine mounted on crawlers, tyres, or a truck carrier.
- ☐ The **5 principal types** are: **C**rawler, **T**ruck-mounted, **A**ll-terrain, **R**ough-terrain, **P**ick-and-carry (**"CTARP"**).
- ☐ **Load Moment = Load × Radius** — this is the foundation of all stability calculations.
- ☐ Always deduct: **Hook block + slings + spreader + any in-line auxiliary equipment** from gross capacity.
- ☐ **75% rule (ASME B30.5 / IS 4573):** Never lift more than 75% of the tipping load (FoS = 1.33) over outriggers; 80% for pick-and-carry (FoS = 1.25).
- ☐ **LMI (Load Moment Indicator)** is a mandatory electronic safety device on hydraulic mobile cranes.
- ☐ **Outriggers** must be fully extended and pads placed on firm, level ground with adequate bearing capacity.
- ☐ **Minimum boom length** = $\sqrt{H_{req}^{2} + R^{2}}$ + safety margin (10–15%).
- ☐ **Crawler cranes** have low ground pressure (≈ 50–100 kN/m²) → ideal for soft ground.
- ☐ **Counterweight** must be increased (or radius reduced) if tipping moment exceeds 75% of resisting moment.
- ☐ **Wind, swing, and boom deflection** must be added to the operational radius for accurate load chart reading.
- ☐ Always read the **load chart** for the **specific configuration** (outrigger extension, counterweight package, number of boom sections).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overhead Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overhead Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Overhead Crane (Bridge Crane):** An *overhead travelling crane* is a material-handling machine in which the **hoisting mechanism** is mounted on a **trolley** that traverses a **bridge girder**, while the bridge itself runs longitudinally on elevated **gantry rails** (runway tracks) supported by building columns or free-standing gantry legs. The crane operates within a three-dimensional rectangular working envelope defined by *span (S)*, *lift (H)*, and *runway length (L)*.

The salient specification parameters (as per **IS 3177 : 1999** and **IS 807 : 2006**) are:

- **Safe Working Load (SWL)** — Maximum vertical load the crane is designed to lift, expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**.
- **Span (S)** — Centre-to-centre distance between the two runway rails, in **metres (m)**.
- **Lift (H)** — Vertical hook travel above the floor level, in **metres (m)**.
- **Crane Runway Length (L)** — Total longitudinal travel distance, in **metres (m)**.
- **Class of Duty / Duty Cycle** — Light, Medium, Heavy, or Severe (as per **FEM / IS 3177** classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Overhead cranes are the most common *in-shop* / *in-plant* heavy-material-handling equipment in **steel fabrication yards**, **precast concrete casting beds**, **power plants**, **shipyards**, and **heavy-engineering workshops**. The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on **load combinations, wheel-load distribution, and fatigue design** of the bridge girder and runway girders.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ceiling Railway"

Imagine a **monorail train running on the ceiling of a large hall**. The "train" is the *trolley*, the "track" is the *bridge girder*, and the entire "ceiling track" running the length of the building is the *gantry runway*. Now imagine that the train itself can move sideways (across the hall) **and** lengthwise (along the hall) **and** can lower/raise a hook vertically. That is exactly how an overhead crane works.

A simple mental picture:

- **Building hall** = a workshop, e.g., a precast yard.
- **Trolley (with hook)** = moves *across* the hall (X-axis).
- **Bridge girder** = moves *along* the hall (Y-axis), carrying the trolley.
- **Hook + rope** = moves *up-down* (Z-axis), lifting the load.
- **Runway rails on columns** = the elevated tracks the bridge runs on.

> [!TIP]
> **Three-axis coverage** is what makes the overhead crane the *most versatile* in-plant material mover: any point inside the rectangular working envelope can be reached without the load ever touching the floor.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Overhead Cranes

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-running bridge crane** | Bridge rolls on rails *on top* of the runway girders | Heavy industry, power plants |
| **Under-running (suspended) crane** | Bridge is suspended *below* the runway girder flanges | Light-duty workshops |
| **Gantry crane** | Bridge supported on **leg columns** instead of building columns | Outdoor yards, shipyards |
| **Semi-gantry crane** | One end on building column runway, other on gantry leg | Mixed indoor/outdoor |
| **Jib crane (wall-mounted / pillar-mounted)** | A single arm rotating about a vertical axis | Localised lifting at a workstation |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examination purposes, the term *"overhead crane"* most commonly refers to the **EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) bridge crane** — a top-running, double-girder or single-girder bridge carrying an electric wire-rope hoist on a cross-travel trolley.

---

## 1.4 Main Components of an EOT Crane

1. **Bridge girders** (main girders) — carry the trolley rail; usually 2 nos. for double-girder cranes.
2. **End carriages** — connect the two bridge girders and carry the **bridge travel wheels**.
3. **Trolley** — carries the hoist; travels across the bridge on the trolley rail.
4. **Hoist** — wire-rope or chain type, with hook block, drum, motor, gearbox, brake.
5. **Bridge travel wheels** — run on the runway rails; **4-wheel, 8-wheel, or 16-wheel** configurations.
6. **Runway rails and girders** — supported on building columns.
7. **Crane girders / gantry girders** — the building's runway girders designed for crane loading.
8. **Buffers, rail sweeps, rail clips, electrification (conductor bars / festoon system)**.
9. **Operator's cabin** (or pendant / radio control).
10. **Bridge / trolley / hoist drive motors** with **long-travel, cross-travel, and hoist motions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How an Overhead Crane Works (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Load Engagement:** The hook is lowered, engaged to the load, and the hoist motor winds the rope on the drum, raising the load vertically (**hoist motion**).
- **Step 2 — Cross Travel:** The trolley motor drives the trolley wheels along the trolley rail fixed on the bridge girder, moving the load across the span (**cross-travel / traversing motion**).
- **Step 3 — Long Travel:** The bridge motors drive the bridge travel wheels along the runway rails, moving the entire bridge (with trolley + load) along the length of the building (**long-travel motion**).
- **Step 4 — Load Placement:** The hook is lowered at the destination, the load is disengaged, and the hook is hoisted back up.
- **Step 5 — Return Travel:** The bridge and trolley return to the loading point for the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each motion is driven by a **separate electric motor** with its own **fail-safe electromagnetic / disc brake** and **limit switch**. KTU examiners frequently test the **3-motion classification** and the role of **slack-rope / over-winding limiters**.

---

## 2.2 Design Load Combinations for Crane-Supporting Structures

Crane loads are **dynamic** and **moving**, so they are treated as **moving live loads** on the runway girders. As per **IS 875 (Part 2) : 1987** and **IS 800 : 2007 (clause 10.8)**:

$$C_c = C_{cg} + C_{cq}$$

where $C_{cg}$ = self-weight of crane, $C_{cq}$ = crane live load (vertical, longitudinal, lateral).

The **vertical wheel load** on a runway girder has three components to consider:

- **Static vertical wheel load** $V_s$
- **Impact factor** $I$ (to account for dynamic effects of lifting and sudden releases) — typically **1.1 to 1.25** for electric cranes per IS 875.
- **Vertical dynamic factor** $C_V$ — accounts for vibration due to travel on uneven rails.

The **maximum vertical wheel load** is:

$$V_{max} = V_s \times (1 + I) \times C_V$$

**Lateral (horizontal) surge load** — caused by acceleration/braking of the bridge or trolley, swinging of the load, and skewing of the bridge. Per IS 875, this is taken as a percentage of the **maximum vertical wheel load**:

$$H_L = k \cdot V_{max}$$

where the coefficient $k$ typically ranges from **0.05 (light duty)** to **0.20 (heavy / outdoor cranes)**.

**Longitudinal surge load** — caused by acceleration/braking of the bridge along the runway; often taken as **10 % of the static vertical wheel load**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Applicable Clause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{max} = \dfrac{(SWL + Trolley\_wt + Bridge\_wt)}{n} \times (1+I)$ | $n$ = number of wheels on one end carriage | kN | IS 875 / IS 800 |
| 2 | $H_L = k \cdot V_{max}$ | $k$ = lateral surge coefficient | 0.05 – 0.20 | IS 875 Part 2 |
| 3 | $H_{long} = 0.10 \cdot V_{max}$ | longitudinal surge | kN | IS 875 Part 2 |
| 4 | $M_{max} = \dfrac{(V_{max} \cdot a)(L - a)}{L}$ | $a$ = distance of load from support, $L$ = span | kN·m | Influence line for simply supported girder |
| 5 | $M_{max} \vert_{influence} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{4}$ (for single concentrated load at midspan) | $P$ = wheel load, $L$ = span | kN·m | Absolute max BM |
| 6 | $\sigma_{b,max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}}$ | $Z_{xx}$ = section modulus of runway girder | N/mm² | Bending check |
| 7 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{A_{web}}$ | $A_{web}$ = shear area of web | N/mm² | Shear check |
| 8 | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I}$ (mid-span) | $E I$ = flexural rigidity | mm | Deflection check |
| 9 | Permissible deflection | runway girder | $\delta \le L / 500$ to $L/1000$ | IS 800 / IS 456 |
| 10 | Fatigue stress range | $\Delta\sigma$ | N/mm² | IS 800 cl. 13 |
| 11 | Local flange buckling | $\dfrac{b}{t_f} \le \epsilon \sqrt{\dfrac{250}{f_y}}$ | $\epsilon = 0.55$ | IS 800 cl. 8.6 |
| 12 | Web crippling (under wheel) | bearing length check | — | IS 800 cl. 8.7 |
| 13 | Buffer stop reaction | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | energy absorbed | Crane stop design |
| 14 | Hoist motor power | $P = \dfrac{SWL \times v}{\eta}$ | kW, $v$ = hoist speed, $\eta$ = efficiency | Hoist design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not** confuse **"impact factor" (I)** in crane loading with the **"wind factor" or "seismic factor"** used in building design. In crane design, $I$ is *always* applied to the *vertical* wheel load to account for **dynamic amplification during lifting and lowering**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel plants** — overhead cranes of **100 t to 500 t SWL** are used to handle ladles, coils, and ingots. These are the *most heavily loaded* in-plant cranes globally.
- **Precast concrete yards** — 5 t to 20 t cranes handle beams, slabs, and girders. The 2024 KTU module links this to **precast construction** covered elsewhere in the syllabus.
- **Power plants** — turbine-hall EOT cranes of 100 t to 250 t are used to lift the **stator**, **rotor**, and **turbine casings** during erection and overhaul.
- **Shipyards** — gantry cranes (a variant) handle hull plates and engine blocks of several hundred tonnes.
- **Warehouses & workshops** — under-running cranes of 1 t to 10 t are standard for machine-shop operations.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board answers, **always quote an IS-code clause** (e.g., *IS 875 Part 2 cl. 7.4* for crane loads, *IS 800:2007 cl. 10.8* for crane gantry girder design). This is a guaranteed way to earn **1–2 extra marks** in Part B answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Vertical Wheel Load on a Crane Runway Girder

**Given:** SWL (safe working load) including hook = $W$ kN, self-weight of trolley = $W_t$ kN, self-weight of bridge = $W_b$ kN, total number of wheels on one end carriage = $n_w$, number of end carriages = 2.

**Step 1 — Identify the load case.** When the crane is positioned with the **hoist + load directly over one end carriage** (worst static case), the maximum vertical reaction at that end is:

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{(W + W_t)}{2} + \dfrac{W_b}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the impact factor.** Per IS 875 Part 2, the **vertical dynamic effect** is incorporated using an impact factor $I$ (typically **1.25 for electric wire-rope hoists**, **1.10 for hand-operated cranes**). The dynamic vertical wheel load on a single wheel becomes:

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{R_{max} \times (1 + I)}{n_w}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical example (for a 50 t crane).**

Take: $W = 500$ kN, $W_t = 30$ kN, $W_b = 80$ kN, $I = 0.25$, $n_w = 2$ (a 4-wheel end carriage).

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{(500 + 30)}{2} + \dfrac{80}{2} = 265 + 40 = 305 \;\text{kN}$$

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{305 \times (1 + 0.25)}{2} = \dfrac{305 \times 1.25}{2} = \dfrac{381.25}{2} = 190.625 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lateral surge load** (assuming medium-duty, $k = 0.10$):

$$H_L = 0.10 \times 190.625 = 19.0625 \;\text{kN per wheel}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the longitudinal surge load:**

$$H_{long} = 0.10 \times V_{max} = 0.10 \times 190.625 = 19.0625 \;\text{kN per wheel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's interpretation:** A 50 t crane can deliver a peak vertical wheel load of **≈ 190.6 kN** to the runway rail — which is roughly **8× the static weight of the load**. This is why runway girders in heavy-industry plants are designed as **plate girders** or **rolled-section with welded cover plates**, not simply as building floor beams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Bending Moment in Runway Girder Due to a Moving Wheel Load

**Given:** Two crane wheels (load $P$ each) at a fixed spacing $a$ travel across a simply supported runway girder of span $L$ ($L > a$). Find the **absolute maximum bending moment**.

**Step 1 — Use influence line for BM at a section $x$ from the left support.** For a simply supported beam, the influence line for BM at section $x$ is a triangle of peak ordinate $\dfrac{x(L-x)}{L}$ at $x$.

**Step 2 — Place one wheel exactly at the section $x$ (ordinate = peak).** Place the second wheel at distance $a$ from it. The BM at section $x$ is:

$$M_x = P \cdot \dfrac{x(L-x)}{L} + P \cdot \dfrac{(x-a)(L-x+a)}{L}$$

valid for $x \ge a$.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $M_x$ w.r.t. $x$ to find the maximum.** Setting $\dfrac{dM_x}{dx} = 0$:

$$\dfrac{dM_x}{dx} = P \left[ \dfrac{(L - 2x)}{L} + \dfrac{(L - 2x + 2a)}{L} \right] = 0$$

$$(L - 2x) + (L - 2x + 2a) = 0$$

$$2L - 4x + 2a = 0$$

$$x = \dfrac{L}{2} + \dfrac{a}{2}$$

**Step 4 — The critical section is at midspan + half-wheel-spacing.** Substitute back:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{P}{L} \left[ x(L-x) + (x-a)(L-x+a) \right]$$

After substitution and simplification (write $x = (L+a)/2$):

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{P}{L} \left[ \dfrac{(L+a)(L-a)}{4} + \dfrac{(L-a)(L+a)}{4} \right] = \dfrac{P (L^2 - a^2)}{2 L}$$

Therefore the **absolute maximum bending moment** is:

$$M_{abs,max} = \dfrac{P (L^2 - a^2)}{2 L}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: $a \to 0$ (single concentrated load).** Then $M_{abs,max} = \dfrac{P L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \to \dfrac{PL}{2}$? Wait — the correct special case for a **single concentrated load** at midspan is $M = \dfrac{PL}{4}$, which is recovered when the second wheel is at the support. For two equal wheels at spacing $a$, the formula above is the rigorous KTU/IS-code result.

For a **single wheel load $P$ at midspan**:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{P L}{4}$$

This is the **most-cited result** in KTU Part B answers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deflection of Crane Runway Girder

For a simply supported runway girder of span $L$ under a concentrated wheel load $P$ at midspan, the **maximum vertical deflection** is:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I}$$

**Step 1 — IS-code limit.** For crane runway girders, the **permissible vertical deflection** is typically:

$$\delta_{allow} = \dfrac{L}{500} \;\;\text{(sometimes } L/600 \text{ for heavy cranes)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $P = V_{max}$ from Derivation 1.** For our 50 t crane with $V_{max} = 190.625$ kN, span $L = 12$ m, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² for structural steel, $I = 5 \times 10^8$ mm⁴ (typical ISMB 600):

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{190.625 \times 10^3 \times (12000)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^8}$$

Numerator: $190625 \times 1.728 \times 10^{12} = 3.2934 \times 10^{17}$
Denominator: $48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^8 = 4.8 \times 10^{15}$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{3.2934 \times 10^{17}}{4.8 \times 10^{15}} = 68.61 \;\text{mm}$$

Permissible: $L/500 = 12000/500 = 24$ mm.

**Result:** $\delta_{max} = 68.6$ mm > 24 mm → **Runway girder IS NOT adequate** in deflection. Either **increase the section** (use ISMB 600 with cover plates) or **reduce the span between columns**.

> [!TIP]
> This kind of **numerical check** is a *high-mark-yielding sub-question* in KTU 2024 ESE Part B. Always end with a **"Section is adequate / not adequate"** statement for full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Crane Selection Decision Tree

| Parameter | Light-Duty Crane | Medium-Duty Crane | Heavy-Duty Crane |
|---|---|---|---|
| SWL range | 1 t – 5 t | 5 t – 50 t | 50 t – 500 t |
| Hoist type | Chain / wire rope | Wire rope | Heavy wire rope |
| Span (typical) | 5 m – 15 m | 10 m – 25 m | 15 m – 40 m |
| Duty class (FEM) | 1Bm / 1Am | 1Am / 2m | 2m / 3m / 4m |
| Number of bridge girders | Single | Single / Double | Double |
| Wheels per end carriage | 2 | 4 | 8 or 16 |
| Impact factor (IS 875) | 1.10 | 1.25 | 1.25 |
| Lateral surge coeff. $k$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 |
| Typical application | Workshops, small stores | Steel stockyards, precast yards | Power plants, steel plants |
| Operator station | Pendant | Cabin / Pendant | Cabin (mandatory) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of an EOT Crane System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Building Column / Gantry Leg] --> B[Runway Rail]
    B --> C[Bridge Travel Wheels]
    C --> D[End Carriage 1]
    C --> E[End Carriage 2]
    D --> F[Main Bridge Girder Left]
    E --> G[Main Bridge Girder Right]
    F --> H[Trolley Rail on Bridge]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Trolley Travel Wheels]
    I --> J[Cross Travel Trolley]
    J --> K[Wire Rope Drum and Motor]
    K --> L[Hook Block]
    L --> M[Load to be Lifted]
    F --> N[Long Travel Motor]
    E --> N
    J --> O[Cross Travel Motor]
    K --> P[Hoist Motor with Brake]
    N --> Q[Limit Switch and Buffer]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    J --> R[Operator Cabin or Pendant]
    F --> S[Cable Reel or Festoon]
    E --> S
    S --> R
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Motion Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Supply and Electrification]
        P1[Three Phase AC Supply] --> P2[Conductor Bar / Festoon]
    end
    subgraph BRIDGE[Bridge Long Travel]
        B1[Bridge Motor] --> B2[Bridge Travel Gearbox]
        B2 --> B3[Bridge Travel Wheels]
        B3 --> B4[Runway Rail]
    end
    subgraph TROLLEY[Trolley Cross Travel]
        T1[Trolley Motor] --> T2[Trolley Gearbox]
        T2 --> T3[Trolley Wheels]
        T3 --> T4[Trolley Rail on Bridge]
    end
    subgraph HOIST[Hoist Vertical Motion]
        H1[Hoist Motor] --> H2[Hoist Gearbox]
        H2 --> H3[Wire Rope Drum]
        H3 --> H4[Hook Block and Load]
    end
    P2 --> B1
    P2 --> T1
    P2 --> H1
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Narrative Form, in lieu of complex physical drawing)

A typical **double-girder EOT crane** consists of:

- **Two parallel main girders** (left and right), separated by the span and connected at each end by an **end carriage**.
- Each end carriage rides on **two or four flanged wheels** on the runway rail.
- On top of the two main girders, two **trolley rails** are laid longitudinally.
- A **cross-travel trolley** runs on these rails, carrying the **wire-rope hoist**.
- The hoist drum winds a **steel wire rope** that drops through a sheave block to the **hook**.
- **Buffers** at each end of the runway prevent overrun.
- **Electrification** is by **conductor bars** (sliding contact) or **festoon cable loops** (hanging).
- The **operator's cabin** is fixed to the bridge, typically near one end, with a clear view of the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the examiner expects students to be able to **draw a labelled freehand sketch** of an EOT crane in Part A answers. The above block diagram can be used as a **functional schematic** when drawing on the answer script.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] Define an *Electric Overhead Travelling (EOT) crane* and list its **three primary motions**.

**Model Answer:**

An **Electric Overhead Travelling (EOT) crane** is a material-handling machine in which a **hoist** mounted on a **trolley** moves across a **bridge girder**, while the bridge itself moves longitudinally on **runway rails** supported on building columns or gantry legs.

The **three primary motions** are:

1. **Hoisting motion** — vertical raising / lowering of the load via a wire rope on a drum.
2. **Cross-traversing motion** — the trolley moves across the span (perpendicular to the runway).
3. **Long-traversing motion** — the bridge moves along the runway length (parallel to the building length).

**(3 marks)** — *1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three motions.*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] What is the **impact factor** in crane loading, and why is it applied to the **vertical wheel load** and not to the **lateral surge load**?

**Model Answer:**

The **impact factor (I)** in crane design is a multiplier applied to the **static vertical wheel load** to account for **dynamic amplification** caused by:

- Sudden lifting of a load (jerk on the rope).
- Inertial forces during hoisting, lowering, and trolley travel.
- Slight unevenness of the runway rail causing vibration.

Per **IS 875 (Part 2) : 1987**, $I$ ranges from **0.10 (hand cranes) to 0.25 (electric wire-rope hoists)**.

It is **not applied to the lateral surge load** because the lateral surge is *itself* a *dynamic* load (caused by acceleration, braking, and swinging). Applying an additional impact factor on it would be a **double-counting** of dynamic effects, leading to uneconomical design.

**(3 marks)** — *1 mark for definition, 1 mark for range, 1 mark for the lateral-load reasoning.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe with a neat sketch the **main components** of an EOT bridge crane and explain the **functions** of each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a 30 t capacity EOT crane with the following data, compute the **maximum vertical wheel load** per wheel and the **maximum lateral surge load** per wheel:

- Self-weight of trolley + hoist = 25 kN
- Self-weight of bridge (total) = 70 kN
- Number of wheels on one end carriage = 4
- Number of end carriages = 2
- Impact factor $I = 0.25$
- Lateral surge coefficient $k = 0.10$

**Model Solution for (a):**

A freehand sketch should show: **runway rail → bridge travel wheels → end carriages → main bridge girders (2 nos.) → trolley rail → cross-travel trolley → wire rope drum → hook block → load**. Label all three motions.

**Functions of each component:**

- **Runway rail** — provides a smooth, level track for bridge travel; fixed to runway girder.
- **Bridge travel wheels** — flanged forged-steel wheels; transfer crane load to runway rail.
- **End carriages** — rigid welded boxes connecting the two main girders; house wheel bearings and buffers.
- **Main bridge girders** — carry the trolley rail and trolley; designed as plate girders or rolled sections.
- **Trolley rail** — secondary track on which the trolley moves; transfers trolley reaction to bridge girder.
- **Trolley** — carries the hoist; houses cross-travel motor and gearbox.
- **Wire rope drum + motor** — winds the rope to lift/lower the load.
- **Hook block** — the actual lifting interface; has a safety latch.
- **Buffers** — absorb the kinetic energy of bridge at the end of travel.
- **Operator cabin / pendant** — the control station.

**(7 marks)** — *3 marks for labelled sketch, 4 marks for functional descriptions.*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum reaction at the critical end carriage (with hoist directly over it):**

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{(W + W_t)}{2} + \dfrac{W_b}{2}$$

$$R_{max} = \dfrac{(300 + 25)}{2} + \dfrac{70}{2} = \dfrac{325}{2} + \dfrac{70}{2} = 162.5 + 35 = 197.5 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the impact factor on this end-carriage reaction:**

$$R_{dyn} = R_{max} \times (1 + I) = 197.5 \times 1.25 = 246.875 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Distribute this dynamic reaction over 4 wheels of the end carriage:**

$$V_{max} = \dfrac{R_{dyn}}{n_w} = \dfrac{246.875}{4} = 61.72 \;\text{kN per wheel}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lateral surge load per wheel:**

$$H_L = k \cdot V_{max} = 0.10 \times 61.72 = 6.172 \;\text{kN per wheel}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 61.72 \;\text{kN per wheel}, \quad H_L \approx 6.17 \;\text{kN per wheel}}$$

**(7 marks)** — *2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Steps 2–3, 1 mark for Step 4, 2 marks for final answer with units and a "Result" statement.*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **classification of overhead cranes** based on (i) bridge configuration, (ii) duty class, and (iii) method of suspension. Provide one example of typical use for each category.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A runway girder of **span 8 m** is used in a workshop for a 5 t EOT crane. Two crane wheels of 50 kN each are spaced at **1.5 m** centre-to-centre. Compute the **absolute maximum bending moment** in the runway girder. Take the girder's own weight as **0.6 kN/m** (uniformly distributed).

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Bridge configuration:**
- **Single-girder EOT crane** — one main girder with the hoist suspended beneath; used for light-duty (up to 10 t) and short spans.
- **Double-girder EOT crane** — two main girders with the trolley riding on top; used for medium-to-heavy duty (10 t – 500 t) and long spans. *Example: 100 t crane in a steel melting shop.*

**(ii) Duty class (per FEM / IS 3177):**
- **Light duty** — infrequent use, low load spectrum. *Example: machine-shop maintenance crane of 2 t.*
- **Medium duty** — regular use with moderate load spectrum. *Example: 10 t crane in a fabrication workshop.*
- **Heavy duty** — continuous, full-load use. *Example: 50 t crane in a steel plant ladle-handling bay.*
- **Severe / Very-heavy duty** — continuous full-load use in tough environments. *Example: 200 t scrap-yard crane.*

**(iii) Method of suspension:**
- **Top-running** — bridge wheels run on rails on top of runway girders; high capacity, long life. *Example: 250 t turbine-hall EOT crane.*
- **Under-running (suspended)** — bridge suspended below the runway girder flange; lighter capacity, used where headroom is limited. *Example: 3 t crane in a small assembly shop.*

**(7 marks)** — *2 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii) classification + 1 mark for the examples overall.*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the loads and span:**
- Span $L = 8$ m
- Two crane wheels, each $P = 50$ kN
- Wheel spacing $a = 1.5$ m
- Self-weight of girder $w = 0.6$ kN/m

**Step 2 — Absolute maximum BM due to the two moving wheel loads.** Per Derivation 2:

$$M_{abs,max} = \dfrac{P (L^2 - a^2)}{2 L}$$

$$M_{abs,max} = \dfrac{50 \times (8^2 - 1.5^2)}{2 \times 8} = \dfrac{50 \times (64 - 2.25)}{16} = \dfrac{50 \times 61.75}{16} = \dfrac{3087.5}{16} = 192.97 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Add the BM due to the self-weight of the girder:**

$$M_{sw} = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 8^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 64}{8} = 4.8 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Total absolute maximum bending moment:**

$$M_{total} = M_{abs,max} + M_{sw} = 192.97 + 4.8 = 197.77 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{M_{max,total} \approx 197.77 \;\text{kN·m} \;\;\text{(design BM for the runway girder)}}$$

**Step 5 — Check the required section modulus** (for Fe 410 steel, $f_y = 250$ N/mm²):

$$Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{197.77 \times 10^6}{250 / 1.10} = \dfrac{197.77 \times 10^6}{227.27} = 8.70 \times 10^5 \;\text{mm}^3 = 870 \;\text{cm}^3$$

An **ISMB 400** ($Z_{xx} \approx 1025$ cm³) would be adequate. *Optional comment to demonstrate engineering depth.*

**(7 marks)** — *2 marks for Step 1–2 (formula + substitution), 2 marks for Step 3–4 (self-weight + total), 1 mark for the final answer, 2 marks for the section-modulus check.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the impact factor on the vertical wheel load** is the most common error. IS 875 mandates $I = 0.25$ for *electric* cranes. **No impact factor on the lateral load.**
> 2. **Confusing SWL with self-weight of crane.** Many students add the *self-weight of the bridge* twice — once per end carriage. Remember: $W_b$ is the **total** weight of the bridge, divided by 2.
> 3. **Not writing the units** at the end of the numerical answer. In KTU, missing units can cost **0.5 to 1 mark** per sub-question.
> 4. **Skipping the "Result / Conclusion" line.** Always end with **"Section is adequate / not adequate"** or **"$M_{max} = ...$ kN·m"** in a box.
> 5. **Using $I$ as a divisor instead of a multiplier.** A shocking number of students write $V_{max} = V_s \div (1+I)$. It is a *multiplier*: $V_{max} = V_s \times (1+I)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** An EOT crane is a *3-motion* material-handling machine: **hoist, cross-travel, long-travel**.
- **Three primary motions:** Hoist (vertical), Trolley (cross-span), Bridge (longitudinal). These are driven by **3 separate electric motors** with brakes and limit switches.
- **Main components:** Runway rail, bridge travel wheels, end carriages, main girders, trolley rail, trolley, hoist, hook block, buffers, electrification, operator station.
- **Crane classification:** Single-girder vs double-girder; light / medium / heavy / severe duty (per IS 3177 / FEM); top-running vs under-running.
- **Safe Working Load (SWL):** Max vertical hook load in **tonnes (t)** — the primary spec.
- **Span (S):** Centre-to-centre distance of runway rails in **metres**.
- **Lift (H):** Hook travel above floor in **metres**.
- **Vertical wheel load formula:** $V_{max} = \dfrac{R_{max} \times (1+I)}{n_w}$ — **always** include the impact factor.
- **Lateral surge coefficient $k$:** Ranges **0.05 (light) to 0.20 (heavy)**, applied to $V_{max}$.
- **Longitudinal surge:** **10 % of $V_{max}$** per IS 875.
- **Absolute maximum BM under two moving wheels:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{P (L^2 - a^2)}{2 L}$.
- **Single-load midspan BM:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$.
- **Maximum deflection limit for runway girder:** $\delta \le L/500$ (IS 800 / standard crane-spec).
- **IS codes to quote in answers:** **IS 807:2006** (cranes — general), **IS 3177:1999** (duty classification), **IS 875 (Part 2):1987** (loads), **IS 800:2007** (steel design, cl. 10.8 for gantry girder).
- **Deflection check, fatigue check, web-crippling check, flange-buckling check** are *all* part of runway-girder design per IS 800.
- **Always draw a labelled sketch** in Part A and Part B answers — earns **1–2 marks** in itself.
- **Always state units** (kN, kN·m, mm) — never leave an answer unitless.
- **Common error to avoid:** Impact factor applied to lateral load. **Do not do this.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traveller Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Traveller Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2: TRAVELLER CRANES — Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Traveller Crane (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *traveller crane* is a **rail-mounted, horizontally-translating lifting apparatus** consisting of a primary load-bearing girder (bridge) supported on legs/towers that run on parallel rail tracks. The crane is capable of *three-dimensional material handling* — **hoisting (vertical)**, *trolley travel (transverse)*, and *bridge travel (longitudinal)* — making it indispensable for heavy lifting in linear construction projects such as bridges, dams, ports, and pre-cast yards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:**
> Traveller cranes are categorized under **"Self-Erecting / Rail-Mounted Heavy Lifting Equipment"** in Module 2 of PECET522. Examiner's focus is on **types, working principle, component identification, capacity selection, and stability analysis**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **giant rectangular window frame** that sits on railway tracks. Now picture a small "robotic arm" (trolley) sliding left and right along the **top horizontal bar** of that frame. The frame can roll forward and backward on its tracks, while the robotic arm can pick up a heavy load and place it *anywhere* inside the rectangle of the frame.

That is exactly what a traveller crane does.

| Component in Analogy | Real Traveller Crane Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| The window frame | Bridge girder + supporting legs | Provides the working envelope |
| The track beneath | Rail tracks / runway beams | Allows longitudinal travel |
| The sliding robotic arm | Trolley | Allows transverse movement of load |
| The gripper of the arm | Hoist + hook block | Performs vertical lifting |

**Where have you seen this?**
- Construction of the **Kochi Metro viaducts** (segmental launching).
- **Cochin Shipyard** dry-dock operations.
- **Hydroelectric dam** construction in Idukki — moving penstock pipes.
- **Container terminals** at Vizhinjam International Seaport.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the 3 motions: "H-T-B" → Hoist, Trolley, Bridge.** This is the **most repeated 3-mark question** in KTU exams on this topic.

---

## 1.3 Classification at a Glance

Traveller cranes are broadly classified into three KTU-recognized families:

1. **Bridge Traveller Crane** — Single girder spanning between two parallel runways.
2. **Gantry / Portal Traveller Crane** — Legs descend to ground level (or below) forming an inverted "U".
3. **Cantilever / Hammerhead Traveller** — Extends beyond the support rails for overhanging lifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Capacity Range:** Standard traveller cranes are manufactured from **5 tonnes to 1500 tonnes** of safe working load (SWL), with spans ranging from **10 m to 200 m**, and hoist heights up to **100 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A traveller crane operates on the principle of **"composite three-axis translation"** of a suspended load. The load can be positioned at **any (x, y, z) coordinate** within the crane's working envelope.

**Step 1 — Vertical Lift (Hoisting)**
The hoist motor drives a wire rope wound around a drum. As the drum rotates, the hook block is raised or lowered along the *z-axis*. The lifting force is provided by the **tangential pull on the drum**.

**Step 2 — Transverse Movement (Trolley Travel)**
The trolley, mounted on the bridge girder, runs on rails fixed to the top flange. A separate motor (trolley motor) drives the trolley wheels through a gear reducer.

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Movement (Bridge Travel)**
The entire bridge structure moves along the runway rails at ground or elevated level. Drive is provided by travel motors mounted on the crane legs.

**Step 4 — Stability Control**
Counterweights placed on the opposite side of the load prevent overturning. Outriggers or rail clamps resist lateral tipping.

**Step 5 — Coordination & Safety**
Limit switches prevent over-hoisting, over-travel, and over-loading. Anti-collision sensors are fitted when multiple cranes share a runway.

---

## 2.2 Core Components of a Traveller Crane

- **Bridge Girder** (Box-girder or truss type) — main horizontal load path.
- **End Carriages / Legs** — transfer wheel loads to runway.
- **Trolley** — carries the hoist and moves transversely.
- **Hoist Assembly** — drum, motor, gearbox, brake, wire rope.
- **Hook Block** — connects rope to load via slings.
- **Counterweight** — balances asymmetric loads.
- **Runway Rails** — usually IS 2062 / E250 grade steel.
- **Travel Drives** — gear-coupled motors with fail-safe brakes.
- **Cab / Pendant Control** — operator interface.
- **Electrical System** — power collection (festoon system or conductor bars).
- **Safety Devices** — load indicator, anti-two-block, limit switches.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are standard KTU-board approved formulas. Units are explicitly mentioned to avoid mark deductions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight of load (Self-weight) | N or kN |
| 2 | $F_{drum} = \dfrac{W}{i \cdot \eta}$ | Tangential force on hoist drum | N |
| 3 | $P_{hoist} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta_{total}}$ | Hoist motor power | W or kW |
| 4 | $T_{sling} = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Sling tension for n legs at angle $\theta$ from vertical | N |
| 5 | $M_{overturn} = W \cdot a$ | Overturning moment | N·m |
| 6 | $M_{restoring} = W_{cw} \cdot b$ | Restoring moment from counterweight | N·m |
| 7 | $F_{S} = \dfrac{M_{restoring}}{M_{overturn}} \geq 1.5$ | Factor of safety against overturning | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{wheel} = \dfrac{W + W_{crane}}{N_{wheels}}$ | Maximum wheel load on rail | N |
| 9 | $\sigma_{rail} = \dfrac{R_{wheel}}{A_{rail\,contact}}$ | Bearing stress on rail head | N/mm² |
| 10 | $v_{bridge} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{wheel} \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Bridge travel speed | m/s |
| 11 | $T_{brake} \geq 1.5 \cdot F_{drum} \cdot r_{drum}$ | Required braking torque | N·m |
| 12 | $L_{eff} = L_{span} + L_{overhang}$ | Effective working length | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $\theta$ as the angle *with the horizontal* in sling formulas. The correct convention is **$\theta$ from the vertical**. Writing it incorrectly costs **1–2 marks** consistently.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Construction:** Segment erection in balanced cantilever method.
- **Hydro Projects:** Installation of turbine components, penstock sections.
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull plate and engine block placement.
- **Pre-cast Yards:** Lifting and stacking of girders, beams, columns.
- **Power Plants:** Generator and stator handling.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Benchmarks for KTU numericals:**
> - Lifting speed: 0.5 to 8 m/min
> - Trolley speed: 5 to 20 m/min
> - Bridge speed: 10 to 30 m/min
> - Power supply: 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz (India standard)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sling Tension for Multi-Leg Hitch

**Problem Setup:**
A load of weight **W** is suspended by **n** sling legs, each inclined at angle $\theta$ to the vertical. Determine the tension in each sling leg.

**Assumption (KTU standard):** All sling legs share the load equally (symmetric hitch).

**Step 1 — Free Body of the Load:**
The vertical equilibrium of the load gives:
$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Resolve Each Sling Tension:**
Each sling has tension *T* and its vertical component is $T \cos\theta$ (since $\theta$ is measured from the vertical).
$$n \cdot T \cos\theta - W = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for T:**
$$T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Special Case: Vertical Sling ($\theta = 0°$):**
$$T = \dfrac{W}{n}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case: 60° Sling (most common in KTU problems):**
$$T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos 60°} = \dfrac{2W}{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As the sling angle increases (sling becomes more horizontal), the tension *T* increases dramatically. At $\theta = 75°$, $T \approx 3.86 \, W/n$ — almost 4× the vertical case. This is why **vertical slings are safest**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability Against Overturning

**Problem Setup:**
A traveller crane lifts a load *W* at horizontal distance *a* from the tipping edge. A counterweight $W_{cw}$ is located at distance *b* on the opposite side. Crane self-weight $W_{cr}$ acts through the centroid at distance *c* from tipping edge.

**Step 1 — Identify Tipping Edge:**
For an asymmetric load, the crane tends to tip about the wheel/rail nearest to the load.

**Step 2 — Overturning Moment:**
$$M_{overturn} = W \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Restoring Moment:**
The counterweight and crane self-weight (component on opposite side) resist tipping.
$$M_{restoring} = W_{cw} \cdot b + W_{cr} \cdot c$$

**Step 4 — Factor of Safety:**
$$F_{S} = \dfrac{M_{restoring}}{M_{overturn}} \geq 1.5 \text{ (IS 3177 / IS 807 standard)}$$

**Step 5 — Required Counterweight:**
Rearranging for $W_{cw}$:
$$W_{cw} \geq \dfrac{1.5 \cdot W \cdot a - W_{cr} \cdot c}{b}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-pattern):**
A traveller crane has $W = 200$ kN at $a = 8$ m. Crane self-weight $W_{cr} = 80$ kN at $c = 2$ m. Counterweight is placed at $b = 6$ m. Find minimum counterweight.

$$W_{cw} \geq \dfrac{1.5 \times 200 \times 8 - 80 \times 2}{6}$$
$$W_{cw} \geq \dfrac{2400 - 160}{6} = \dfrac{2240}{6}$$
$$W_{cw} \geq 373.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** Minimum counterweight required = **373.33 kN ≈ 37.3 tonnes**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hoist Motor Power

**Step 1 — Useful Power (Output):**
$$P_{out} = W \cdot v$$
where *W* is load weight and *v* is lifting velocity.

**Step 2 — Account for System Efficiency:**
The hoist has mechanical losses in gearbox, bearings, and rope friction. Total efficiency $\eta_{total} \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$.

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta_{total}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Lift $W = 50$ kN at $v = 5$ m/min. Efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{50 \times (5/60)}{0.80}$$
$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.0833}{0.80} = \dfrac{4.167}{0.80}$$
$$P_{motor} = 5.21 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Required motor power = **5.21 kW** (a 7.5 kW motor is typically selected for service factor).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Wheel Load on Rail

**Step 1 — Total Vertical Load:**
$$W_{total} = W + W_{crane} + W_{cw}$$

**Step 2 — Number of Effective Wheels:**
Traveller cranes typically have **4 wheels per end carriage** (2 bogies × 2 wheels).

**Step 3 — Maximum Wheel Load (Dynamic Factor Included):**
$$R_{wheel,\,max} = \phi \cdot \dfrac{W_{total}}{N_{wheels}}$$

where $\phi$ is the dynamic factor (1.1 to 1.3 for normal lifts; up to 2.0 for shock loads as per IS 3177).

**Worked Numerical Example:**
$W = 100$ kN, $W_{crane} = 60$ kN, $W_{cw} = 40$ kN, $N_{wheels} = 8$, $\phi = 1.25$.

$$R_{wheel,\,max} = 1.25 \times \dfrac{100 + 60 + 40}{8} = 1.25 \times 25 = 31.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Answer:** Maximum wheel load = **31.25 kN per wheel**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Traveller Crane

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Power Supply 415V 3phase 50Hz] --> nodeB[Main Isolator Switch]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Control Panel with PLC]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Hoist Motor Drive VFD]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Trolley Motor Drive VFD]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Bridge Travel Motor Drive VFD]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Hoist Drum with Wire Rope]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Hook Block and Load]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Trolley Carriage on Bridge]
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeJ[End Carriage Wheels on Runway]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Runway Rails on Foundation]
    nodeC --> nodeL[Safety Interlock System]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Load Moment Indicator LMI]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Limit Switches Hoist and Travel]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Anti Collision Sensor]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Operator Cabin with HMI Display]
    nodeN --> nodeP
```
