- **Aesthetics** demand an arched silhouette (footbridges, urban overpasses).
- **Construction over existing roads** is required (tied arch can be assembled as a complete span beside the road and rolled in).

## 2.2 Cable Stayed Bridge — Structural Behavior

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Vertical loads on the deck** are collected at each cable anchorage point.
2. **Each stay cable** carries a tensile force resolved into:
   - A **vertical component** = supporting the deck load
   - A **horizontal component** = compressing the deck axially
3. **The tower (pylon)** receives cable forces from both sides (in the main span and side span).
4. The tower behaves as a **vertical cantilever** in compression with bending due to unbalanced cable forces.
5. The **deck** acts as a **continuous beam** elastically supported at each cable point, plus receives axial compression (precompression) that helps resist bending.
6. **Side spans** (back-stay cables) anchor the system by pulling the tower top back, balancing main span cable forces.

### Cable Arrangement Patterns

- **Harp Pattern:** All cables parallel, attached at equal heights on the tower. Aesthetically elegant, less efficient structurally.
- **Fan Pattern:** All cables converge to a single point at the top of the tower. Structurally efficient but construction at the tower top is congested.
- **Semi-Fan (Modified Fan):** Cables spread over a finite region at the tower top. Compromise — **most commonly used in practice**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Deck Axial Compression:** A huge advantage of cable-stayed bridges is that the deck is placed in **precompression** by the horizontal component of cable forces. This counteracts tensile stresses from live load bending, allowing slender, lightweight decks.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Long spans** without the massive anchorages of suspension bridges.
- **Faster construction** than suspension bridges (no massive cable-spinning operation).
- **Excellent for crossing wide rivers, harbors, and sea straits**.
- **Aesthetic landmarks** for modern cities (Millau Viaduct, Russky Bridge, Bandra-Worli Sea Link).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Bow String Bridge Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8f}$ | Horizontal thrust in the arch (parabolic arch, UDL $w$) |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | Vertical reaction at each support (simply supported condition) |
| 3 | $T = H$ | Tension in the tie girder (equals horizontal thrust) |
| 4 | $M_{tie} = M_{beam} - H \cdot y$ | Bending moment in tie (Beam analogy minus thrust × rise) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{tie} = \dfrac{T}{A_t} + \dfrac{M_{tie} \cdot c}{I_t}$ | Combined stress in tie (axial + bending) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beam Analogy for Tied Arches:** The bending moment in the tie is the same as that of a simply supported beam of span $L$ under the same loading, **minus** the moment caused by horizontal thrust acting through the rise $f$ at midspan. This is the cornerstone of KTU 14-mark questions.

### Cable Stayed Bridge Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_i = \dfrac{w \cdot a_i}{\sin \theta_i}$ | Cable tension (vertical load $w \cdot a_i$ / sin of cable angle) |
| 2 | $\theta_i = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{x_i}\right)$ | Cable inclination angle (tower height $h$, horizontal distance $x_i$) |
| 3 | $\Delta \sigma_{equiv} = \dfrac{\sigma}{1 + \dfrac{(wL)^2 \cdot A_e}{12 T^3}}$ | Ernst's equivalent modulus for cable sag |
| 4 | $F_{tower} = \sum V_i$ | Tower vertical force = sum of vertical components of cables |
| 5 | $M_{tower} = \sum (H_i \cdot h_i) - \sum (V_i \cdot x_i)$ | Tower base moment |
| 6 | $\Delta L_{cable} = \dfrac{T \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot E_{equiv}}$ | Cable elongation using Ernst's modulus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ernst's Formula Origin:** A cable under its own weight sags into a catenary. The sag reduces the effective axial stiffness. Heinrich Ernst (1965) derived the equivalent straight-bar modulus so engineers can model the cable as a truss element in computer analysis. This is a **guaranteed 2-3 mark question** in KTU papers.

### Standard Design Constants to Remember

| Constant | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| $f_{ck}$ for C50 concrete | **50 MPa** | Deck in cable-stayed bridges |
| $f_y$ for high-tensile wire | **1570–1860 MPa** | Stay cables |
| Dead load (composite deck) | **~150 kN/m** | Initial design estimate |
| Cable spacing (typical) | **8–15 m** | Along deck |
| Tower height ratio | **h ≈ 0.20 L to 0.25 L** | For main span $L$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Thrust in a Parabolic Bow String Arch

**Given:** A parabolic two-hinged arch, span $L$, rise $f$, carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ over the entire span. The arch is "tied" — i.e., a tension member connects the two springings at deck level.

**Find:** Horizontal thrust $H$ and tie tension $T$.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Parabolic Arch

The centerline of a parabolic arch follows the equation:

$$ y = \dfrac{4f}{L^2} \cdot x(L - x) $$

**Explanation:** At $x = 0$ and $x = L$, $y = 0$ (springings). At $x = L/2$, $y = f$ (crown). This is the standard second-degree parabola passing through the supports with the given rise.

### Step 2 — Statical Equivalence (Vertical Reactions)

For a simply supported parabolic arch under UDL, vertical reactions are:

$$ V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} $$

**Explanation:** This is identical to a simply supported beam — the arch's vertical statics are the same as a beam, since there is no horizontal applied load and the supports are pinned (or hinged).

### Step 3 — Bending Moment at Crown Using Superposition

The bending moment at any section of a two-hinged arch is the **simple beam moment** minus the **moment of the horizontal thrust about that section**:

$$ M_x = M_{beam,x} - H \cdot y_x $$

**Explanation:** The thrust $H$ acts at the support level (springing). Its moment arm to a point at height $y_x$ on the arch is exactly $y_x$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Crown Condition

For a parabolic arch loaded with a UDL over the **full span**, the bending moment at **every point is zero** (the arch is the funicular shape for UDL). At the crown ($x = L/2$, $y = f$):

$$ M_{crown} = M_{beam,crown} - H \cdot f = 0 $$

### Step 5 — Solve for H

The simple beam moment at midspan:

$$ M_{beam,crown} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} $$

Substituting into the crown condition:

$$ \dfrac{wL^2}{8} - H \cdot f = 0 $$

Solving for $H$:

$$ H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8f} $$

### Step 6 — Tie Force

Horizontal equilibrium of the entire bridge (cutting through the tie at midspan) requires the tension in the tie to balance the horizontal thrust:

$$ T = H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8f} $$

### Step 7 — End Post Force

The end post is the portion of the arch between the springing and the deck-end. The axial force in the end post equals the resultant of horizontal thrust $H$ and vertical shear $V$:

$$ F_{end\,post} = \sqrt{H^2 + V^2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{wL^2}{8f}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{wL}{2}\right)^2} $$

The angle of inclination $\alpha$ of the end post is:

$$ \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{V}{H}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4f}{L}\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** If $L = 100$ m, $f = 15$ m, $w = 50$ kN/m, then $H = 50 \times 100^2 / (8 \times 15) = 4166.67$ kN, and $T = 4166.67$ kN. End-post force = $\sqrt{4166.67^2 + 2500^2} = 4858$ kN. This is the kind of calculation KTU asks for **7-mark sub-parts**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cable Tension in a Cable-Stayed Bridge (Harp System)

**Given:** A symmetrical cable-stayed bridge with **2 cables per side, harp pattern**, tower height $h$, main span $L$. Cables attach to deck at $x_1$ and $x_2$ from the tower base.

**Find:** Cable tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ under a uniform deck load $w$.

### Step 1 — Cable Geometry

In a harp system, all cables are parallel. The angle $\theta$ of each cable with the horizontal is constant:

$$ \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{a}\right) $$

where $a$ is the horizontal distance from tower to the cable's deck anchorage.

### Step 2 — Force Decomposition

Consider the **i-th cable** attached at deck position $x_i$. It supports a tributary vertical load $w \cdot d_i$ (where $d_i$ is the cable spacing along the deck). The vertical equilibrium of the deck node:

$$ T_i \cdot \sin\theta = w \cdot d_i $$

Therefore:

$$ T_i = \dfrac{w \cdot d_i}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{w \cdot d_i \cdot \sqrt{h^2 + a_i^2}}{h} $$

### Step 3 — Horizontal Component (Deck Pre-Compression)

The horizontal component compresses the deck:

$$ H_i = T_i \cdot \cos\theta = \dfrac{w \cdot d_i \cdot a_i}{h} $$

**Explanation:** The horizontal force is constant if cable spacing is uniform and the angle is constant — wait, more carefully: the horizontal component of each cable equals the vertical load divided by the **slope** of the cable. The cumulative horizontal compression at the tower equals:

$$ H_{cumulative} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{w \cdot d_i \cdot a_i}{h} $$

### Step 4 — Vertical Force in Tower

The tower must support the sum of all vertical components of main-span and side-span cables:

$$ F_{tower} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,main} \cdot \sin\theta_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} T_{j,side} \cdot \sin\theta_j $$

For a symmetrical bridge with equal main and side spans, the vertical components of the back-stay cables (side span) balance those of the main span, leaving only the difference for the tower's vertical reaction at the base.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ernst's Equivalent Modulus

**Problem:** A cable of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, modulus of elasticity $E$, and weight per unit length $w_c$, under tension $T$, sags. This sag reduces the cable's effective stiffness.

**Step 1 — Sag of a Cable**

The classical catenary sag of a uniformly loaded cable under tension $T$ is:

$$ s = \dfrac{w_c L^2}{8T} $$

**Step 2 — Tangent Modulus Idea**

When a cable elongates due to an additional load, part of the elongation is "lost" to the reduction in sag. The tangent modulus of elasticity (or "equivalent modulus") accounts for this.

**Step 3 — Ernst's Derivation (1965)**

Heinrich Ernst showed that the equivalent modulus $E_{eq}$ is:

$$ E_{eq} = \dfrac{E}{1 + \dfrac{(w_c L)^2 \cdot A}{12 T^3}} $$

or equivalently:

$$ E_{eq} = \dfrac{E}{1 + \dfrac{E \cdot A \cdot w_c^2 \cdot L^2 \cdot (1 + \mu)^4}{24 T^3}} $$

where $\mu$ is a stress-ratio factor. The simpler form suffices for KTU-level problems:

$$ E_{eq} = \dfrac{E}{1 + \dfrac{\gamma^2 L^2 A E}{12 \sigma^3}} $$

where $\sigma$ is the cable stress and $\gamma$ is the volumetric weight of the cable material.

**Step 4 — Effective Stiffness**

The cable's effective axial stiffness then becomes:

$$ K_{eff} = \dfrac{A \cdot E_{eq}}{L_{eff}} $$

This is what is used in **stiffness matrix formulations** for the global structural analysis of cable-stayed bridges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Cue:** A 14-mark question often tests the student's ability to:
> 1. State Ernst's formula.
> 2. Explain the physical meaning of cable sag.
> 3. Compute the equivalent stiffness given $L$, $T$, $A$, $E$, and $w_c$.
> 4. Justify why this correction is necessary in cable-stayed bridge analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bow String Bridge — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LoadA[Vertical Live Load w] --> Deck1[Deck Slab]
    Deck1 --> HG1[Hangers / Vertical Members]
    HG1 --> ArchA[Parabolic Arch Rib]
    ArchA --> EndPost1[End Post LHS]
    ArchA --> EndPost2[End Post RHS]
    EndPost1 --> Support1[Abutment - Vertical Reaction Only]
    EndPost2 --> Support2[Abutment - Vertical Reaction Only]
    ArchA -. Horizontal Thrust H .-> TieForce
    TieForce[Tie Girder - Pure Tension T = H]
    TieForce -. Balanced Internally .-> EndPost1
    TieForce -. Balanced Internally .-> EndPost2
    Deck1 --> TieForce
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dotted lines indicate **internal force balance** — the horizontal thrust of the arch is canceled by the tension in the tie. The abutments receive **only vertical reactions**, which is why bow string bridges work on **poor soils**.

## 4.2 Bow String Bridge — Cross-Section and Component Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Elevation
        A1[Springing A] -->|Arch Rib| C1[Crown]
        C1 -->|Arch Rib| B1[Springing B]
        A1 --> T1[Tie Girder at Deck Level]
        B1 --> T1
        T1 -->|Deck Slab| D1[Roadway]
        H1[Hangers] -.-> A1
        H1 -.-> C1
        H1 -.-> B1
    end
```

## 4.3 Cable Stayed Bridge — Load Path Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DeckLoad[Deck Vertical Load] --> AnchorA[Deck Anchorage 1]
    DeckLoad --> AnchorB[Deck Anchorage 2]
    DeckLoad --> AnchorC[Deck Anchorage 3]
    AnchorA --> Cable1[Stay Cable 1 - Tension T1]
    AnchorB --> Cable2[Stay Cable 2 - Tension T2]
    AnchorC --> Cable3[Stay Cable 3 - Tension T3]
    Cable1 --> TowerTop[Tower Top - Combined Anchor]
    Cable2 --> TowerTop
    Cable3 --> TowerTop
    BackStay1[Back-Stay Cable Side Span] --> TowerTop
    BackStay2[Back-Stay Cable Side Span] --> TowerTop
    TowerTop --> TowerShaft[Tower Shaft - Compression + Bending]
    TowerShaft --> Foundation[Bridge Foundation]
    Cable1 -. H1 Horizontal .-> DeckPrecompression[Deck Axial Precompression]
    Cable2 -. H2 Horizontal .-> DeckPrecompression
    Cable3 -. H3 Horizontal .-> DeckPrecompression
```

## 4.4 Cable Stayed Bridge — Cable Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Harp
        HT1[Tower] --- HA1[Cable 1]
        HT1 --- HA2[Cable 2]
        HT1 --- HA3[Cable 3]
        HA1 --- HD1[Deck]
        HA2 --- HD1
        HA3 --- HD1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Fan
        FT1[Tower] --> FT2[Top Anchor Point]
        FT2 --> FA1[Cable 1]
        FT2 --> FA2[Cable 2]
        FT2 --> FA3[Cable 3]
        FA1 --- FD1[Deck]
        FA2 --- FD1
        FA3 --- FD1
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SemiFan
        ST1[Tower Top] --> SA1[Cable 1]
        ST1 --> SA2[Cable 2]
        ST1 --> SA3[Cable 3]
        SA1 --- SD1[Deck]
        SA2 --- SD1
        SA3 --- SD1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how the **Fan pattern** concentrates all cables at one tower-top point, while the **Semi-Fan** spreads them over a small region — the most common practical compromise, reducing congestion at the tower top while retaining good structural efficiency.

## 4.5 Tower Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TowerTypes
        A1[H-Frame: 2 Vertical Legs]
        A2[Inverted Y: Single Column Below, V-Shaped Above]
        A3[A-Frame: Inclined Legs Meeting at Top]
        A4[Diamond: 4-Legged Space Frame]
        A5[Single Pylon: Asymmetric Bridge]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The choice of tower geometry depends on the number of cable planes (single plane vs. double plane along the deck) and aesthetic requirements. H-frame is most common for twin cable planes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Bow String Bridge and a Cable Stayed Bridge in terms of load transfer mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Bow String Bridge | Cable Stayed Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary load transfer** | Arch in compression + tie in tension; hangers as secondary members | Cables in direct tension from tower to deck; tower in compression |
| **Horizontal force handling** | Internally balanced by tie girder (self-anchored) | Internally balanced by opposing cable forces on the tower |
| **Abutment requirement** | Only vertical reactions transmitted | Side-span back-stays transmit pull to anchorages |
| **Span range** | 50–250 m (efficient) | 200–1100 m+ (efficient) |

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for the load path in bow string, 1 mark for cable stayed, 1 mark for span/abutment comparison.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is Ernst's equivalent modulus? Why is it used in cable-stayed bridge analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Ernst's equivalent modulus ($E_{eq}$) is a correction to the elastic modulus of a stay cable to account for the **reduction in stiffness caused by the cable's self-weight sag** under tension.

**Formula:**

$$ E_{eq} = \dfrac{E}{1 + \dfrac{(w_c L)^2 \cdot A}{12 T^3}} $$

**Why used:**
- Cables sag into a catenary under their own weight.
- This sag reduces the effective axial stiffness.
- $E_{eq}$ models the cable as an equivalent straight truss element for stiffness matrix analysis.
- Provides accurate results in global structural analysis using software.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark formula, 1 mark physical reason (sag), 1 mark utility in analysis.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose either A or B**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **structural behavior and components of a Bow String Bridge**. Discuss the differences between **Langer, Nielsen, and Lohse bridges**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic bow string arch has a span of **80 m** and a rise of **12 m**. It carries a uniformly distributed load of **40 kN/m** over the entire span. Calculate:
- (i) The **horizontal thrust** in the arch
- (ii) The **tie force**
- (iii) The **vertical reactions** at supports
- (iv) The **bending moment** in the tie at a distance of 20 m from the left support
- (v) The **axial force in the end post** (inclination with vertical to be computed) **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Structural Behavior & Components (7 Marks):**

**Components of a Bow String Bridge:**

1. **Arch Rib:** The curved upper member, typically parabolic in shape, that carries loads primarily in **axial compression**. Materials: steel, reinforced concrete, or composite construction.
2. **Tie Girder:** A horizontal tension member at deck level. Resists the horizontal thrust of the arch. Often a **prestressed concrete box girder** or a **steel plate girder**.
3. **Hangers / Vertical Members:** Transfer deck loads to the arch. May be vertical (Langer) or inclined (Nielsen, Lohse).
4. **End Posts:** The portions of the arch between the springing points and the deck-ends. Carry the combined axial force from the arch and the shear from the deck.
5. **Deck Slab:** Carries the traffic load. Supported on the tie girder.
6. **Abutments:** Receive **only vertical reactions** (since the tie absorbs the horizontal thrust).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Langer | Nielsen | Lohse |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stiff Member** | Tie Girder | Arch Rib | Both (stiff) |
| **Flexible Member** | Arch Rib | Tie Girder | — |
| **Hanger Inclination** | Vertical | Inclined | Inclined |
| **Typical Span** | 40–100 m | 80–200 m | 50–150 m |
| **Hanger Force** | Tensile only | Compressive too (in some) | Compressive too |
| **Aesthetic** | Simple | Elegant | Hybrid |

> **Mark split:** 4 marks for components (with sketch), 3 marks for type comparison.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Span $L = 80$ m
- Rise $f = 12$ m
- UDL $w = 40$ kN/m
- Distance of section $x = 20$ m from left support

**Step 1 — Vertical Reactions:**

$$ V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{40 \times 80}{2} = 1600 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Thrust:**

$$ H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8f} = \dfrac{40 \times (80)^2}{8 \times 12} = \dfrac{40 \times 6400}{96} = \dfrac{256000}{96} = 2666.67 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Tie Force:**

By horizontal equilibrium of the bridge:

$$ T = H = 2666.67 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment in Tie at x = 20 m:**

First, compute the rise $y$ at $x = 20$ m using the parabolic equation:

$$ y = \dfrac{4f}{L^2} \cdot x(L - x) = \dfrac{4 \times 12}{(80)^2} \cdot 20 \times (80 - 20) $$

$$ y = \dfrac{48}{6400} \cdot 20 \times 60 = 0.0075 \times 1200 = 9.0 \text{ m} $$

Simple beam moment at $x = 20$ m:

$$ M_{beam} = V_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w \cdot x^2}{2} = 1600 \times 20 - \dfrac{40 \times 400}{2} = 32000 - 8000 = 24000 \text{ kN·m} $$

Bending moment in the tie:

$$ M_{tie} = M_{beam} - H \cdot y = 24000 - 2666.67 \times 9.0 $$

$$ M_{tie} = 24000 - 24000.03 = -0.03 \approx 0 \text{ kN·m} $$

**Explanation:** For a parabolic arch under uniform full-span load, the moment in the tie is theoretically **zero at every point** because the arch is funicular for UDL. The small discrepancy is due to rounding.

**Step 5 — End Post Axial Force:**

Vertical component at end post = $V_A = 1600$ kN.
Horizontal component = $H = 2666.67$ kN.

Resultant:

$$ F_{end} = \sqrt{H^2 + V_A^2} = \sqrt{(2666.67)^2 + (1600)^2} $$

$$ F_{end} = \sqrt{7111129 + 2560000} = \sqrt{9671129} = 3109.85 \text{ kN} $$

Inclination with vertical:

$$ \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{H}{V_A}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2666.67}{1600}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.6667) = 59.04^{\circ} $$

> **Mark split for Part (b):** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 1 mark, (iv) 2 marks, (v) 1.5 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **structural action and components of a cable-stayed bridge** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **fan, harp, and semi-fan cable arrangements** with their relative merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cable-stayed bridge has **3 cables on each side** of a single tower in a **fan arrangement**. The tower height is **30 m** above the deck. The cables anchor the deck at horizontal distances of **20 m, 35 m, and 50 m** from the tower base on each side. Each cable supports a vertical load of **200 kN**. Calculate:
- (i) The **inclination angles** of the three cables
- (ii) The **tension** in each cable
- (iii) The **vertical force** transmitted to the tower by all 3 main-span cables
- (iv) The **horizontal compression** induced in the deck by these cables
- (v) If a back-stay cable on each side makes an angle of **30°** with the horizontal and carries a tension of **1500 kN**, determine the **resultant force and its direction at the tower top** due to all cables on one side. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Structural Action & Cable Patterns (7 Marks):**

**Components:**
1. **Tower (Pylon):** Carries the cables and transfers loads to the foundation. Made of reinforced/prestressed concrete or steel. Common shapes: H-frame, A-frame, inverted Y, single pylon.
2. **Stay Cables:** High-tensile steel wire bundles (PWS or PSS), typically 1570–1860 MPa. Transfer deck loads to tower.
3. **Deck:** Orthotropic steel deck, prestressed concrete, or composite. Acts as a continuous beam elastically supported at cable anchor points; also experiences **axial compression** from cable horizontal components.
4. **Anchorages:** Critical details at both the deck and the tower ends. Must resist fatigue and corrosion.
5. **Side Spans / Back-Stay Cables:** Anchor the system, providing the reaction that balances the main-span cable forces.

**Cable Arrangement Comparison:**

| Pattern | Description | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Harp** | All cables parallel, equal spacing on tower | Aesthetically pleasing; simple geometry | Less efficient — long upper cables carry more load |
| **Fan** | All cables converge to one point at tower top | Most efficient; shortest upper cables | Tower top congestion; construction difficulty |
| **Semi-Fan** | Cables spread over a finite region at tower top | Balanced efficiency and constructability | More complex analysis |

> **Mark split:** 3 marks for components + sketch, 4 marks for cable pattern comparison.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Tower height $h = 30$ m
- Anchorage distances: $x_1 = 20$ m, $x_2 = 35$ m, $x_3 = 50$ m
- Vertical load per cable: $P = 200$ kN

**Step 1 — Inclination Angles:**

For each cable, $\theta_i = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{x_i}\right)$:

$$ \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30}{20}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31^{\circ} $$

$$ \theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30}{35}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.857) = 40.60^{\circ} $$

$$ \theta_3 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{30}{50}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.6) = 30.96^{\circ} $$

**Step 2 — Cable Tensions:**

For a fan pattern, vertical equilibrium at the deck anchorage:

$$ T_i \cdot \sin\theta_i = P $$

$$ T_1 = \dfrac{200}{\sin(56.31^{\circ})} = \dfrac{200}{0.8321} = 240.36 \text{ kN} $$

$$ T_2 = \dfrac{200}{\sin(40.60^{\circ})} = \dfrac{200}{0.6508} = 307.31 \text{ kN} $$

$$ T_3 = \dfrac{200}{\sin(30.96^{\circ})} = \dfrac{200}{0.5141} = 389.03 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 3 — Vertical Force on Tower from Main-Span Cables:**

The vertical component of each cable pulls down on the tower top.

$$ V_{tower} = \sum P_i = 200 + 200 + 200 = 600 \text{ kN (per side)} $$

**Step 4 — Horizontal Compression in Deck:**

The horizontal component of each cable pushes the deck toward the tower:

$$ H_1 = T_1 \cdot \cos\theta_1 = 240.36 \times \cos(56.31^{\circ}) = 240.36 \times 0.5547 = 133.34 \text{ kN} $$

$$ H_2 = 307.31 \times \cos(40.60^{\circ}) = 307.31 \times 0.7593 = 233.31 \text{ kN} $$

$$ H_3 = 389.03 \times \cos(30.96^{\circ}) = 389.03 \times 0.8577 = 333.69 \text{ kN} $$

**Total horizontal compression in the deck on this side:**

$$ H_{total} = 133.34 + 233.31 + 333.69 = 700.34 \text{ kN} $$

**Step 5 — Resultant at Tower Top with Back-Stay:**

Back-stay cable: $T_b = 1500$ kN, $\theta_b = 30°$ (inclined up toward back-stay anchorage).

Horizontal component of back-stay (pulling tower top away from main span):

$$ H_b = T_b \cdot \cos(30^{\circ}) = 1500 \times 0.866 = 1299 \text{ kN} $$

Vertical component of back-stay (downward on tower):

$$ V_b = T_b \cdot \sin(30^{\circ}) = 1500 \times 0.5 = 750 \text{ kN} $$

Net horizontal force at tower top (main span side compression minus back-stay pull):

$$ F_x = 700.34 - 1299 = -598.66 \text{ kN} $$

Net vertical force at tower top (all cables pull down on tower):

$$ F_y = 600 + 750 = 1350 \text{ kN (downward on tower)} $$

Resultant force at tower top:

$$ R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{(598.66)^2 + (1350)^2} = \sqrt{358394 + 1822500} = \sqrt{2180894} = 1476.8 \text{ kN} $$

Direction:

$$ \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{F_y}{F_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1350}{598.66}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2.255) = 66.07^{\circ} \text{ (from horizontal)} $$

> **Mark split for Part (b):** (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 1 mark, (iv) 1 mark, (v) 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing tie force with thrust in non-parabolic arches:** The relation $T = H$ holds **only for parabolic arches under UDL**. For triangular or concentrated loads, re-derive.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Many students write $H$ in kN but forget to specify. Always write **units explicitly**.
> 3. **In cable-stayed problems, students often confuse the angle convention** — angle of cable with the horizontal vs. angle with the vertical. State the convention at the start.
> 4. **In Ernst's formula problems, students often write the formula but fail to explain the physical meaning** — that is mandatory for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in 7-mark descriptive questions — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing a labeled diagram.
> 6. **Mixing up Langer and Nielsen types** — a classic trap question. Remember: **Langer = stiff tie; Nielsen = stiff arch**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Bow String Bridges
- [ ] Bow string = tied arch = arch + horizontal tension member.
- [ ] **Horizontal thrust** $H = wL^2 / (8f)$ for parabolic arch under UDL.
- [ ] **Tie force** $T = H$ for full-span UDL.
- [ ] **Bending moment in tie** $M_{tie} = M_{beam} - H \cdot y$ (beam analogy).
- [ ] **Langer** — stiff tie, flexible arch, vertical hangers.
- [ ] **Nielsen** — stiff arch, flexible tie, inclined hangers.
- [ ] **Lohse** — both stiff, inclined hangers.
- [ ] **End post** force = resultant of $H$ and $V$ = $\sqrt{H^2 + V^2}$.
- [ ] Abutments carry **only vertical reactions** (no horizontal thrust).
- [ ] Span range: **50–250 m**.

### Cable Stayed Bridges
- [ ] Deck supported by **straight cables** directly from tower.
- [ ] Difference from suspension bridge: no catenary cable, no suspender hangers.
- [ ] **Cable tension** $T_i = w \cdot d_i / \sin\theta_i$.
- [ ] **Cable angle** $\theta_i = \tan^{-1}(h / x_i)$.
- [ ] **Harp, Fan, Semi-Fan** — semi-fan is most common.
- [ ] **Deck pre-compression** is a major design advantage.
- [ ] **Ernst's equivalent modulus** accounts for cable sag:

$$ E_{eq} = \dfrac{E}{1 + \dfrac{(w_c L)^2 A}{12 T^3}} $$

- [ ] Tower shapes: H-frame, A-frame, inverted Y, diamond, single pylon.
- [ ] Cable material: high-tensile steel (**1570–1860 MPa**).
- [ ] Span range: **200 m to 1100 m+**.
- [ ] Tower height ≈ **0.20 L to 0.25 L** for economy.

### Quick Comparative Memory Aid
- **Bow String** = "Arch and string of a bow, tied together" → self-anchored.
- **Cable Stayed** = "Cables stay the deck directly to the tower" → semi-self-anchored.
- **Suspension** (not in this module, but good contrast) = "Cables hang the deck via vertical suspenders from a draped main cable."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme asks 14-mark questions with internal choice. Always attempt the question you are **most confident** with. Bow-string numerical problems (thrust, tie force, end post) and Ernst's formula derivations appear in **every alternate semester** — practice these.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Launching Techniques for Heavy Decks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Launching Techniques for Heavy Decks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Launching Techniques for Heavy Decks** refer to a specialized class of mechanized construction methodologies employed to position, translate, or install large-span, high-tonnage structural deck units (typically bridge decks, girders, and platform assemblies weighing in excess of **100 metric tonnes**) into their final design position when conventional crane-based erection is either physically impossible, economically unviable, or geometrically obstructed by site constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PECET522 — Modern Construction Technology), launching techniques constitute the **Module 3** nucleus, emphasizing heavy civil engineering applications such as:
- Long-span bridge superstructure erection.
- Viaduct and elevated metro/flyover deck placement.
- Industrial platform installations and offshore module placements.

The four dominant launching philosophies recognized by KTU are:

| S. No. | Technique | Core Principle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Incremental Launching Method (ILM)** | Segment-by-segment casting behind an abutment, then horizontal jacking forward over piers. |
| 2 | **Strand Jack / Heavy Lift Method** | Vertical or inclined translation using pre-stressed steel strands and hydraulic rams. |
| 3 | **Skidding / Sliding Method** | Translation of pre-assembled deck on lubricated PTFE-Teflon bearing pads using horizontal jacks. |
| 4 | **Floating / Gantry Erection** | Transporting the deck over water via pontoons or atop an overhead launching gantry. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Sliding a Heavy Sofa Into a Tight Corner**
>
> Imagine trying to move a **500 kg marble sofa** into a room where the doorway is too narrow for a crane and the floor is too delicate for a forklift. You would:
> 1. Build a **slippery runway** (PTFE pads) on the floor.
> 2. Push it with a **hydraulic piston** (strand jack) attached to the wall.
> 3. Move it in **small controlled pushes** (incremental launching) so that you never exceed the floor's load capacity at any one point.
>
> Launching techniques for heavy decks follow the same logic — we engineer the **path**, **support reaction envelope**, and **propulsion system** so that the deck reaches its final position safely, segment by segment.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Industry Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark Thresholds for "Heavy Deck" Classification**
> - **Deck Mass Range:** 200 – 25,000 tonnes per segment/launch unit.
> - **Launching Velocity:** 5 – 30 m/hr (incremental launching); 0.5 – 2 m/min (strand jack lift).
> - **Hydraulic Jack Capacity (Modern):** Up to **1,200 tonnes per ram** with multi-strand systems reaching **15,000 tonnes** total lift.
> - **Coefficient of Friction (PTFE-on-Stainless Steel):** $\mu \approx 0.02 - 0.06$ (unlubricated); $\mu \approx 0.01$ (lubricated with silicone grease).
> - **Standard Governing Codes:** EN 1991-3 (Actions on Bridges), AASHTO LRFD, IS 1343 (Prestressed Concrete), BS 5400.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Incremental Launching Method (ILM)

The ILM is the most commonly examined technique in KTU 2024 ESE papers. It operates on six discrete operational stages:

### Stage 1 — Casting Bed Preparation
- A reinforced concrete casting yard is established **behind the abutment** (typically 1.0× to 1.5× the longest span length).
- Formwork (steel or specialized self-launching) is mounted, and the first deck segment (3–25 m long) is cast.

### Stage 2 — Prestressing
- Longitudinal pre-stressing tendons (typically **12T15 / 19T15 / 27T15** systems) are stressed to **70%–80% of ultimate tensile strength (UTS)**, i.e., $\sigma_p \approx 0.70 \, f_{pu} - 0.80 \, f_{pu}$.
- The segment is now a self-supporting cantilever under its own weight.

### Stage 3 — Launching (Jacking Forward)
- **Vertical jacks** at the rear abutment push the segment forward; **vertical jacks** at each pier nose support the leading edge.
- A **launching nose** (a lighter steel truss, typically 60%–70% of the deck's moment of inertia) is bolted to the front to reduce cantilever moments over piers.

### Stage 4 — Segment Casting & Repetition
- The next segment is cast directly against the previously launched one (match-cast joint or wet joint).
- The process repeats: cast → prestress → launch → cast → prestress → launch.

### Stage 5 — Closure
- The final segment is cast in a closure gap (usually at mid-span or near a fixed pier) to compensate for accumulated geometric tolerances.

### Stage 6 — Bearing Replacement
- Temporary PTFE launching bearings are replaced with **permanent pot-PTFE or elastomeric bearings** after final position is reached.

## 2.2 The Launching Nose — Geometric Optimization

The launching nose is a **steel lattice truss** that protrudes ahead of the deck during the launch. Its primary role is to **counter the cantilever moment** when the leading deck edge is between two piers.

The optimal nose length $L_n$ is:

$$\boxed{L_n = 0.60 \, L_{span} \text{ to } 0.80 \, L_{span}}$$

where $L_{span}$ is the longest span in the bridge. The **nose weight** is typically **15%–25% of the deck's self-weight** per unit length.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat-Sheet — All Numerical Problems in KTU 2024 are solvable from this table.**

| S. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cantilever moment (nose) | $M_c = \dfrac{q_d \, x^2}{2} + \dfrac{q_n \, (x-L_n)^2}{2}$ for $x > L_n$ | kN·m | $q_d$ = deck UDL, $q_n$ = nose UDL |
| 2 | Maximum launch force | $F_{launch} = \mu \cdot \sum R_i + F_{wind} + F_{gradient}$ | kN | $\sum R_i$ = total vertical reaction |
| 3 | Frictional resistance | $F_{fric} = \mu \cdot W_{total}$ | kN | $W_{total}$ = total weight on sliding bearings |
| 4 | Strand jack capacity | $P_{jack} = n_{strands} \cdot A_{strand} \cdot f_{pu} \cdot \eta_{eff}$ | kN | $\eta_{eff} \approx 0.85$ efficiency |
| 5 | Pier reaction during launch | $R_i = \dfrac{q_d \cdot L_{eff,i}}{2} + \dfrac{M_{cantilever,i}}{L_{i,i+1}}$ | kN | Computed via influence lines |
| 6 | Skidding track design | $\sigma_{bearing} = \dfrac{R_{max}}{A_{pad}} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | MPa | Pad stress check |
| 7 | Launching nose stiffness ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{(EI)_n}{(EI)_d}$ | — | Recommended: $0.10 \leq \eta \leq 0.25$ |
| 8 | Gantry lifting force | $F_{gantry} = \dfrac{W_{deck}}{n_{trolleys}} \cdot SF$ | kN | $SF \geq 1.5$ |
| 9 | Deflection of cantilever nose | $\delta = \dfrac{q_n \cdot L_n^4}{8 (EI)_n}$ | m | Used for geometric pre-camber |
| 10 | Prestress after elastic shortening | $f_{pe} = f_{pj} - \Delta f_p$ | MPa | $\Delta f_p$ = losses (~$10\% f_{pj}$) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Bridges:** The Millau Viaduct (France) and the Second Severn Crossing (UK) used ILM for spans up to **148 m**.
- **Metro/Rail Viaducts:** All elevated metro systems in India (Delhi, Bengaluru, Kochi) use launching gantries for spans of **25–35 m** and deck weights of **200–400 tonnes**.
- **Offshore Modules:** North Sea oil platforms use strand jacks to lift topsides of **30,000+ tonnes**.
- **Industrial Construction:** Heavy lift skidding is used for refinery modules and LNG tanks where crane reach is impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Launching Force on a Multi-Span ILM Bridge

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style):**
A 5-span continuous bridge is launched incrementally. Each span $L = 40$ m. The deck is a single-cell PSC box girder with **self-weight $q_d = 180$ kN/m** (uniform along its length). The launching nose has $q_n = 35$ kN/m and length $L_n = 30$ m. Coefficient of friction on PTFE bearings $\mu = 0.04$. The total deck weight at the moment of full launch $W_{total} = 180 \times (5 \times 40) = 36,000$ kN.

**Find:** (a) Total launch force required; (b) Maximum reaction at the leading pier; (c) Maximum cantilever moment at the leading edge.

---

### Step 1 — Total Launch Force (Overcoming Friction)

The launch force must overcome friction on **all bearings simultaneously**, because as the deck moves forward, every pier experiences sliding contact.

$$F_{launch} = \mu \cdot W_{total}$$

$$F_{launch} = 0.04 \times 36{,}000$$

$$F_{launch} = 1{,}440 \text{ kN}$$

So **two 800 kN jacks** at the rear abutment are sufficient (with $SF = 1.1$).

---

### Step 2 — Maximum Reaction at the Leading Pier (Influence Line Method)

During launch, the leading pier (P5) supports a portion of the deck cantilever. Using the **influence line for shear at the penultimate pier** when the leading edge is just at the pier:

$$R_{P5, max} = q_d \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{span}}{2} + L_n \right) \cdot k_{distribution}$$

For a simply supported span moment transfer with overhang:

$$R_{P5, max} = 180 \times \left( 20 + 30 \right) \times 0.55$$

$$R_{P5, max} = 180 \times 50 \times 0.55$$

$$R_{P5, max} = 4{,}950 \text{ kN}$$

---

### Step 3 — Maximum Cantilever Moment at the Leading Pier

The worst-case moment occurs when the **nose tip is just touching the next pier** (P6) and the deck has maximum overhang. The cantilever length is $L_{c} = L_{span} = 40$ m, of which $L_n = 30$ m is occupied by the (lighter) nose.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{cantilever, max} &= \frac{q_d \cdot (L_c - L_n)^2}{2} + \frac{q_n \cdot L_n^2}{2} \cdot \alpha \\
&= \frac{180 \times (40 - 30)^2}{2} + \frac{35 \times 30^2}{2} \cdot 0.85 \\
&= \frac{180 \times 100}{2} + \frac{35 \times 900}{2} \cdot 0.85 \\
&= 9{,}000 + 15{,}750 \times 0.85 \\
&= 9{,}000 + 13{,}387.5 \\
&= 22{,}387.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $\alpha = 0.85$ accounts for the partial composite action between the nose and the deck stub.

---

### Step 4 — Required Pre-Stress to Counter Cantilever Moment

For a PSC box girder with section modulus $Z_{bottom} = 8.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$ and $Z_{top} = 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$:

Required pre-stress force at the bottom fiber:

$$F_{ps} = \frac{M_{cantilever, max}}{Z_{bottom} \cdot e_{ps}} \cdot k_{eccentricity}$$

For tendons at $e_{ps} = 600$ mm eccentricity and $k_{eccentricity} = 1.2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ps} &= \frac{22{,}387.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}}{8.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 \times 600 \text{ mm}} \times 1.2 \\
&= \frac{22{,}387.5 \times 10^6}{5.1 \times 10^9} \times 1.2 \\
&= 4.39 \times 1.2 \\
&= 5.27 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to approximately **36 strands of 15.7 mm diameter** (each strand ≈ 1860 MPa UTS, area 150 mm² → 279 kN per strand, 70% stressing → 195 kN per strand).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Launching Technique

| Decision Parameter | ILM | Strand Jack Lift | Skidding | Gantry Launching |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Span Range** | 30 – 100 m | Any | ≤ 50 m | 25 – 60 m |
| **Deck Weight** | 5,000 – 50,000 t | 500 – 30,000 t | 1,000 – 15,000 t | 200 – 2,000 t |
| **Site Access** | Long straight alignment | Flexible | Requires skid track | Clear overhead |
| **Tolerance Control** | ±10 mm | ±3 mm | ±15 mm | ±20 mm |
| **Construction Duration** | Slow (segmental) | Fast (single lift) | Medium | Fast |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | 0.8 | 1.5 | 1.0 | 0.9 |
| **Typical Application** | Highway bridges | Bridge replacements | Industrial modules | Metro viaducts |
| **Critical Risk** | Pier overload | Strand failure | Bearing shear | Trolley derailment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Incremental Launching Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Site Survey and Pier Construction] --> A2[Build Casting Bed Behind Abutment]
    A2 --> A3[Cast First PSC Segment 3 to 25 m]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Longitudinal Prestressing 70 to 80 percent UTS]
    A4 --> A5[Attach Launching Nose to Front]
    A5 --> A6[Activate Hydraulic Jacks at Rear Abutment]
    A6 --> A7[Slide Segment Forward 200 to 500 mm Stroke]
    A7 --> A8[Monitor Pier Reactions in Real Time]
    A8 --> A9{Nose Tip Reaches Next Pier?}
    A9 -- No --> A7
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Lock Nose onto Next Pier Bearings]
    A10 --> A11[Cast Next Segment Behind Previous]
    A11 --> A12[Prestress and Launch Combined Assembly]
    A12 --> A13{Final Position Reached?}
    A13 -- No --> A11
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Cast Closure Segment at Mid Span]
    A14 --> A15[Replace Temporary Bearings with Permanent Pot PTFE Bearings]
    A15 --> A16[Load Test and Deck Commissioning]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Strand Jack System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Central Hydraulic Power Pack] --> B2[Master Control Console]
    B2 --> B3[Strand Jack Unit 1 Anchor Frame]
    B2 --> B4[Strand Jack Unit 2 Anchor Frame]
    B2 --> B5[Strand Jack Unit 3 Anchor Frame]
    B2 --> B6[Strand Jack Unit 4 Anchor Frame]
    B3 --> C1[Pre Stressed Strands Bundle 1]
    B4 --> C2[Pre Stressed Strands Bundle 2]
    B5 --> C3[Pre Stressed Strands Bundle 3]
    B6 --> C4[Pre Stressed Strands Bundle 4]
    C1 --> D1[Heavy Deck Load Block]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Load Cells Continuous Monitoring]
    E1 --> B2
    D1 --> F1[Position Encoders Stroke Feedback]
    F1 --> B2
    B1 --> G1[Synchronization PLC]
    G1 --> B2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree for Launching Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Heavy Deck to be Installed] --> S2{Is Overhead Clearance Available?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Deck Weight greater than 5000 tonnes?}
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is the Bridge on a Straight Alignment?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Use Incremental Launching Method ILM]
    S3 -- No --> S6{Single Span or Replacement Project?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S7[Use Skidding Method with PTFE Bearings]
    S4 -- No --> S8{Can Heavy Lift Crane Access Site?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S9[Use Strand Jack Lifting System]
    S6 -- No --> S10[Use Segmental Launching with Gantry]
    S8 -- Yes --> S11[Use Conventional Crane Erection]
    S8 -- No --> S12[Use Self Propelled Modular Transporter SPMT]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Incremental Launching Method (ILM). State any two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Incremental Launching Method (ILM) is a technique of bridge construction in which a pre-stressed concrete deck is cast in **short segments behind an abutment** and progressively pushed forward — using hydraulic jacks — over the piers to its final position.

> **Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
> 1. Requires **minimal falsework** and no scaffolding in the span, making it ideal for crossing valleys, rivers, and active traffic.
> 2. Casting is done at **ground level** behind the abutment, ensuring superior quality control and worker safety.
> 3. The method is highly **economical for long multi-span bridges** (typically > 400 m total length).

> **Limitation (1 mark):**
> - ILM is best suited for **straight or uniformly curved bridges** with constant cross-section; sharply curved or variable-depth decks cannot be launched.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]
**What is a launching nose? Why is it used in the Incremental Launching Method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **launching nose** is a **lightweight steel lattice truss** (typically 60%–80% of the longest span length) attached to the front of the deck during ILM to **reduce the cantilever bending moment** that would otherwise develop when the leading edge of the deck is between two piers.

> **Why it is used (2 marks):**
> 1. It shifts the **center of gravity of the overhang** forward, causing the resultant reaction to remain on the rear pier until the nose reaches the next pier.
> 2. It reduces the **maximum negative moment** at the leading pier by approximately **40%–60%**, thus minimizing the prestress required and the cross-section size of the deck.
> 3. It allows the **span-to-depth ratio** of the deck to be kept economical (typically L/15 to L/18).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step construction sequence** of the Incremental Launching Method (ILM) for a 4-span continuous PSC box girder bridge. Support your answer with a labeled schematic of the casting yard and pier arrangement. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A PSC box girder bridge with **5 equal spans of 35 m** is being launched using ILM. Deck self-weight $q_d = 165$ kN/m. Launching nose weight $q_n = 28$ kN/m and length $L_n = 25$ m. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.03$. Calculate:
- (i) The total launch force required. **\[2 marks\]**
- (ii) The maximum cantilever moment at the leading pier. **\[3 marks\]**
- (iii) The number of 15.7 mm dia strands (each 195 kN working load) required at the bottom fiber to counter the cantilever moment, assuming section modulus $Z_b = 6.5 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$ and tendon eccentricity $e = 550$ mm. **\[2 marks\]**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction Sequence \[7 marks\]

1. **Site Preparation & Pier Construction (1 mark):** Construct all piers and abutments to their final design elevation. Install temporary launching bearings (PTFE-faced) atop each pier.

2. **Casting Yard Setup (1 mark):** Build a casting bed directly behind the starting abutment, of length at least **1.2 × longest span** (i.e., 1.2 × 35 = 42 m). Install internal and external steel formwork.

3. **First Segment Casting (1 mark):** Cast the first PSC segment (typically 10–20 m long) using high-strength concrete (M50/M60). Install and stress longitudinal tendons once the concrete achieves **75% of $f_{ck}$**.

4. **Launching Nose Attachment (1 mark):** Bolt a steel launching nose (lattice truss, $L_n = 25$ m) to the front of the cast segment using high-strength preloaded bolts.

5. **Hydraulic Jacking (1 mark):** Activate rear-abutment jacks to push the segment forward in strokes of 200–500 mm. Continuously monitor pier reactions.

6. **Subsequent Segments (1 mark):** Cast the next segment against the previously launched one (match-cast joint), prestress, and repeat. Each cycle advances the deck by one segment length.

7. **Closure & Bearing Replacement (1 mark):** Cast a closure segment near mid-span, release the temporary bearings, and replace them with **permanent pot-PTFE bearings**.

> **Schematic (to be drawn by student in exam):** A longitudinal elevation showing: Casting bed (left) → Rear jacks → 5 piers with PTFE bearings → Launching nose at front → Direction of launch arrow.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution \[7 marks\]

**(i) Total Launch Force (2 marks):**

$$W_{total} = q_d \times 5L = 165 \times 5 \times 35 = 28{,}875 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{launch} = \mu \cdot W_{total} = 0.03 \times 28{,}875 = 866.25 \text{ kN} \approx \mathbf{867 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Cantilever Moment (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{c,max} &= \frac{q_d \cdot (L_{span} - L_n)^2}{2} + \frac{q_n \cdot L_n^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{165 \times (35 - 25)^2}{2} + \frac{28 \times 25^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{165 \times 100}{2} + \frac{28 \times 625}{2} \\
&= 8{,}250 + 8{,}750 \\
&= \mathbf{17{,}000 \text{ kN·m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of Strands (2 marks):**

Required pre-stressing force:

$$F_{ps} = \frac{M_{c,max}}{Z_b \cdot e} = \frac{17{,}000 \times 10^6}{6.5 \times 10^6 \times 550} = \frac{17{,}000 \times 10^6}{3.575 \times 10^9} = 4.76 \text{ MN} = 4{,}755 \text{ kN}$$

Number of strands:

$$n = \frac{F_{ps}}{P_{strand}} = \frac{4{,}755}{195} = 24.4 \approx \mathbf{25 \text{ strands}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **Strand Jack Lifting Method** for heavy deck erection. List its components and explain its working principle with a neat sketch. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A bridge deck weighing **8,500 tonnes** is to be lifted by a strand jack system. The system uses **strands of 15.7 mm diameter** ($A_s = 150 \text{ mm}^2$, UTS = 1,860 MPa, stressing level 70%). If the system efficiency is 85% and the safety factor required is 1.5, calculate:
- (i) The **capacity of one strand**. **\[2 marks\]**
- (ii) The **total number of strands** required in the system. **\[2 marks\]**
- (iii) If the deck must be lifted to a height of **12 m** in **90 minutes**, determine the **required average lifting speed** in mm/min. **\[3 marks\]**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Strand Jack Method \[7 marks\]

**Working Principle (3 marks):** The strand jack system uses **pre-stressed steel strands** anchored between a fixed anchor frame and a lifting anchor frame. Hydraulic rams grip the strands through **wedge grips** and lift the load. After each stroke, the upper wedges lock onto the strands while the lower wedges release and reset, enabling a continuous lift.

**Components (2 marks):**
1. **Hydraulic Power Pack** — supplies pressurized oil.
2. **Master Control Console** — synchronizes multiple jacks.
3. **Strand Jack Units** — each contains a hollow hydraulic ram with two sets of wedge grips.
4. **Pre-stressing Strands** — 15.7 mm or 18 mm dia, galvanised or greased.
5. **Anchor Frames** — fixed and lifting frames.
6. **Load Cells** — for real-time load monitoring.
7. **Position Encoders** — for stroke tracking.

**Sketch (2 marks):** Student should draw a labeled diagram showing anchor frame (top), hydraulic jack, strands passing through, lifting frame attached to deck, and arrows indicating load direction.

#### Part (b) — Numerical \[7 marks\]

**(i) Capacity of one strand (2 marks):**

$$P_{strand} = A_s \cdot f_{pu} \cdot \text{stress level} = 150 \times 1860 \times 0.70 = 195{,}300 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{195 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Total number of strands (2 marks):**

Required total capacity with $SF = 1.5$ and $\eta_{eff} = 0.85$:

$$F_{required} = \frac{W_{deck} \cdot SF}{\eta_{eff}} = \frac{8{,}500 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}{0.85}$$

$$F_{required} = \frac{125{,}077.5}{0.85} = 147{,}150 \text{ kN}$$

$$n_{strands} = \frac{F_{required}}{P_{strand}} = \frac{147{,}150}{195} = 754.6 \approx \mathbf{755 \text{ strands}}$$

**(iii) Average lifting speed (3 marks):**

$$v_{avg} = \frac{h}{t} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ mm}}{90 \text{ min}} = \mathbf{133.33 \text{ mm/min}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert deck weight to force (kN)** using $W = m \cdot g$. A student writing "8,500 kN" instead of "83,385 kN" loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor** in the strand count. KTU explicitly tests $SF \geq 1.5$ for heavy lift operations.
> 3. **Mixing up units** in launching-force problems (kN vs. tonnes-force). Always state the unit in every step.
> 4. **Skipping the launching nose** in ILM sketches — examiners allot 1 full mark for a correctly drawn nose.
> 5. **Not mentioning the temporary vs. permanent bearing transition** — this is a KTU-favourite 1-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- **Heavy Deck Threshold:** > 100 tonnes (industry); > 500 tonnes (KTU exam convention).
- **Four Launching Methods:** ILM, Strand Jack, Skidding, Gantry — know at least **two** in detail.
- **Launching Nose Length:** $0.6L$ to $0.8L$ of the longest span.
- **Nose Stiffness Ratio:** $\eta = (EI)_n / (EI)_d \approx 0.10$ to $0.25$.
- **PTFE Friction Coefficient:** $\mu = 0.01$ (lubricated) to $0.06$ (dry).
- **Strand Jack Strand Capacity:** ~195 kN per 15.7 mm strand at 70% UTS.
- **Safety Factor for Heavy Lifting:** $SF \geq 1.5$ (mandatory in KTU).
- **ILM Sequence:** Cast → Prestress → Jack → Slide → Repeat → Closure → Bearing replacement.
- **Gantry Lifting Force:** $F = W / n_{trolleys}$ with $SF \geq 1.5$.
- **Critical Checks:** Pier reaction, cantilever moment, friction force, nose deflection, tendon eccentricity.
- **Real-World Examples:** Millau Viaduct (ILM), Delhi Metro (Gantry), North Sea Platforms (Strand Jack).
- **Preferred Method for Straight Multi-Span Bridges:** ILM.
- **Preferred Method for Single Heavy Lift (e.g., bridge replacement):** Strand Jack.
- **Always mention pre-camber** when discussing ILM nose deflection control.
- **Code References:** EN 1991-3, AASHTO LRFD, IS 1343, BS 5400 — do **not** cite fabricated references in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rapid Wall Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rapid Wall Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Wall Construction** is a modern, industrialized building methodology that employs prefabricated, modular, or factory-engineered walling systems to drastically reduce in-situ construction time. It shifts the major share of wall production from the open construction site to a controlled off-site fabrication environment, where the panels are cast, cured, finished, and quality-assured before being transported and assembled at the building location using cranes and mechanical connections.

Under the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECET522 – Modern Construction Technology), **Rapid Wall Construction** is classified as a sub-system of **Industrialized Building Systems (IBS)** and encompasses:

- **Precast Large Panel Systems (LPS)**
- **Aluminium Formwork (Mivan Technology)**
- **Tunnel Formwork Systems**
- **3D Volumetric / Modular Construction**
- **Sandwich / Composite Panel Systems**
- **Light Gauge Steel Frame (LGSF) Walls**
- **Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (AAC) Block Rapid Walling**

The primary objective of rapid walling is to achieve a wall cycle time of **24 to 72 hours per floor**, compared to the **7 to 14 days** required in conventional brick / RCC column-beam construction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Building" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Rapid Wall Construction as building with giant industrial Lego blocks.**

Imagine constructing a house the way a child assembles Lego:
1. The walls are **pre-made in a factory** (just like pre-moulded Lego pieces).
2. They arrive at the site **ready to install** — no waiting for curing, no plastering delays.
3. A **crane lifts each panel** and places it onto pre-set anchor bolts or dowels.
4. The joints are **welded, bolted, or grouted**, much like snapping Lego studs together.
5. The entire skeleton (walls + slabs) of a floor can be ready in **one to three days**, after which the next floor cycle begins.

In conventional construction, walls are **built piece-by-piece on-site** (brick by brick, with curing time in between). Rapid walling reverses this paradigm — **the site becomes an assembly yard, not a manufacturing plant**.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following benchmark parameters are evaluated in KTU examination questions on this topic:

| Parameter | Conventional Construction | Rapid Wall Construction |
|---|---|---|
| **Wall cycle time** | 7–14 days / floor | **24–72 hours / floor** |
| **Labour intensity** | 100 % (relative) | **30–50 %** (relative) |
| **Wastage of materials** | 8–12 % | **1–3 %** |
| **Quality control** | Site-dependent | **Factory-controlled (ISO 9001)** |
| **Weather dependency** | High | **Low** |
| **Formwork reusability** | 5–10 cycles (timber) | **100–250 cycles (aluminium / steel)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The student is expected to compare rapid wall systems with conventional construction with respect to **speed, cost, quality, durability, and seismic behaviour** — these are the five mandatory comparison axes in ESE questions.

---

## 1.4 Classification Tree of Rapid Wall Systems

The systems are classified on the basis of **material**, **structural action**, and **degree of prefabrication**:

```mermaid
%%<comparison_chart_placeholder>
```
<!-- (Mermaid chart redrawn in Section 4) -->

> **Classification Basis:**
> - **By Material:** Concrete-based, Steel-based, Composite (sandwich), Lightweight (AAC)
> - **By Structural Action:** Load-bearing, Non-load-bearing (partition), Shear-wall
> - **By Prefabrication Degree:** Component (2D panel) → Volumetric (3D module)

---

## 1.5 Why Rapid Wall Construction? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> The five drivers pushing rapid wall adoption in India (especially post-2020 PMAY, RERA, and Smart Cities push):

1. **Time Compression:** Real-estate IRR improves by **20–35 %** due to earlier handover.
2. **Quality Uniformity:** Tolerances reach **±3 mm** (factory) vs **±15 mm** (site).
3. **Labour Shortage:** Migrant-labour risk (post-COVID) makes mechanised walling essential.
4. **Sustainability:** Formwork reuse cuts embodied carbon by **15–25 %**.
5. **Seismic Resilience:** Monolithic joints (in Mivan / tunnel form) outperform stitched brickwork.

---

## 1.6 Intuitive "Site vs Factory" Workflow Snapshot

| Stage | Conventional Site Activity | Rapid Wall Activity |
|---|---|---|
| Wall casting | Wet masonry / in-situ RCC | **Factory cast / off-site precast** |
| Curing | 7–14 days (open site) | **Accelerated steam curing (6–8 hrs)** |
| Plastering | 12–20 mm both sides | **Factory finish, minimal touch-up** |
| Installation | Built in place | **Crane-lifted, mechanically anchored** |
| Jointing | Mortar joints | **Welded / bolted / dry-grouted** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Rapid Wall Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every rapid wall system, regardless of the technology brand, operates on **four fundamental engineering pillars**. KTU questions on this topic almost always test at least one of these pillars.

### Pillar 1 — **Standardisation & Repetition**
- Standardised panel dimensions (typically **600 mm modular grid**).
- Repetitive floor plan (G+3 to G+40 high-rise repetition).
- Reduces custom formwork, custom detailing, and on-site decision-making.

> **Why it matters:** Standardisation is the *enabler* of factory economics — without it, precast becomes uneconomical.

### Pillar 2 — **Factory-Controlled Production**
- Casting beds with **vibratory compaction** and **steam curing at 60–80 °C**.
- Achieves **M-30 to M-60 concrete strength in 24 hours**.
- Tolerances: **±3 mm** dimensional, **±2 mm** squareness.

### Pillar 3 — **Mechanical Handling & Connection**
- Crane capacity: **5 T to 20 T** (typical for residential panels).
- Connection types: **bolted, welded, grouted dowel, post-tensioned**.
- Connection design must transmit **shear, tension, and compression** between panels.

### Pillar 4 — **Monolithic or Seismically-Tied Behaviour**
- Walls must act as a **continuous diaphragm** with the floor slab.
- Joints must be ductile enough to absorb **seismic drift of 0.4 % to 1.5 %** of building height.
- This is verified through **IS 15916:2011** (IS Code for seismic design of precast) and **IS 11447:2014**.

---

## 2.2 Engineering Equations Governing Rapid Wall Systems

### 2.2.1 Panel Handling Stress (Crane-Lift Design)

When a precast wall panel is lifted off the casting bed, it experiences **bending stresses** due to self-weight acting as a cantilever. The KTU-standard equation is:

$$
\sigma_{lift} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8 Z} \quad \text{(uniformly distributed self-weight)}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{lift}$ = bending stress at the critical section during lifting (N/mm²)
- $M_{max}$ = maximum bending moment (N·mm)
- $w$ = self-weight per unit length (N/mm)
- $L$ = equivalent span between lift points (mm)
- $Z$ = section modulus of the panel (mm³)

For a panel lifted by **two-point top-mounted inserts** (most common case):

$$
\sigma_{lift} = \frac{w L^{2}}{2 Z}
$$

> **Design Rule:** The lifting stress must **not exceed 40 % of the 28-day concrete strength** at the time of lift, to prevent cracking (per ACI ITG-7 and IS 4990:2011).

### 2.2.2 Formwork Reuse Economics (Aluminium / Mivan Formwork)

$$
N_{reuse} = \frac{C_{formwork}}{C_{saved-per-cycle}} = \frac{C_{formwork}}{(C_{plaster} + C_{labour} - C_{amortised-formwork-per-cycle})}
$$

In typical Indian high-rise projects:

$$
N_{reuse}^{economic-break-even} \approx 100 \text{ to } 150 \text{ cycles}
$$

| Formwork Type | Reuse Cycles | Cost per m² (INR) | Wall Finish Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timber** | 5–10 | 250–400 | Poor (requires plaster) |
| **Plywood + Steel prop** | 20–30 | 400–600 | Moderate |
| **Aluminium (Mivan)** | **200–250** | **1500–2000** | **Excellent (no plaster)** |
| **Steel Tunnel** | **400–500** | **2500–3500** | Excellent |

### 2.2.3 Sandwich Panel Composite Action (Thermal + Structural)

For a **three-layer sandwich wall** (two wythes + insulation core), the composite efficiency factor:

$$
\eta = \frac{(EI)_{effective}}{(EI)_{fully-composite}}
$$

The thermal transmittance (**U-value**, a KTU-favourite parameter) is:

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{t_{i}}{k_{i}} + R_{se}}
$$

where:
- $R_{si}$ = internal surface resistance = **0.13 m²K/W**
- $R_{se}$ = external surface resistance = **0.04 m²K/W**
- $t_{i}$ = thickness of layer *i* (m)
- $k_{i}$ = thermal conductivity of layer *i* (W/mK)

> **Acceptance Threshold (ECBC 2017 / GRIHA):** $U \le 0.4 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}$ for external walls in **5-star rated** commercial buildings.

### 2.2.4 Wall Cycle Time Compression

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{erect} + T_{plumb} + T_{brace} + T_{deck} + T_{pour}
$$

For **Mivan technology**:

$$
T_{cycle}^{Mivan} = 1.0 + 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ days / floor}
$$

For **conventional RCC frame + brick infill**:

$$
T_{cycle}^{conv} = 5.0 + 3.0 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 2.0 = 12 \text{ to } 14 \text{ days / floor}
$$

> **Time Compression Factor:** $\frac{T_{cycle}^{conv}}{T_{cycle}^{Mivan}} \approx 4.0 \text{ to } 5.0$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all governing equations** for Rapid Wall Construction. Memorise the column "Exam Use" — that is exactly the framing expected in a 14-mark KTU ESE question.

| # | Equation | Variables / Units | Primary Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{lift} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{2 Z}$ | $\sigma$ in N/mm², $L$ in mm, $Z$ in mm³ | Lifting-stress check of precast panel |
| 2 | $\sigma_{lift} \le 0.4 f_{ck}$ | $f_{ck}$ in N/mm² | Codal compliance for safe lift |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum t_i / k_i + R_{se}}$ | $U$ in W/m²K, $t$ in m, $k$ in W/mK | Sandwich panel thermal design |
| 4 | $N_{reuse} = C_{formwork} / C_{saved}$ | $N$ dimensionless | Formwork economy proof |
| 5 | $T_{cycle} = T_{erect} + T_{plumb} + T_{brace} + T_{deck} + T_{pour}$ | Days | Time-compression comparison |
| 6 | $\eta = (EI)_{eff} / (EI)_{composite}$ | Dimensionless | Sandwich panel efficiency |
| 7 | $\theta_{drift} = \Delta / H$ | Drift angle (rad) | Seismic joint-design check |
| 8 | $V_{joint} = \tau \cdot A_{contact} + \mu \cdot N_{pre}$ | $V$ in N, $A$ in mm², $N$ in N | Shear capacity of dry joint |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Lifting Stress in a Precast Wall Panel

> **Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
> A precast wall panel of dimensions **3000 mm × 2400 mm × 150 mm** is to be lifted by **two symmetrically placed top inserts** located at **750 mm from each end**. The unit weight of RCC is **25 kN/m³**. Determine the maximum lifting stress and verify whether the panel can be safely lifted at a concrete strength of **20 N/mm²** (one-day strength after steam curing).

### Step 1 — Convert Panel Geometry to a 1-D Line Load

The wall panel is essentially a flat plate, but for lifting analysis we model it as a **beam of length equal to the panel's longer dimension** (i.e., 3000 mm) with a uniformly distributed self-weight.

First, compute the **self-weight per unit length (w)**:

$$
w = \rho \cdot g \cdot t \cdot 1
$$

where:
- $\rho \cdot g = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (unit weight of RCC, given)
- $t = 0.150 \text{ m}$ (panel thickness)
- The factor "1" represents 1 m of panel width

$$
w = 25 \times 0.150 \times 1.0 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Convert to N/mm for SI consistency (since stress is in N/mm² and section modulus in mm³):

$$
w = 3.75 \text{ kN/m} = 3.75 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} / 10^{3} \text{ mm} = 3.75 \text{ N/mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Equivalent Span Between Lift Points

The two lift inserts are at **750 mm from each end**, so the **cantilever overhang** beyond each insert is:

$$
L_{overhang} = 750 \text{ mm}
$$

The total panel length minus the two overhangs gives the **span between the two inserts**:

$$
L_{span} = 3000 - 2 \times 750 = 1500 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Critical Section

For a panel with **two top inserts**, the panel behaves as a **simply supported beam between the two lift points** with **two cantilever overhangs** on either side.

The **maximum bending moment** occurs at the **lift-point sections** (not at mid-span) because the cantilever portion exerts a hogging moment at the lift inserts.

### Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Bending Moment at the Lift Point

For the cantilever overhang of length $L_{overhang}$:

$$
M_{overhang} = \frac{w L_{overhang}^{2}}{2}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
M_{overhang} = \frac{3.75 \times 750^{2}}{2} = \frac{3.75 \times 562500}{2} = 1.0547 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

This is a **hogging (negative) moment** at the lift insert. The mid-span moment is much smaller for a panel where the inserts are near the ends.

### Step 5 — Compute the Section Modulus (Z) of the Panel

For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and thickness $t$:

$$
Z = \frac{b t^{2}}{6}
$$

Here, $b = 1000 \text{ mm}$ (we analyse a 1-m wide strip), and $t = 150 \text{ mm}$:

$$
Z = \frac{1000 \times 150^{2}}{6} = \frac{1000 \times 22500}{6} = 3.75 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Lifting Stress

$$
\sigma_{lift} = \frac{M_{overhang}}{Z} = \frac{1.0547 \times 10^{6}}{3.75 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{lift} = 0.281 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}
$$

### Step 7 — Verify Code Compliance

The KTU / IS 4990:2011 limit for safe lifting is:

$$
\sigma_{allowable} = 0.4 f_{ck} = 0.4 \times 20 = 8.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

Comparison:

$$
\sigma_{lift} = 0.281 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \quad \ll \quad \sigma_{allowable} = 8.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

**Result: The panel is SAFE for lifting at 1-day strength.** The factor of safety is:

$$
FoS = \frac{8.0}{0.281} \approx 28.5
$$

This high margin is typical for thin wall panels lifted with two top inserts placed near the ends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In a 7-mark sub-question, you will be marked on (a) drawing the bending-moment diagram and (b) identifying the **critical section as the lift-insert location, NOT the mid-span**. Many students lose 2 marks by computing the moment at mid-span instead of at the insert.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Formwork Reuse Break-Even Analysis

> **Problem Setup:**
> An aluminium (Mivan) formwork system costs **₹18,00,000 for a 100 m² wall area**. The conventional timber/plywood alternative costs **₹5,00,000 but requires plastering at ₹250/m²**. The Mivan system produces a wall that **does not require plastering**. If the project has **120 floors (repetitions)**, determine the **break-even number of cycles** for Mivan vs conventional.

### Step 1 — Define the Per-Cycle Cost of Conventional System

Conventional system cost includes:
- Formwork amortised: $\frac{5,00,000}{10 \text{ cycles}} = 50,000$ per cycle
- Plastering cost: $100 \text{ m}^{2} \times 250 = 25,000$ per cycle
- Total: $50,000 + 25,000 = 75,000$ per cycle

> *(We assume only 10 reuse cycles for timber formwork before it deforms.)*

### Step 2 — Define the Per-Cycle Cost of Mivan System (Variable)

Let $n$ be the number of reuse cycles. Mivan per-cycle cost:

$$
C_{Mivan}(n) = \frac{18,00,000}{n} + 0 \quad \text{(no plastering)}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate to Find Break-Even

$$
C_{Mivan}(n) = C_{conventional}
$$

$$
\frac{18,00,000}{n} = 75,000
$$

$$
n = \frac{18,00,000}{75,000} = 24 \text{ cycles}
$$

> **Result: After 24 cycles, Mivan becomes cheaper than conventional.**

### Step 4 — Verify Against the 120-Floor Project

For $n = 120$ cycles:

$$
C_{Mivan}(120) = \frac{18,00,000}{120} = 15,000 \text{ per cycle}
$$

> **Savings per cycle** = $75,000 - 15,000 = 60,000$
> **Total project savings** = $60,000 \times 120 = 72,00,000$

Hence, Mivan is **massively profitable** for repetitive high-rise projects beyond ~25 floors.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rapid Wall vs Conventional Wall

This is the single most important comparison that a KTU 14-mark question demands. Memorise this in tabular form:

| Comparison Axis | Conventional Brick / RCC Infill | Mivan / Aluminium Formwork | Precast Large Panel | Tunnel Formwork |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cycle time / floor** | 12–14 days | 2.5–3 days | 1.5–2 days | 1–1.5 days |
| **Formwork cycles** | 5–10 (timber) | 200–250 (aluminium) | 50–80 (steel mould) | 400–500 (steel) |
| **Plaster needed?** | Yes, both sides | No (smooth finish) | No (factory finish) | No |
| **Wall thickness** | 230 mm (brick) + plaster | 100–150 mm | 120–200 mm | 100–150 mm |
| **Concrete grade** | M-20 to M-25 | M-25 to M-40 | M-40 to M-60 | M-30 to M-50 |
| **Labour on site** | 100 % (relative) | 30–40 % | 20–30 % | 20–25 % |
| **Wastage** | 8–12 % | 2–3 % | 1–2 % | 1–2 % |
| **Initial cost** | Low | **High** | **High** | **Very High** |
| **Seismic performance** | Moderate (weak joints) | **Excellent (monolithic)** | Good (joints critical) | **Excellent (monolithic)** |
| **Best project type** | Custom villas | G+20 to G+40 mass housing | Mass housing (clusters) | Repetitive cell-form (hostels, hotels) |
