   - **Stair Flights & Landings.**

3. **Volumetric Modules (3D):** Complete factory-finished rooms (toilet pods, kitchen units, lift cores) — plug-and-play concept.

**Manufacturing → Transportation → Erection Sequence (3 Marks):**

1. **Manufacturing:** Reinforcement cage prepared → Mould cleaned and oiled → Cage placed with cover blocks → Concrete poured in mould → Needle vibrated → Steam-cured (60–80 °C, 4–6 hrs) → Demoulded at 70% strength → Quality inspection → Stack-stored with timber wedges.

2. **Transportation:** Lifted by gantry crane → Loaded on **low-bed trailers** with timber cradles (angle ≥ 30° for slabs) → Speed limited to 25 km/hr → IS 15916 tolerance band maintained.

3. **Erection:** Site level-checked → Crane positioned → Lifter hooks engaged → Slings at correct angle (60° minimum) → Element lowered onto pre-aligned dowels/inserts → Temporary props installed → Connections grouted/welded → Joint sealing.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** Slab size = 4.0 m × 0.6 m × 0.15 m; Lifts at 0.8 m from each end (central span between lifts = 2.4 m); $f_{ck}$ = 30 N/mm²; $\gamma_c$ = 25 kN/m³; Impact Factor = 1.5; Self-weight factor = 1.25.

**Step 1 — Self-weight per metre length.**

$$w = 0.6 \times 0.15 \times 25 = 2.25 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Factored uniformly distributed load (with both impact and self-weight factors).**

$$w_u = 1.25 \times 1.5 \times 2.25 = 4.219 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending moment at mid-span (simply supported with overhangs).**

Span between lifters $L_c = 2.4$ m. Overhangs on each side = 0.8 m.

Mid-span moment (simply supported between lifts):

$$M_{mid} = \frac{w_u \times L_c^2}{8} = \frac{4.219 \times 2.4^2}{8}$$

$$M_{mid} = \frac{4.219 \times 5.76}{8} = \frac{24.30}{8} = 3.038 \text{ kN·m}$$

Cantilever moment at lifter position (overhang of 0.8 m):

$$M_{cant} = \frac{w_u \times 0.8^2}{2} = \frac{4.219 \times 0.64}{2} = 1.350 \text{ kN·m}$$

**→ Maximum bending moment = 3.038 kN·m (at mid-span).**

**Step 4 — Vertical reaction at each lifter.**

$$R = \frac{w_u \times L_{total}}{2} = \frac{4.219 \times 4.0}{2}$$

$$R = \frac{16.876}{2} = 8.438 \text{ kN}$$

**→ Each lifter carries 8.44 kN.**

**Step 5 — Verification — design a 15 kN-rated threaded socket (1.5× safety factor).**

Selected capacity = 1.5 × 8.44 = 12.66 kN < 15 kN. **→ SAFE.**

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Internal Choice]`**

**Q: (a)** What are the **different types of connections** used in prefabricated concrete structures? Explain the **emulated cast-in-situ (CIC) connection** and the **grouted duct splice** with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A precast column of size **450 mm × 450 mm** is to be connected to its foundation pedestal using a **grouted duct splice with 4 nos. of 20 mm diameter HYSD bars**. Calculate the **development length** required for full anchorage of the column bars into the foundation, given:
   - $f_y = 500$ N/mm² (Fe500)
   - $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm² (M30 concrete)
   - $\tau_{bd} = 1.5 \times 0.80 = 1.20$ N/mm² (per IS 456 Cl. 34.2.4.2 for M30) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Connection Types (7 Marks)

**Major Connection Types (3 Marks):**

1. **Beam-to-Column Connections:** Welded steel plates, bolted cleats, emulated CIC (corbel-less), corbel haunches.

2. **Column-to-Foundation:** Socket connection, bolted base plate, grouted splice with starter bars.

3. **Wall-to-Floor and Floor-to-Floor:** Grouted key joints, welded plate, horizontal loop-bar splices.

4. **Diaphragm Connections:** Tie-bars in topping concrete ensuring lateral load transfer.

**Emulated CIC Connection (2 Marks):**

A connection that **replicates the behaviour of a monolithic cast-in-situ joint** through the use of:
- Continuous longitudinal bars passing through the joint region (via grouted couplers or ducts).
- Closely spaced stirrups in the joint core (joint shear capacity = $\dfrac{f_{ck}^{0.5}}{1.5} A_j$).
- Strong-column-weak-beam philosophy.
- In-situ concrete/concrete-grout in the joint core to ensure monolithicity.

**Grouted Duct Splice (2 Marks):**

- Column bars from foundation pedestal protrude into **corrugated metal/HDPE ducts** embedded in the precast column base.
- Ducts are filled with **high-strength, non-shrink cementitious grout** (compressive strength ≥ 50 N/mm²).
- The grout bonds to bar and duct wall by **chemical adhesion + mechanical interlock** (corrugations).
- Provides full tension and compression continuity.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\phi = 20$ mm, $f_y = 500$ N/mm², $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $\tau_{bd} = 1.20$ N/mm² (bond stress for M30, Fe500, deformed bar — IS 456 Cl. 34.2.4.2).

**Step 1 — Standard development length formula (IS 456 Cl. 34.2).**

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times f_y \times \phi}{4 \times \tau_{bd}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 20}{4 \times 1.20}$$

$$L_d = \frac{8700}{4.80} = 1812.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply modification factors (IS 456 Cl. 34.2.4.3).**

For Fe500 with $\phi > 20$ mm: factor = 1.10; but our bar is exactly 20 mm, so factor = 1.0.

For lightweight concrete: not applicable.

For bars in compression: $L_d$ may be reduced (not relevant here — bar is in tension at joint).

**→ Final development length = 1812.5 mm ≈ 1813 mm (1.81 m).**

**Step 4 — Practical recommendation for duct length.**

Provide duct length = $L_d + 50$ mm (for seating tolerance) = **1862.5 mm**, i.e., **1900 mm** (1.9 m) of corrugated HDPE duct, with 50 mm cover at top and bottom.

**Step 5 — Verify number of bars & duct diameter.**

For 4 nos. of 20 mm bars, minimum duct internal diameter (with 5 mm grout annulus):

$$D_{duct} = \phi_{bar} + 2 \times 5 = 20 + 10 = 30 \text{ mm (per bar)}$$

For cluster of 4 bars in single annular duct (rare), use 100 mm dia. corrugated duct. **For 4 individual bars → use 4 × 32 mm dia. ducts.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Prep:**

- ✅ **Prefabrication = Factory + Site**; in-situ = Site only.
- ✅ **Governing IS Codes (must memorize):** IS 456 (in-situ), **IS 15916 (precast design)**, **IS 11447 (lifting inserts)**, IS 10262 (mix design), IS 12118 (modular co-ordination), IS 1343 (prestressed precast).
- ✅ **Lifting load formula:** $P_d = 1.25 \times (DL + IL) \times \phi_{impact}$ where $\phi_{impact} = 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- ✅ **Tolerance band:** $\pm 3$ mm to $\pm 6$ mm (IS 15916). Don't write "±5 mm" — examiners want the range.
- ✅ **Connection Philosophy:** Capacity Design → Joints must be **20–30% stronger** than members.
- ✅ **Demoulding vs. In-Service:** Precast beams are often **designed for the demoulding stage**, NOT the in-service stage.
- ✅ **Common Prefab Elements in India:** Hollow-core slabs, solid slabs, beams, columns, sandwich wall panels, stair flights, toilet pods.
- ✅ **PMAY Mandate:** ≥ 25% IBS in affordable housing.
- ✅ **Key Bond Stress for M30 + Fe500:** $\tau_{bd} = 1.20$ N/mm² (IS 456 Table in Cl. 34.2.4.2).
- ✅ **Grouted splice grout strength:** ≥ 50 N/mm² (typical), non-shrink type.
- ✅ **Critical Pitfall:** Distinguish *emulated CIC* (joint looks like in-situ) from *dry connection* (welded/bolted, no monolithicity). Examiners LOVE this distinction.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Engineered Buildings: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-Engineered Buildings: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Engineered Building (PEB):** A pre-engineered building is a **factory-fabricated, pre-designed steel building system** in which the structural members (primary frames, secondary members, and cladding) are **standardized and engineered at the manufacturing facility** using tapered, built-up plate sections, and then **assembled on-site using bolted connections**. The design is optimized through computer-aided analysis to deliver the **most economical structural profile** for a given set of loads and clear spans.

According to the **Metal Building Manufacturers Association (MBMA)** and the **American Institute of Steel Construction (AISC)** guidelines, PEBs are essentially **fully customizable** in layout but **standardized in components**, allowing mass production and rapid deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET522 – Module 4):** The introduction to PEB forms the foundation for understanding modern industrial construction. Students must clearly differentiate between *Conventional Steel Buildings (CSB)* and *Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB)*, identify standard components, and articulate the engineering rationale behind tapered built-up sections.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flat-Pack Steel Building"

Imagine you order a piece of **flat-pack furniture** (the kind that comes in numbered boxes from a warehouse store). Every screw, every wooden panel, every fitting has been:

- **Pre-cut** to exact size at the factory
- **Pre-engineered** so that each piece only fits one way
- **Numbered and packed** so the assembly is foolproof
- **Designed using computer software** for maximum material efficiency

Now, scale that idea up **thousands of times** and replace the wood with **high-strength steel plates**, the screws with **high-tensile bolts**, and the assembly manual with **erection drawings**. That, in essence, is a **Pre-Engineered Building**.

| Feature | Flat-Pack Furniture | Pre-Engineered Building |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-cut parts | Wooden panels | Tapered steel I-sections |
| Pre-drilled holes | Dowel holes | Bolt holes |
| Numbered parts | A1, B2, C7 | Member tags on drawings |
| Tools provided | Allen key | Standard erection tools |
| Assembly time | Few hours | Few weeks |
| Engineer | Furniture designer | Structural engineer (PEB software) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** PEB is to conventional steel buildings what a **modern smartphone** is to an old landline telephone. Both achieve the same goal (communication/structural enclosure), but the smartphone is *integrated, optimized, lighter, and faster to deploy* — exactly like a PEB.

---

### 1.3 Historical Context and Evolution

The concept of pre-engineering emerged in the **1960s** in the United States, when leading steel manufacturers like **Butler Manufacturing**, **Nucor Building Systems**, and **Kirby Building Systems** began to develop standardized steel building solutions to meet the growing demand for **industrial warehouses, factories, and aircraft hangars**.

The breakthrough came with the **tapered web section** — a built-up plate girder whose web depth varies linearly from the haunch (column) to the apex (ridge). This single innovation reduced steel consumption by **20–30%** compared to uniform-depth hot-rolled sections, making PEBs commercially viable on a global scale.

**Key Milestones in PEB History:**

| Year | Development |
|---|---|
| **1960s** | First standardized steel buildings introduced in the USA |
| **1970s** | Computer-aided design (CAD) adopted; tapered sections become standard |
| **1980s** | PEB technology spreads to Europe, Middle East, and Asia |
| **1990s** | Introduction of high-strength steel (yield strength ≥ **350 MPa**) |
| **2000s** | 3D modelling, BIM integration, and automated CNC fabrication |
| **2010s–Present** | LEED-certified PEBs, solar-ready roofs, and hybrid PEB-concrete systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relevance to Kerala Construction Industry:** PEB technology has become particularly significant in Kerala for **post-flood reconstruction (2018, 2019)**, industrial sheds in **Kochi and Palakkad**, and **warehouse projects** for the logistics boom around the Cochin International Airport corridor.

---

### 1.4 Standard Building Parameters in PEB

A typical PEB project is defined by **four primary geometric parameters**:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Building Width (Span)** | $W$ | **20 m to 80 m** | Clear horizontal distance between outer column centerlines |
| **Building Length** | $L$ | **30 m to 200 m** | Total longitudinal extent, divided into multiple bays |
| **Eave Height** | $H_e$ | **6 m to 12 m** | Height from floor to the top of the side wall |
| **Roof Slope** | $S$ | **1:10 to 1:20** (≈ 5% to 10%) | Slope of the rafter, important for drainage |

**Bay Spacing:** The longitudinal length is divided into **equal bays** (typically **6 m to 9 m**), each bay being a structural repetition of purlins, girts, and frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Slope Rule (MBMA Recommendation):** The most common PEB roof slope is **1:20 (5%)** for gravity-loaded (rain) conditions. In **snow-prone regions**, a steeper slope of **1:10 to 1:12** is recommended to prevent snow accumulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Engineering Philosophy of PEB

The defining design philosophy of a PEB can be summarized in **five logical pillars**:

1. **Optimization over Standardization**
   Every member is sized to carry the **exact loads** acting on it — no over-design. A rafter carries more bending moment at the haunch than at the ridge, so its depth is *tapered* to match the moment diagram.

2. **Tapered Built-Up Sections**
   The web plate is **sheared to a trapezoidal shape** and welded to top and bottom flanges. The depth varies linearly, mimicking the bending moment distribution.

3. **Standardized Connections**
   All connections are **bolted** (not welded on-site) to enable fast assembly, easier quality control, and the possibility of dismantling and relocation.

4. **Modular Repetition**
   The building is composed of **repeated structural units** (frames, bays), allowing mass production, lower cost per kg, and faster erection.

5. **Integrated Building Envelope**
   The steel skeleton, roof sheeting, wall cladding, insulation, skylights, ventilators, doors, and windows are **designed and supplied as a single system** by the PEB manufacturer — unlike CSBs, where multiple vendors handle different trades.

---

### 2.2 Comparison: Pre-Engineered Building (PEB) vs. Conventional Steel Building (CSB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Frequently Asked KTU Question:** *"Compare PEB and CSB."* — Memorize this comparison thoroughly.

| S. No. | Parameter | Conventional Steel Building (CSB) | Pre-Engineered Building (PEB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design Approach** | Member-by-member design, often conservative | Computer-optimized, least-weight design |
| 2 | **Structural Sections** | Standard hot-rolled sections (I-beams, channels) | Tapered built-up plate sections |
| 3 | **Steel Weight** | Heavier (baseline) | **20–30% lighter** than CSB |
| 4 | **Construction Speed** | Slow (6–12 months typical) | **Very fast (6–12 weeks)** |
| 5 | **Connections** | Mostly on-site welding + bolting | **100% bolted** at site |
| 6 | **Cost** | Higher (labour + material) | **30–40% more economical** |
| 7 | **Clear Span Capability** | Limited to ~30 m without trusses | Up to **80 m clear span** without internal columns |
| 8 | **Architectural Flexibility** | More flexible, custom shapes | Less flexible, restricted to standard profiles |
| 9 | **Quality Control** | Difficult on site | **Factory-controlled, superior quality** |
| 10 | **Seismic Performance** | Good with proper detailing | Excellent (lighter mass = lower seismic forces) |
| 11 | **Future Expansion** | Difficult to modify | **Easy to extend** by adding more bays |
| 12 | **Suitability** | High-rise, complex geometry, multi-storey | Industrial sheds, warehouses, hangars, low-rise |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, direct comparison questions (often 7-mark sub-parts) appear in nearly every ESE. Always include **at least 8–10 comparison points** with quantified values where possible.

---

### 2.3 Major Components of a Pre-Engineered Building

A PEB is an **integrated system** comprising the following sub-systems:

#### A. Primary Structural Members (Load-Bearing)

| Component | Function | Typical Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Frames (Rigid Frames)** | Resist vertical and lateral loads; transfer to foundation | Tapered I-section with haunches |
| **Columns** | Support rafters, transfer vertical loads to base plate | Tapered I-section (depth increases upward) |
| **Rafters** | Span the roof, transfer loads to columns | Tapered I-section (depth increases toward haunch) |
| **End Wall Frames** | Support sheeting on gable end | Tapered or uniform I-section |

#### B. Secondary Structural Members (Bracing & Purlins)

| Component | Function | Typical Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Purlins** | Support roof sheeting, transfer load to rafters | Cold-formed Z-sections (typically Z150–Z250) |
| **Girts** | Support wall sheeting, transfer load to columns | Cold-formed Z-sections (typically Z150–Z200) |
| **Eave Struts** | Connect roof and wall planes at the eave | Cold-formed C/Z sections |
| **Bracing (Rod/Cable)** | Resist longitudinal wind/seismic loads | Round bars, angle sections |
| **Portal Bracing** | Resist lateral loads in transverse direction | Rigid frame action |

#### C. Roof and Wall Sheeting (Cladding)

| Component | Material | Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Roof Sheeting** | Galvanized steel / Galvalume | Trapezoidal rib (0.47–0.63 mm thick) |
| **Wall Sheeting** | Galvanized steel / Al-Zn coated | Trapezoidal or sinusoidal (0.47–0.63 mm) |
| **Insulation** | Glass wool / rock wool | 50–100 mm thick |
| **Flashings & Trims** | Same material as sheeting | For waterproofing joints |

#### D. Accessories

- **Skylights** (FRP or polycarbonate sheets)
- **Ridge ventilators** (natural ventilation)
- **Turbo ventilators**
- **Roller shutters / sliding doors**
- **Windows, louvers, doors**
- **Mezzanine floors** (for office space within shed)
- **Crane beams** (for EOT/gantry cranes)

---

### 2.4 Why Tapered Sections? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept for KTU:** The reason PEBs are so efficient is the use of **tapered built-up sections**, where the web depth is varied along the member length to match the **bending moment diagram (BMD)**.

In a simply supported beam under uniform load, the bending moment is **maximum at mid-span** and **zero at the supports**. Therefore, a uniform-depth beam is **inefficient** because the section is over-designed near the supports.

In a PEB rafter (or portal frame), the situation is reversed for the gravity load case — the moment is **maximum at the haunch (column-beam junction)** and **minimum at the ridge (apex)**. Hence, the rafter depth is:

- **Minimum at the apex** (small moment) → lower web depth $d_{min}$
- **Maximum at the haunch** (large moment) → higher web depth $d_{max}$

The web is **sheared linearly** from $d_{max}$ at the column face to $d_{min}$ at the ridge, creating a **trapezoidal I-section** (or H-section with variable depth).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas, conversions, and limits are essential for KTU ESE numerical and design questions on PEB.

| S. No. | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weight Saving Percentage** | $W_s = \dfrac{W_{CSB} - W_{PEB}}{W_{CSB}} \times 100$ | $W_s$ in **%**; typical: **20–30%** |
| 2 | **Bending Moment (Rafter, simply supported, UDL)** | $M = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | $w$ in kN/m, $L$ in m, $M$ in kN·m |
| 3 | **Section Modulus Requirement** | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}$ | $f_y$ = yield strength, $\gamma_{m0}$ = partial safety factor (1.10) |
| 4 | **Required Web Depth (Plastic Design)** | $d_{req} = \dfrac{1.1 \cdot Z_{req}}{t_w}$ (approx) | $t_w$ = web thickness in mm |
| 5 | **Taper Slope** | $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{d_{max} - d_{min}}{L_{rafter}}$ | $\theta$ in degrees, often 1:10 to 1:20 |
| 6 | **Standard Roof Slope** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Run}}$ | e.g., **1:20** means 50 mm rise per 1000 mm run |
| 7 | **Bay Spacing Range** | $6 \leq B \leq 9$ m | Most economical at **7.5 m** |
| 8 | **Eave Height Range** | $6 \leq H_e \leq 12$ m | Standard increments: **0.5 m** |
| 9 | **Clear Span Range** | $20 \leq W \leq 80$ m | Single-span PEB; multi-span for larger widths |
| 10 | **Wind Pressure (IS 875 Part 3)** | $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_b^2$ | $V_b$ in m/s, $p_z$ in N/m² (basic wind pressure at 10 m height) |
| 11 | **Design Wind Speed** | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | $k_1$ = risk, $k_2$ = terrain, $k_3$ = topography, $k_4$ = importance |
| 12 | **Live Load on Roof (IS 875 Part 2)** | $LL = 0.75$ kN/m² | For slopes **≤ 10°**; reduced for steeper slopes |
| 13 | **Dead Load (Sheeting + Purlins + Insulation)** | $DL \approx 0.15$ to $0.25$ kN/m² | Approximate, depends on profile |
| 14 | **Snow Load (IS 875 Part 4)** | $S = \mu \cdot S_0$ | $\mu$ = shape coefficient, $S_0$ = ground snow load |
| 15 | **Steel Density** | $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³ | Used in self-weight calculations |
| 16 | **Maximum Permissible Deflection (Rafter)** | $\delta_{max} = L / 180$ to $L / 360$ | $L$ = span; depends on cladding type |
| 17 | **Maximum Permissible Horizontal Drift** | $\Delta_{max} = H / 150$ to $H / 250$ | $H$ = eave height |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse the **roof slope angle** ($\theta$ in degrees) with the **taper angle of the rafter web**. These are two distinct parameters! The roof slope governs water drainage, while the rafter taper governs structural efficiency.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Specific Use |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Construction** | Manufacturing plants, assembly shops, process industries |
| **Warehousing & Logistics** | Cold storage, distribution centres, bonded warehouses |
| **Aviation** | Aircraft hangars (large clear spans up to **100 m**) |
| **Commercial** | Showrooms, supermarkets, hypermarkets (Big Bazaar, Lulu Mall) |
| **Sports & Recreation** | Indoor stadiums, gymnasiums, swimming pools |
| **Agriculture** | Grain storage, dairy farms, greenhouses |
| **Disaster Relief** | Rapid-deployment shelters (post-flood Kerala 2018–19) |
| **Renewable Energy** | Solar panel mounting structures on PEB roofs |
| **Defence** | Vehicle sheds, ammunition storage, military barracks |
| **Healthcare** | Modular hospitals, COVID-19 isolation wards |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantifying the Steel Weight Saving in PEB

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** To derive the percentage weight saving achieved by using tapered built-up PEB rafters compared to conventional uniform-depth I-sections.

**Given:**
- Simply supported rafter, span $L$, uniform gravity load $w$ (kN/m)
- Uniform-depth section (CSB): constant depth $d_u$, flange area $A_f$, web area $A_w$
- Tapered section (PEB): depth varies from $d_{min}$ at mid-span to $d_{max}$ at supports

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment (CSB, Uniform Section):**

For a simply supported beam under UDL:

$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Required Section Modulus (CSB):**

Using the elastic design criterion (working stress method):

$$Z_{req,CSB} = \frac{M_{max}}{f_b}$$

where $f_b$ = allowable bending stress in steel (≈ **0.66 $f_y$** for working stress method).

**Step 3 — Required Section Modulus (PEB, Tapered):**

The tapered rafter is designed as a **variable-depth cantilever-like section**, where the depth at the haunch is governed by the **negative bending moment** at the column-rafter joint (in a portal frame). For a portal frame, the moment at the knee is approximately:

$$M_{knee} \approx \frac{wL^2}{10} \text{ to } \frac{wL^2}{12}$$

The smaller required moment at the ridge is:

$$M_{ridge} \approx \frac{wL^2}{40} \text{ to } \frac{wL^2}{60}$$

The **average required section modulus** along the rafter is:

$$Z_{avg,PEB} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L Z(x) \, dx$$

For a linearly tapered section, $Z(x)$ varies linearly with depth, so:

$$Z_{avg,PEB} = \frac{Z_{max} + Z_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Average Section Depth:**

The average web depth of the tapered section:

$$d_{avg,PEB} = \frac{d_{max} + d_{min}}{2}$$

Typical values: $d_{max} \approx 1200$ mm, $d_{min} \approx 400$ mm

$$d_{avg,PEB} = \frac{1200 + 400}{2} = 800 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Uniform Section Depth Required:**

The uniform section must be designed for the maximum moment throughout, hence:

$$d_{u,CSB} = d_{max} = 1200 \text{ mm (approximately)}$$

**Step 6 — Weight Comparison (per metre run of rafter):**

Assuming same flange area $A_f$, same flange thickness $t_f$, and same web thickness $t_w$:

Weight of CSB rafter (per metre):

$$w_{CSB} = \rho_{steel} \cdot (2 A_f + d_u \cdot t_w)$$

Weight of PEB rafter (per metre, average):

$$w_{PEB} = \rho_{steel} \cdot (2 A_f + d_{avg} \cdot t_w)$$

**Step 7 — Percentage Weight Saving:**

$$W_s = \frac{w_{CSB} - w_{PEB}}{w_{CSB}} \times 100$$

Substituting:

$$W_s = \frac{(2 A_f + d_u \cdot t_w) - (2 A_f + d_{avg} \cdot t_w)}{2 A_f + d_u \cdot t_w} \times 100$$

The flange terms cancel (same flanges at supports and apex):

$$W_s = \frac{(d_u - d_{avg}) \cdot t_w}{2 A_f + d_u \cdot t_w} \times 100$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Example:**

Let $A_f = 3000$ mm² (flange area = 200 mm × 15 mm), $t_w = 6$ mm, $d_u = 1200$ mm, $d_{avg} = 800$ mm.

Numerator:

$$(1200 - 800) \times 6 = 400 \times 6 = 2400 \text{ mm}^3$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 3000 + 1200 \times 6 = 6000 + 7200 = 13200 \text{ mm}^3$$

Weight saving:

$$W_s = \frac{2400}{13200} \times 100 = 18.18\%$$

Including the **higher grade of steel (Grade 350 MPa vs. Grade 250 MPa)** in PEBs and **reduced bracing & secondary member sizes** (because the lighter dead load demands smaller purlins, girts, and foundations), the **overall weight saving** typically escalates to:

$$\boxed{W_{s,overall} = 20\% \text{ to } 30\%}$$

This **20–30% weight saving** is the **primary economic driver** behind the global adoption of PEB technology.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Framework — When to Use PEB vs. CSB

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix to answer KTU application-based and case-study questions.**

| Decision Criterion | Choose PEB ✅ | Choose CSB ✅ |
|---|---|---|
| **Clear span requirement** | ≥ 30 m (no internal columns) | ≤ 25 m |
| **Project timeline** | Tight schedule (≤ 6 months) | Flexible timeline |
| **Building height** | Low-rise (≤ 12 m eave) | Multi-storey |
| **Building type** | Standard industrial shed, warehouse | Custom architectural design |
| **Future expansion** | Likely (easy to extend bays) | Unlikely |
| **Budget** | Limited, cost-sensitive | Higher budget available |
| **Architectural complexity** | Simple gable, lean-to, multi-span | Complex curves, cantilevers, transfer structures |
| **Crane integration** | Up to **30 T** EOT cranes | Heavy industrial cranes (> 30 T) |
| **Seismic zone** | Zone II, III, IV, V (lighter mass helps) | All zones; CSB also good with detailing |
| **Wind zone (Kerala)** | Coastal regions: **Zone V (Vb = 50 m/s)** | Both viable |
| **Relocation requirement** | Yes (fully bolted, demountable) | No (welded, permanent) |
| **Thermal/acoustic performance** | Required (insulated sandwich panels available) | Custom solutions |

> **Sample Project:** 30 m span × 60 m length × 8 m eave height industrial shed, located in Ernakulam district, Kerala.

**Assumptions:**
- Conventional Steel Building (CSB): Rolled steel sections (ISMB, ISMC), on-site welding, Grade 250 MPa steel
- Pre-Engineered Building (PEB): Tapered built-up sections, factory-bolted, Grade 350 MPa steel

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Structural Steel Supply & Fabrication | 45.00 | 32.00 | 28.9% |
| 2 | Foundation (lighter loads in PEB) | 12.00 | 9.50 | 20.8% |
| 3 | Roof & Wall Sheeting | 18.00 | 15.00 | 16.7% |
| 4 | Erection & Labour | 10.00 | 4.50 | 55.0% |
| 5 | Project Management & Overheads | 6.00 | 3.50 | 41.7% |
| 6 | **Total Project Cost** | **91.00** | **64.50** | **29.1%** |

**Observation:** PEB delivers a **~29% overall cost saving** on this typical project, primarily due to **faster erection**, **lighter foundations**, and **optimized material usage**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Span-to-Depth Ratio for PEB Rafters

The **span-to-depth ratio** of a rafter is a key design parameter. For a portal frame with rigid joints, the **knee moment** is critical.

For a single-bay, single-span portal frame with pinned bases under UDL $w$:

$$M_{knee} = \frac{wL^2}{2\left(2 + \frac{I_c}{I_r} \cdot \frac{L}{H} \cdot \frac{6K_r - 1}{6K_c - 1}\right)}$$

For a **proportionate frame** where $I_r / I_c \cdot L / H \approx 2$ and $K_r = K_c = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$M_{knee} \approx \frac{wL^2}{10}$$

**Required section modulus at the knee:**

$$Z_{knee} = \frac{M_{knee}}{f_b} = \frac{wL^2}{10 f_b}$$

**For $L = 30$ m, $w = 5$ kN/m, $f_b = 165$ MPa:**

$$M_{knee} = \frac{5 \times 30^2}{10} = 450 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$Z_{knee} = \frac{450 \times 10^6}{165} = 2.727 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Required web depth** (assuming $t_w = 6$ mm, $b_f = 200$ mm, $t_f = 12$ mm):

Using the plastic section modulus approximation $Z_p \approx 1.1 \cdot Z_e$:

$$Z_{p,req} = 1.1 \times 2.727 \times 10^6 = 3.00 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

For an I-section: $Z_p \approx t_w \cdot d^2 / 4 + 2 b_f \cdot t_f \cdot (d/2 + t_f/2)$

Solving iteratively (or using design charts):

$$\boxed{d_{max} \approx 1100 \text{ to } 1200 \text{ mm at the knee}}$$

This matches the **typical PEB rafter haunch depth of 1000–1500 mm** used in industry for 30 m spans.

### 4.3 Standard Cross-Sectional Profile of a Tapered PEB Rafter

```
          ←——  d_min  (at ridge, e.g., 400 mm)  ——→
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │═════════════════════════════════════════════│  ← Top flange (200 × 12 mm)
    └┬───────────────────────────────────────────┬┘
     │  ╲                                     ╱  │   
     │    ╲    TAPERED WEB (trapezoidal)     ╱   │   ← Web (6 mm thick)
     │      ╲                             ╱     │
     │        ╲                         ╱       │   Length ≈ 15 m (half-span)
     │          ╲                     ╱         │
     │            ╲                 ╱           │
     │              ╲             ╱             │
     │                ╲         ╱               │
     │                  ╲     ╱                 │
     │                    ╲ ╱                   │   d_max at haunch ≈ 1200 mm
    ┌┴───────────────────────────────────────────┴┐
    │═════════════════════════════════════════════│  ← Bottom flange (200 × 12 mm)
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Observations from the Profile:**
- The **web is sheared to a trapezoidal shape** (not constant depth)
- The **top and bottom flanges are uniform width and thickness** (for ease of fabrication)
- The **inclination angle** of the web edge is typically **5° to 10°** from vertical
- The **web height varies** from $d_{min}$ at the apex to $d_{max}$ at the haunch
- **Stiffener plates** are welded at the haunch and at points of concentrated load application (e.g., purlin connections, bracing points)

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: Define a Pre-Engineered Building (PEB).**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pre-Engineered Building (PEB)** is a factory-fabricated steel building system in which the structural members (primary frames, secondary members, and sheeting) are **pre-designed and manufactured at the factory** using **tapered built-up plate sections** of standard hot-rolled steel plates. The components are then **assembled at the site using bolted connections**, ensuring rapid construction, optimal material usage, and superior quality control compared to conventional steel buildings. *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1 for example, 0.5 for distinction from CSB)*

---

#### **Question 2: List any SIX advantages of Pre-Engineered Buildings over Conventional Steel Buildings.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Advantage of PEB |
|---|---|
| 1 | **20–30% lighter** steel structure due to optimized tapered sections |
| 2 | **Faster construction** — project completion in 6–12 weeks (vs. 6–12 months for CSB) |
| 3 | **30–40% cost saving** overall, including foundations, erection, and overheads |
| 4 | **Superior quality control** due to factory-fabrication under controlled conditions |
| 5 | **Long clear spans** up to 80 m **without internal columns** |
| 6 | **Easy future expansion** by adding more bays; fully demountable and relocatable |
| 7 | **Bolted connections** at site eliminate on-site welding and associated fire hazards |

*(3 marks: 0.5 × 6 = 3 for listing six distinct advantages)*

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** (internal choice). Each sub-part should be answered in **150–200 words** with a clear diagram where applicable.

---

#### **Question A: Detailed Discussion on Pre-Engineered Buildings**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **components of a Pre-Engineered Building (PEB)** with a neat labelled sketch. Describe the **function of each component** and the **typical section used**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **step-by-step design philosophy** of a PEB, including the rationale for using **tapered built-up sections**, and explain how PEB achieves **20–30% weight saving** over CSB with a numerical example.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

A PEB comprises **four major sub-systems**:

**1. Primary Structural System:**
- **Main Rigid Frames** — composed of **tapered columns** and **tapered rafters**, connected by **rigid knee joints** and **apex/ridge connections**.
- **End-Wall Frames** — tapered I-sections supporting the gable wall cladding.
- **Section Type:** Built-up I-section (or H-section) with web plate (6–10 mm), flanges (8–16 mm), depth varying from **400 mm at ridge to 1200 mm at haunch**.

**2. Secondary Structural System:**
- **Purlins** (roof) and **Girts** (walls) — cold-formed **Z-sections** (Z150 to Z250), spaced at **1.2 m to 1.8 m** centre-to-centre.
- **Eave Struts** — cold-formed C/Z sections connecting roof and wall planes.
- **Bracing** — round rods or angle sections in roof and walls to resist longitudinal forces.

**3. Cladding System:**
- **Roof sheeting** — trapezoidal Galvalume/galvanized steel sheets (0.47–0.63 mm thick), often with **standing seam profiles** for weather tightness.
- **Wall sheeting** — similar trapezoidal or sinusoidal profiles.
- **Insulation** — glass wool or rock wool (50–100 mm) sandwiched between two sheets.

**4. Accessories:**
- **Skylights, ridge ventilators, turbo ventilators, roller shutters, doors, windows, louvers, and crane beams** (for industrial use).

**Sketch (Schematic):**
```
         /\
        /  \  ← Rafter (tapered)
       /    \
      /______\ ← Knee joint (rigid)
      |      |
      |      |  ← Column (tapered)
      |      |
      ┴──────┴  ← Base plate
    ~~~~~~~~~~  ← Foundation
   _ _ _ _ _ _  ← Floor
```

*(7 marks: 2 for components list, 2 for functions, 2 for sketch, 1 for typical sections)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

The **PEB design philosophy** is built on **five principles**:

1. **Optimization** — every member sized for *exact* loads (no over-design)
2. **Tapered sections** — web depth varies to match the **bending moment diagram (BMD)**
3. **Standardized connections** — 100% bolted at site
4. **Modular repetition** — repeated bays enable mass production
5. **Integrated envelope** — single-vendor supply of skeleton + cladding + accessories

**Why Tapered Sections?**
In a portal frame, the **bending moment is maximum at the knee (haunch)** and **minimum at the ridge (apex)**. A uniform-depth section designed for the maximum moment is **inefficient** near the apex. A **tapered section** matches the actual moment demand, saving ~20% steel in the rafter alone.

**Numerical Example (Weight Saving):**
Consider a rafter of **span $L = 30$ m**, UDL $w = 5$ kN/m.
- **CSB (uniform depth):** $d_u = 1200$ mm, $A_f = 3000$ mm², $t_w = 6$ mm
  - Weight per metre: $w_{CSB} \propto (2 \times 3000) + (1200 \times 6) = 6000 + 7200 = 13200$ mm³
- **PEB (tapered):** $d_{min} = 400$ mm, $d_{max} = 1200$ mm
  - Average depth: $d_{avg} = (1200 + 400) / 2 = 800$ mm
  - Weight per metre: $w_{PEB} \propto (2 \times 3000) + (800 \times 6) = 6000 + 4800 = 10800$ mm³

$$\text{Weight Saving} = \frac{13200 - 10800}{13200} \times 100 = 18.18\%$$

Including **higher steel grade (350 MPa vs. 250 MPa)** and **lighter secondary members and foundations**, the **overall project weight saving escalates to 20–30%**.

*(7 marks: 2 for design philosophy, 2 for tapered section rationale, 2 for numerical example, 1 for conclusion on 20-30% overall saving)*

---

#### **Question B: Alternative — Comparative Study and Applications**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB)** with **Conventional Steel Buildings (CSB)** in detail, covering at least **10 parameters** (design, sections, speed, cost, span, quality, seismic performance, expansion, connections, applications).

**(b) [7 Marks]** List the **major applications of PEB** across various sectors. Explain why PEB is **particularly suited for the Kerala construction industry**, citing specific scenarios and reasons.

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 4 | 14 Marks | Internal Choice to Question A]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| S. No. | Parameter | CSB | PEB |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design** | Manual, member-by-member, conservative | Software-optimized, computer-aided, least-weight |
| 2 | **Sections** | Standard hot-rolled I-beams (ISMB, ISMC), uniform depth | Tapered built-up I-sections, depth varies with BMD |
| 3 | **Speed** | 6–12 months typical | 6–12 weeks (60–70% faster) |
| 4 | **Cost** | Higher (more material, more labour) | **30–40% lower** (optimized steel, less labour) |
| 5 | **Clear Span** | Limited to ~25–30 m without trusses | Up to **80 m clear span** without columns |
| 6 | **Connections** | On-site welding + bolting (labour-intensive) | 100% **bolted at site** (fast, no welding) |
| 7 | **Quality** | Variable (field conditions affect quality) | **Factory-controlled**, superior consistency |
| 8 | **Seismic Performance** | Good (heavier mass = higher base shear) | **Better** (lighter mass = lower seismic forces) |
| 9 | **Foundation** | Larger footings (heavier superstructure) | **Smaller footings** (lighter loads, cost saving) |
| 10 | **Expansion** | Difficult (welded, permanent) | **Easy** — add more bays as needed |
| 11 | **Architectural Flexibility** | High — complex geometries possible | Limited — standard gable/lean-to profiles |
| 12 | **Relocation** | Not feasible | **Fully demountable** and re-erectable |
| 13 | **Steel Weight** | Heavier baseline | **20–30% lighter** |
| 14 | **Maintenance** | Periodic repainting required | Galvalume sheets need minimal maintenance |

*(7 marks: 0.5 × 14 = 7 for 10+ comparison points; or 1 mark for each well-explained parameter if fewer are listed)*

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Major Applications of PEB:**

| S. No. | Sector | Specific Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Industrial** | Factories, manufacturing plants, process industries, workshops |
| 2 | **Warehousing** | Cold storage, distribution centres, logistics parks |
| 3 | **Aviation** | Aircraft hangars, airport terminals, maintenance bays |
| 4 | **Commercial** | Showrooms, supermarkets, hypermarkets, exhibition halls |
| 5 | **Sports** | Indoor stadiums, gymnasiums, swimming pool enclosures |
| 6 | **Agriculture** | Grain silos, dairy farms, equipment storage sheds |
| 7 | **Defence** | Vehicle sheds, ammunition depots, military barracks |
| 8 | **Healthcare** | Modular hospitals, isolation wards (COVID-19) |
| 9 | **Education** | Auditoriums, school halls, vocational training centres |
| 10 | **Disaster Relief** | Rapid-deployment shelters (post-flood, post-cyclone) |

**Why PEB is Ideally Suited for Kerala:**

1. **High Rainfall & Monsoon Climate** — PEB roof slopes (1:20 to 1:10) ensure rapid water drainage; standing-seam roof profiles prevent leakage.
2. **Coastal Wind Zone (IS 875 Zone V, $V_b = 50$ m/s in Ernakulam, Alappuzha, Thrissur coast)** — PEB buildings are engineered for high wind speeds; lighter mass reduces wind overturning moments.
3. **Seismic Zone III (Parts of Idukki, Wayanad, Palakkad)** — Lighter superstructure = lower seismic base shear = inherently safer.
4. **Post-Disaster Reconstruction** — After the **2018 and 2019 Kerala floods**, PEB technology was extensively used for **rapidly rebuilding** damaged commercial establishments in Kuttanad, Chengannur, and Pathanamthitta.
5. **Land Scarcity & Vertical Urbanization** — PEBs can be built on **difficult terrain** with smaller foundations, ideal for Kerala's constrained urban sites.
6. **Industrial Growth in Kochi-Palakkad Corridor** — PEB warehouses support the **logistics and e-commerce boom** around Cochin International Airport (CIAL) and the proposed SmartCity Kochi.
7. **Tourism Infrastructure** — Beach resorts, convention centres, and sports facilities along the Malabar coast benefit from PEB's **fast construction** during the short working season.
8. **Cost Sensitivity** — Kerala's per-capita income supports SME industrialization, and PEBs offer the **lowest entry cost** for new factories.

*(7 marks: 2 for applications list, 2 for Kerala-specific climatic/geographic reasons, 2 for Kerala-specific economic/sectoral reasons, 1 for conclusion)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in PEB Questions (KTU ESE 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing PEB with Pre-Fabricated Buildings:** PEB is *not* the same as pre-fabricated concrete or modular buildings. PEB specifically refers to **pre-engineered steel structures** with **tapered built-up sections**. Marks are deducted for using these terms interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Omitting the Numerical Justification for Weight Saving:** Simply stating "PEB is 30% lighter" without showing the calculation (or referencing tapered section design) is incomplete. Always provide a **worked numerical example** for full marks.
>
> 3. **Ignoring IS Code References:** KTU expects students to reference **IS 875 (Parts 1–4)**, **IS 800:2007**, and **IS 2062** (steel material) in design-related questions. Marks are deducted for unsourced technical claims.
>
> 4. **Conflating PEB Components:** Students often confuse **purlins** (roof) with **girts** (wall), or **bracing** (longitudinal stability) with **portal action** (transverse stability). Be precise.
>
> 5. **Insufficient Sketch Labelling:** A diagram without **clear labels, dimensions, and member identification** attracts zero marks in the "diagram" component.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Disadvantages:** A balanced answer should mention **at least 2–3 disadvantages of PEB** (e.g., high initial design cost, need for specialized PEB manufacturer, limited aesthetic options). One-sided answers lose balance marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Solutions:** KTU examiners strictly enforce unit consistency. Always state units (kN, mm, MPa, m²) at each step.
>
> 8. **Skipping the "Why" Question:** When asked "Why is PEB economical?", simply listing "less steel" is insufficient. The *reason* must be tied to **tapered sections, factory efficiency, faster erection, and lower foundation costs**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU ESE Revision — Memorize these key points for Module 4 Introduction to PEB:**

- [ ] **PEB = Pre-Engineered Building** = factory-fabricated steel building with **tapered built-up sections** and **bolted site connections**.
- [ ] **Key Distinction:** PEB ≠ CSB. PEB is *optimized*; CSB is *standardized*.
- [ ] **Primary Members:** Tapered I-section columns + tapered I-section rafters (built-up from steel plates).
- [ ] **Secondary Members:** Cold-formed **Z-purlins** (roof) and **Z-girts** (walls).
- [ ] **Standard Building Parameters:** Width 20–80 m, Eave height 6–12 m, Bay spacing 6–9 m, Roof slope 1:10 to 1:20.
- [ ] **Weight Saving:** **20–30%** lighter than CSB due to tapered sections + higher grade steel (350 MPa).
- [ ] **Cost Saving:** **30–40%** overall project cost reduction.
- [ ] **Construction Speed:** **6–12 weeks** (vs. 6–12 months for CSB).
- [ ] **Clear Span:** Up to **80 m** without internal columns.
- [ ] **Connections:** 100% **bolted** at site (no on-site welding).
- [ ] **Steel Grades Used:** IS 2062 Grade **350 MPa** (vs. Grade 250 MPa in CSB).
- [ ] **Design Codes:** IS 800:2007 (general steel), IS 875 (loads), IS 2062 (material), MBMA (PEB-specific), AISC (international).
- [ ] **Sheeting Material:** **Galvalume** (Al-Zn coated) or Galvanized steel, **0.47–0.63 mm** thick, trapezoidal profile.
- [ ] **Insulation:** Glass wool, 50–100 mm, sandwiched in roof/wall panels.
- [ ] **Major Applications:** Warehouses, factories, aircraft hangars, sports halls, showrooms, post-disaster shelters.
- [ ] **Kerala-Specific Applications:** Post-flood reconstruction, CIAL logistics, coastal wind zone construction, smart city projects.
- [ ] **Formula to Remember:** $W_s = \dfrac{W_{CSB} - W_{PEB}}{W_{CSB}} \times 100 \approx 20\text{–}30\%$
- [ ] **Bending Moment Formula:** $M_{knee} \approx wL^2 / 10$ (for a portal frame under UDL)
- [ ] **Top 3 Advantages:** Speed, Cost, Quality.
- [ ] **Top 3 Disadvantages:** Limited aesthetic flexibility, dependence on specialized manufacturer, less suitable for high-rise/multi-storey.
- [ ] **Comparison Table:** Memorize at least **10 comparison points** between PEB and CSB.
- [ ] **Sketch Must Include:** Rafter, column, knee joint, purlin, girt, sheeting, bracing, base plate, foundation.
- [ ] **Key Phrase for Examiners:** "Tapered built-up sections optimize material usage by matching the bending moment diagram."

---

**End of Module 4 — Pre-Engineered Buildings: Introduction | PECET522 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Engineered Buildings: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pre-Engineered Buildings: Advantages # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Pre-Engineered Building (PEB)** is a steel structural system whose components (primary members, secondary members, roof sheeting, wall cladding, and accessories) are **factory-fabricated in standard modular cross-sections**, **pre-designed and pre-cut (including pre-punched holes)**, and then **transported to the site for bolted assembly**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET522 - Module 4):** > A Pre-Engineered Building is defined as a building engineered at a factory to a predetermined specification, where the structural members are fabricated in a controlled environment, shipped to the site, and erected using bolted connections. The design follows a **tapered-section philosophy** where member depth varies with the bending moment diagram, optimizing steel usage. The term "Pre-Engineered" specifically differentiates PEBs from traditional "Conventional Steel Buildings (CSB)" where every member is custom-fabricated (cut, welded, drilled) individually at site based on general engineering drawings. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "IKEA Furniture" Model Imagine the difference between **building a wardrobe from raw timber at home** versus **assembling a flat-pack IKEA wardrobe**: | Aspect | Custom-Made Wardrobe (CSB) | Flat-Pack Wardrobe (PEB) | |---|---|---| | **Where built** | All cutting/joints at your home (site) | All panels cut/drilled at factory | | **Skill needed** | Skilled carpenter on site | Only a wrench and the manual | | **Wastage** | High (sawdust, offcuts) | Minimal (factory-optimized) | | **Speed** | 2 weeks | 2 hours | | **Design** | Custom (expensive) | Standardized (cheap) | A **PEB is to a building what flat-pack furniture is to carpentry** — standardized components, factory precision, and on-site bolt-together assembly. ## 1.3 The Tapered Section Concept (Key to PEB Logic) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Tapered-Web" Innovation** > In conventional buildings, we use **uniform I-sections** (constant depth) even though the bending moment is maximum at the center and zero at supports. PEBs use **tapered I-sections** (deeper at mid-span, shallower at ends) — saving up to **30% steel tonnage**. $$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} \quad \text{(parabolic BMD for UDL)}$$ Since the bending moment varies along the span, the **section depth** is varied to match — heavier where the moment is high, lighter where the moment is low. This is the foundational engineering reason PEBs are economical. ## 1.4 Standard PEB Component Vocabulary (For Exam Definitions) | Component | KTU Term | Function | |---|---|---| | **Primary Members** | Main frame columns + rafters | Carry the major gravity/wind loads | | **Secondary Members** | Purlins, girts | Transfer loads from cladding to primary | | **Bracing** | Rod bracing, portal bracing | Resist lateral loads (wind/seismic) | | **Sheeting** | Roof & wall panels (GI/PPGI/ZincAlum) | Envelope + diaphragm action | | **Accessories** | Turbo ventilators, ridge vents, skylights | Ventilation + lighting | > [!NOTE] > For KTU 14-mark questions, always begin by defining PEB → then state the **pre-engineered vs. conventional** distinction → then enumerate advantages. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix ## 2.1 The Logic of "Pre-Engineering" — Why It Works The philosophy of PEB rests on **three engineering axioms**: 1. **Axiom of Repetition:** Industrial buildings (warehouses, factories, sheds) follow **similar geometric patterns** (clear span, eave height, roof slope). Standardizing these forms reduces design effort per square meter. 2. **Axiom of Optimization:** A structure experiences **varying internal forces** (BM, SF) along its length. Tapered sections match capacity with demand — no over-design. 3. **Axiom of Factory Productivity:** A CNC-controlled fabrication line is **10× faster, 10× more accurate, and 5× cheaper per kg** than site fabrication. ## 2.2 Comparative Engineering Logic: PEB vs. CSB The advantages of PEB are best understood by **contrasting each design decision** with a Conventional Steel Building. ### 2.2.1 Member Cross-Section Logic | Design Aspect | Conventional Steel Building (CSB) | Pre-Engineered Building (PEB) | |---|---|---| | **Column** | Uniform depth I-section (e.g., UB 610×229×140) | Tapered I-section (deeper at haunch) | | **Rafter** | Constant section (heavy throughout) | Tapered depth following BMD | | **Connections** | Welded shop + bolted site (mixed) | All **bolted** at site, welded only at factory | | **Design Code** | IS 800:2007 (basic) | IS 800:2007 + AISC MBMA (optimized) | | **Design Basis** | Standard sections lookup | Custom computer-optimized per project | ### 2.2.2 Load Path Logic Both systems use the same load path: **Sheeting → Purlins/Girts → Rafters/Columns → Base plate → Foundation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The PEB difference is **NOT in the load path** but in the **member geometry** and **fabrication methodology**. The load path remains identical; the economy comes from the **optimized member sizes**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Value | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Max Bending Moment (simply supported, UDL) | $M = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | kN·m | **High** — justifies tapered section | | 2 | Max Shear Force (simply supported, UDL) | $V = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ | kN | **Medium** — governs web design | | 3 | Approx. Steel Saving (PEB vs CSB) | **20% – 30%** | % tonnage | **Very High** — direct KTU question | | 4 | Approx. Cost Saving (PEB vs CSB) | **25% – 40%** | % total cost | **Very High** | | 5 | Construction Speed (PEB) | **3 – 6 weeks** for 1000 m² | time | **High** | | 6 | Construction Speed (CSB) | **12 – 20 weeks** for 1000 m² | time | **High** | | 7 | Typical PEB Span Range | **15 m – 90 m** (single span) | m | **Medium** | | 8 | Typical Roof Slope | **1:10 to 1:20** (5° to 10°) | ratio | **Medium** | | 9 | Eave Height Range | **6 m – 30 m** | m | **Low** | | 10 | Connection Type | **Bolted (grade 8.8/10.9)** | friction bearing | **High** | > [!NOTE] > All values are **standard industry-accepted metrics** from the Metal Building Manufacturers Association (MBMA) guidelines and are widely cited in KTU model answers. Do not invent numbers; use these conservative ranges. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of PEBs PEBs dominate these sectors (highly likely KTU application question): - **🏭 Industrial:** Factories, warehouses, workshops, assembly plants - **📦 Logistics:** Cold storage, distribution centers, container yards - **✈️ Aviation:** Aircraft hangars (very large clear spans) - **🏟️ Commercial:** Showrooms, supermarkets, sports stadiums - **🌾 Agriculture:** Grain storage, dairy farms, poultry shelters - **🏥 Institutional:** School halls, hospital annexes, training centers ## 2.5 The "Why PEB is Economical" — Root Cause Analysis The economic advantage of PEB is **not magic** — it cascades from five engineering decisions: 1. **Tapered sections** → less steel by weight 2. **Factory fabrication** → less labor cost, less wastage 3. **Standardized connections** → less engineering hours per project 4. **Lighter dead load** → smaller foundations → less concrete 5. **Faster erection** → earlier occupancy → earlier revenue > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU answers, **always link each advantage to its root cause**. A vague answer like "PEB is cheaper" gets 1 mark. A structured answer like *"PEB uses 20-30% less steel because tapered sections eliminate over-design in low-moment regions"* gets full marks. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivations ## 3.1 Comparative Tonnage Analysis (Quantitative Proof of PEB Advantage) > **Objective:** Demonstrate mathematically **why** a PEB uses less steel than a CSB for the same building. ### Step 1 — Define a Common Test Case **Building Specification:** - Span: $L = 30 \, \text{m}$ (single span, clear) - Eave height: $H = 6 \, \text{m}$ - Bay spacing: $B = 6 \, \text{m}$ - Roof slope: $\theta = 5.71°$ (1:10) - Dead load (sheeting + purlins): $DL = 0.20 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ - Live load: $LL = 0.75 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ (for non-accessible roof) - Wind pressure: $W = 0.55 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ (basic wind speed 39 m/s, K1=1, K2=0.91, K3=1) - Total design load (ULS): $w = 1.5 \times (DL + LL) = 1.5 \times 0.95 = 1.425 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ **Linear load on rafter (per m of rafter length):** $$w_{rafter} = w \times B = 1.425 \times 6 = 8.55 \, \text{kN/m}$$ **Step 1 — Textual Reasoning:** We convert the **area load** (kN/m²) into a **linear load** (kN/m) on the rafter by multiplying by the bay spacing. This is because the rafter is a 1-D beam spanning 30 m, supporting a strip of roof of width equal to one bay. ### Step 2 — Maximum Bending Moment on the Rafter For a simply supported rafter under UDL: $$M_{max} = \frac{w_{rafter} \cdot L^2}{8}$$ **Substituting values:** $$M_{max} = \frac{8.55 \times 30^2}{8} = \frac{8.55 \times 900}{8} = \frac{7695}{8} = 961.875 \, \text{kN·m}$$ **Step 2 — Textual Reasoning:** The bending moment is maximum at mid-span and zero at the supports. The shape of the bending moment diagram (BMD) is a **parabola** with peak at center. This shape is the foundation of the PEB optimization. ### Step 3 — Bending Moment at Quarter Span (x = L/4 = 7.5 m) For a simply supported beam, BM at distance x from support is: $$M(x) = \frac{w \cdot x}{2} \cdot (L - x)$$ **At x = L/4:** $$M_{L/4} = \frac{8.55 \times 7.5}{2} \times (30 - 7.5) = \frac{64.125}{2} \times 22.5 = 32.0625 \times 22.5 = 721.406 \, \text{kN·m}$$ ### Step 4 — Bending Moment at End of Rafter (x = 0) $$M_{0} = \frac{w \cdot 0}{2} \times (30 - 0) = 0 \, \text{kN·m}$$ ### Step 5 — Tabulated Bending Moment Profile | Location on Rafter | Distance from Support (m) | Bending Moment (kN·m) | % of Mmax | |---|---|---|---| | **Support** | 0 | **0** | 0% | | **Quarter span** | 7.5 | **721.4** | 75% | | **Mid-span** | 15.0 | **961.9** | 100% | | **Three-quarter span** | 22.5 | **721.4** | 75% | **Step 5 — Textual Reasoning:** The BMD is **parabolic** — falling from $M_{max}$ at mid-span to **0 at supports**. A conventional uniform I-section has a constant section modulus $Z_{csb}$, but it is fully designed for $M_{max}$ everywhere — wasting capacity near the supports. ### Step 6 — Steel Volume (Weight) Comparison **For Conventional Steel Building (CSB) — Uniform Section:** $$V_{csb} = Z_{csb} \times L = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \times L$$ $$V_{csb} = \frac{961.875 \times 10^6}{250} \times 30000 \, \text{mm}^3$$ (Assuming $\sigma_{allow} = 250 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for Fe410 steel, depth uniform at, say, 600 mm throughout) **For PEB — Tapered Section (linearly tapered from 300 mm at end to 900 mm at mid-span):** The section modulus $Z(x)$ varies linearly with depth: $$Z(x) \approx Z_{end} + \frac{(Z_{mid} - Z_{end})}{L/2} \cdot x \quad \text{(for } 0 \leq x \leq L/2\text{)}$$ The **average section modulus** is approximately: $$\bar{Z}_{peb} \approx 0.6 \times Z_{csb} \quad \text{(rule of thumb, since only middle region needs full depth)}$$ **Steel volume saved:** $$\text{Saving} \% = \frac{V_{csb} - V_{peb}}{V_{csb}} \times 100 = (1 - 0.6) \times 100 = 40\%$$ **Step 6 — Textual Reasoning:** This is the **mathematical justification** for the 20–30% steel savings quoted in industry. The "1 - 0.6" factor is an *approximation*; actual savings depend on the **BMD shape, span, and end restraint conditions** (e.g., fixed-base columns reduce mid-span moment by 30%, increasing savings further). ### Step 7 — Foundation Loading Comparison | Parameter | CSB | PEB | Difference | |---|---|---|---| | Self-weight per rafter | $\sim 25$ kg/m | $\sim 16$ kg/m | **−36%** | | Total column reaction | 320 kN | 205 kN | **−36%** | | Foundation size | $2.5 \times 2.5 \times 1.2$ m | $1.8 \times 1.8 \times 1.0$ m | **−48% volume** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lighter steel → lighter foundations → less concrete → cascade savings.** This is why KTU answers must trace the *full chain* of savings, not just state the headline 30% number. ## 3.2 Time-Cycle Analytical Matrix The 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Explain why PEB construction is faster."* The analytical matrix below justifies each time-saving: | Activity | Conventional (CSB) | PEB | Time Saved | Root Cause | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Detailing** | Manual shop drawing per piece | Auto-generated from software | ~70% | CAD/CAM integration | | **Cutting** | Manual gas cutting at site | CNC plasma at factory | ~60% | Mechanized accuracy | | **Welding** | Site welds (slow, weather-affected) | Factory welds (robotic) | ~80% | Controlled environment | | **Painting** | Site touch-up after erection | Factory blast + prime coat | ~50% | Quality control | | **Erection** | Welded connections, scaffolding | Bolted with crane lift | ~75% | Bolted = no curing time | | **Total project** | 16 weeks (for 1000 m²) | 4 weeks (for 1000 m²) | **~75%** | Compounded efficiency | > [!NOTE] > The **75% time saving** is the multiplier effect of 6 individual time savings compounded. For 14-mark KTU answers, this matrix format scores the highest because it shows the **examiner your analytical depth**. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PEB Component Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CLIENT REQUIREMENTS
Span eave height bay spacing] --> B[STRUCTURAL DESIGN
IS 800 MBMA AISC] B --> C[FACTORY FABRICATION
CNC cutting welding drilling] C --> D[PRIMARY MEMBERS
Tapered columns rafters] C --> E[SECONDARY MEMBERS
Purlins girts eave struts] C --> F[SHEETING ACCESSORIES
Roof panels wall panels ventilators] D --> G[TRANSPORT TO SITE
Bolted connections only] E --> G F --> G G --> H[SITE ERECTION
Crane lift alignment bolting] H --> I[COMPLETED PEB STRUCTURE
Ready for services and occupancy] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1f5 style D fill:#e1ffe1 style E fill:#e1ffe1 style F fill:#e1ffe1 style G fill:#f5e1ff style H fill:#fff4e1 style I fill:#d0d0d0 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Comparison Flow — Conventional vs PEB Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR Q{What is your
building need?} Q -->|Custom geometry
Multi-storey
Aesthetic facade| CSB[CONVENTIONAL STEEL
Uniform I-sections
Site fabrication
Higher cost] Q -->|Standard span
Single storey
Industrial shed| PEB[PRE-ENGINEERED
Tapered sections
Factory fabrication
Lower cost] CSB --> CS1[Pros: Flexible design] CSB --> CS2[Cons: 30 percent more steel] CSB --> CS3[Cons: 4x longer construction time] PEB --> PS1[Pros: 30 percent steel saving] PEB --> PS2[Pros: 75 percent faster erection] PEB --> PS3[Pros: Single source responsibility] style CSB fill:#ffe1e1 style PEB fill:#e1ffe1 style Q fill:#fff4e1 style CS1 fill:#ffffff style CS2 fill:#ffffff style CS3 fill:#ffffff style PS1 fill:#ffffff style PS2 fill:#ffffff style PS3 fill:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Tapered Section Optimization Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB L[Bending Moment Diagram
Parabolic shape] --> M[Identify Mmax at mid-span] M --> N[Required section modulus
Zreq = Mmax by sigma] N --> O[Tapered I-section depth
Variable from end to mid] O --> P[End depth smaller
300 to 400 mm] O --> Q[Mid depth larger
700 to 1200 mm] P --> R[Average depth saves steel] Q --> R R --> S[Result 25 to 30 percent
steel reduction] style L fill:#fff4e1 style M fill:#ffe1f5 style N fill:#e1f5ff style O fill:#e1ffe1 style S fill:#d0d0d0 ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture Block — PEB Load Transfer Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[External Loads
Wind Rain Seismic Dead Live] --> L2[CLADDING
Roof sheets wall sheets] L2 --> L3[SECONDARY MEMBERS
Purlins girts] L3 --> L4[PRIMARY MEMBERS
Rafters columns] L4 --> L5[BRACING SYSTEM
Rod bracing portal frames] L4 --> L6[BASE PLATE
Anchor bolts] L6 --> L7[FOUNDATION
Isolated pad footings] L1 -.-> L5 style L1 fill:#ffe1e1 style L2 fill:#fff4e1 style L3 fill:#e1ffe1 style L4 fill:#e1f5ff style L5 fill:#f5e1ff style L6 fill:#ffe1f5 style L7 fill:#d0d0d0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Diagram Tip:** In a 14-mark answer, drawing **one schematic** (any of the above) earns **2-3 extra marks** for visual communication. Even a hand-drawn version of the load path is highly rewarded. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define a Pre-Engineered Building. How is it different from a conventional steel building? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Pre-Engineered Building (PEB) is a steel building whose structural components (primary members like columns and rafters, secondary members like purlins and girts, and cladding) are **pre-designed, factory-fabricated in standardized tapered sections, and bolted together at the site**. > **Difference (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | CSB | PEB | |---|---|---| | Member cross-section | Uniform I-sections | **Tapered I-sections** | | Fabrication | Site-based (cutting, welding) | **Factory-based (CNC)** | | Connections at site | Mixed (welded + bolted) | **Bolted only** | | Design time | Long (manual) | Short (standard templates) | --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** List **any four advantages** of Pre-Engineered Buildings over conventional steel structures. **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Reduced construction time** — 3 to 6 weeks vs 12 to 20 weeks for 1000 m² 2. **Lower steel consumption** — 20 to 30% saving due to tapered sections 3. **Reduced overall cost** — 25 to 40% saving in total project cost 4. **Single-source responsibility** — design, fabrication, and erection by one vendor --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model — 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **concept of Pre-Engineered Buildings** with neat sketches. Discuss the **main components** of a PEB system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **advantages and disadvantages** of PEB over conventional steel buildings. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks **Concept of PEB (3 marks):** A Pre-Engineered Building is a **factory-engineered steel building system** where the structural members are designed using **tapered (variable-depth) sections** that follow the bending moment diagram of the member. Components are pre-cut, pre-drilled, and pre-welded in the factory, then transported to site for **bolted assembly**. The key engineering principle is: **member depth is made proportional to the local bending moment** — deeper where moment is high, shallower where moment is low. This **"form follows force"** philosophy results in 20 to 30% steel savings compared to uniform-section conventional buildings. **Main Components of PEB (4 marks):** | Component | Function | Typical Section | |---|---|---| | **Main Frame Columns** | Support rafters, transfer vertical/lateral loads to foundation | Tapered I-section (depth 600–1500 mm) | | **Rafters** | Support purlins, carry roof loads to columns | Tapered I-section (depth variable) | | **End-wall Columns** | Support end-wall girts and sheeting | Tapered or uniform I-section | | **Purlins** | Transfer roof load to rafters | Cold-formed Z or C sections | | **Girts** | Transfer wall load to columns | Cold-formed Z or C sections | | **Bracing** | Resist longitudinal wind/seismic | Rods (round bars with turnbuckles) | | **Sheeting** | Weather envelope + diaphragm | GI/PPGI/Zinc-Alum profiled sheets | | **Accessories** | Ventilation, lighting, drainage | Turbo vents, skylights, gutters | --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks **Advantages (5 marks):** 1. **Economy in Steel (1 mark):** Tapered sections save 20–30% steel by weight. Theoretically justified: depth is proportional to bending moment, eliminating over-design at low-moment regions. 2. **Speed of Construction (1 mark):** Bolted connections enable 1000 m² building erection in 3–6 weeks vs 12–20 weeks for CSB. All cutting/welding done in parallel at factory while site is being prepared. 3. **Lower Cost (1 mark):** 25–40% cost saving from reduced steel tonnage, smaller foundations (lighter superstructure), and shorter project duration (less site overhead). 4. **Quality Control (0.5 mark):** Factory welding under controlled environment with certified procedures ensures higher quality than site welding (weather-affected). 5. **Single-Source Responsibility (0.5 mark):** One vendor handles design → fabrication → erection → commissioning, eliminating interface conflicts. 6. **Flexibility in Future Expansion (0.5 mark):** Bolted connections allow easy dismantling, relocation, or extension of the building. 7. **Large Clear Spans (0.5 mark):** Economical for 30–90 m clear spans (warehouses, aircraft hangars) without intermediate columns. **Disadvantages (2 marks):** 1. **Limited Aesthetic Options (0.5 mark):** Standard modular forms restrict creative architectural expression; unsuitable for premium commercial facades. 2. **Less Fire Resistance (0.5 mark):** Steel loses strength at 550°C — requires fire-proof cladding/treatment in industrial fires. 3. **Corrosion Susceptibility (0.5 mark):** Steel needs regular anti-corrosion treatment, especially in coastal/chemical environments. 4. **Limited Multi-storey Application (0.5 mark):** PEB is most economical for single-storey; multi-storey (above 3 floors) shifts back to CSB territory. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:** > 1. ❌ Writing only "advantages" without linking to **engineering reasoning** (e.g., saying "PEB is faster" without explaining *why bolted connections* are faster). > 2. ❌ **Confusing PEB with Pre-Fabricated Buildings** — they are NOT the same. PEB specifically refers to **steel framing with tapered sections**; pre-fabricated can be concrete panels, modular rooms, etc. > 3. ❌ Omitting **disadvantages** — KTU answers expecting 14 marks for a comparative question must include both sides. > 4. ❌ Forgetting units and numerical values — always quantify (e.g., "30% steel saving" is better than "less steel"). > 5. ❌ Not drawing **even a rough sketch** of the PEB cross-section. A small sketch showing tapered column + tapered rafter + purlin + sheeting easily earns 1–2 extra marks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative 14-Mark Question) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model — 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a comparative table, explain how **Pre-Engineered Buildings differ from Conventional Steel Buildings** in terms of design philosophy, fabrication, and construction methodology. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory shed is to be constructed with a **clear span of 25 m, eave height 7 m, and bay spacing 6 m**. The total roof load (DL + LL, factored) is **1.5 kN/m²**. Calculate the **maximum bending moment on the central rafter** and explain how a PEB would be more economical than a CSB for the same rafter. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks **Comparative Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Conventional Steel Building (CSB) | Pre-Engineered Building (PEB) | |---|---|---| | **Design Basis** | IS 800:2007 (basic) | IS 800:2007 + AISC/MBMA (optimized) | | **Section Selection** | Standard rolled sections (UB, UC, ISMB) | Custom tapered sections per project | | **Design Time** | 4–8 weeks (manual) | 1–2 weeks (standard templates) | | **Fabrication Location** | Site-dominant (60% site, 40% factory) | Factory-dominant (95% factory, 5% site) | | **Cutting/Drilling** | Manual gas cutting, hand drilling | CNC plasma/laser cutting, robotic drilling | | **Welding Quality** | Site welds (weather-affected) | Factory welds (certified procedure) | | **Site Connections** | Mixed (welded + bolted) | Bolted only (Grade 8.8/10.9) | | **Erection Time (1000 m²)** | 12–20 weeks | 3–6 weeks | | **Steel Consumption** | Baseline (100%) | 70–80% of CSB (20–30% saving) | | **Total Project Cost** | Baseline (100%) | 60–75% of CSB (25–40% saving) | | **Aesthetic Flexibility** | High (custom sections, facades) | Limited (standard forms) | | **Best Suited For** | Multi-storey, complex geometry, premium facades | Single-storey, large span, industrial | **Design Philosophy Difference (2 marks):** CSB uses the **"uniform section"** approach — pick one section that satisfies the maximum moment everywhere along the span. This is **conservative** and **wasteful** at low-moment regions. PEB uses the **"tapered section"** approach — section depth is varied along the length to match the actual bending moment demand. This is **optimized** and **efficient** — every kilogram of steel is fully utilized. **Construction Methodology Difference (1 mark):** In CSB, a large portion of work (cutting, welding, drilling) happens **at site** under weather-sensitive conditions, requiring skilled labor and prolonged time. In PEB, all these activities happen **in factory** under controlled conditions, then components are **bolted together at site** in a fraction of the time. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks **Given Data:** - Span: $L = 25 \, \text{m}$ - Bay spacing: $B = 6 \, \text{m}$ - Factored area load: $w_{area} = 1.5 \, \text{kN/m}^2$ **Step 1 — Convert area load to linear load on rafter (1 mark):** $$w_{linear} = w_{area} \times B = 1.5 \times 6 = 9.0 \, \text{kN/m}$$ *Reasoning:* The rafter is a 1-D beam. The 6 m bay width is the tributary width from which the rafter collects roof load. Multiply area load by tributary width to get the linear load on the beam. **Step 2 — Maximum bending moment (2 marks):** For a simply supported rafter under uniformly distributed load: $$M_{max} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = \frac{9.0 \times 25^2}{8} = \frac{9.0 \times 625}{8} = \frac{5625}{8} = 703.125 \, \text{kN·m}$$ *Reasoning:* The BMD is parabolic with peak at mid-span. This is the **critical design moment** that determines the section depth at mid-span. **Step 3 — Required section modulus at mid-span (1 mark):** For Fe410 steel, $\sigma_{allow} = 250 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 250 \, \text{MPa}$ $$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{703.125 \times 10^6}{250} = 2{,}812{,}500 \, \text{mm}^3 \approx 2.81 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3$$ **Step 4 — PEB economy explanation (3 marks):** In a **CSB**, the rafter uses a uniform section throughout — say, an **ISMB 600** (depth 600 mm) with $Z = 3.06 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3$, which satisfies $Z_{req}$. The same 600 mm depth is maintained at the support, where the moment is **0** — pure waste. In a **PEB**, the rafter is **tapered**: deeper at mid-span (where the moment peaks at $703.125$ kN·m) and shallower at supports (where the moment is 0). The depth at mid-span can be ~600 mm, while at the support, it is reduced to ~250–300 mm. The **average depth** of the PEB rafter is approximately **60–70%** of the CSB rafter depth. This translates to: - **Steel weight saved:** ~25–30% per rafter - **Lighter rafter → smaller column → smaller foundation** - **Cascading cost savings:** typically 25–40% on overall project > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall for Part (b):** > - ❌ Forgetting to multiply by bay spacing — using $w = 1.5$ kN/m directly on the rafter (off by 6×) > - ❌ Using unfactored load when the problem states "factored" > - ❌ Not writing units (kN·m, kN/m², mm³) — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit > - ✅ Always show formula → substitution → final answer with units → verbal interpretation --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔑 High-Yield Definitions - **PEB:** Steel building with factory-fabricated, tapered-section components, bolted-assembled at site - **Tapered Section:** Variable-depth I-section matching the local bending moment - **CSB:** Conventional Steel Building with uniform rolled sections and site fabrication - **Eave Height:** Vertical height from floor to the junction of roof and wall sheeting - **Clear Span:** Horizontal distance between the inner faces of two adjacent columns ### 🔑 Numbers to Memorize (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Steel saving in PEB | **20% – 30%** | | Cost saving in PEB | **25% – 40%** | | Construction speed-up | **3× to 4×** | | PEB typical span | **15 m – 90 m** | | PEB typical eave height | **6 m – 30 m** | | Common roof slope | **1:10 to 1:20 (5° to 10°)** | | Bolt grade used | **8.8 / 10.9 (HSFG)** | ### 🔑 Engineering Logic Points (Why PEBs Work) 1. **Tapered section** → matches capacity with demand → less steel 2. **Factory fabrication** → controlled environment → faster, better quality 3. **Bolted site connections** → no welding, no curing → faster erection 4. **Lighter structure** → smaller foundation → less concrete → cascade savings 5. **Single vendor** → single design → no interface conflicts → smoother execution ### 🔑 Key Differences (CSB vs PEB) | CSB | PEB | |---|---| | Uniform section | Tapered section | | Site fabrication | Factory fabrication | | Welded + bolted at site | **Bolted only** at site | | Slow (16 weeks/1000 m²) | **Fast** (4 weeks/1000 m²) | | More steel (100%) | **Less steel** (70–80%) | | High cost (100%) | **Low cost** (60–75%) | | Flexible aesthetics | Limited aesthetics | ### 🔑 Disadvantages of PEB (Don't Skip These) - Limited architectural flexibility (standard forms only) - Poor fire resistance (steel loses strength at 550°C) - Corrosion susceptibility (needs protective coatings) - Not economical for multi-storey (above 3 floors) - Requires careful foundation design (less self-weight = less restraint against uplift) ### 🔑 Typical 14-Mark Answer Structure 1. **Introduction** — definition (2 marks) 2. **Main components** with diagram (3 marks) 3. **Advantages** with engineering reasoning (5 marks) 4. **Disadvantages** (2 marks) 5. **Applications / Conclusion** (2 marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** For "PEB Advantages" questions, **always** — (1) define first, (2) draw at least one schematic, (3) quantify every advantage with numbers, (4) link each advantage to an engineering root cause, (5) close with a comparison table or summary.

topics.title: Pre-Engineered Buildings vs Conventional Steel Buildings - Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET522 - MODERN CONSTRUCTION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-Engineered Buildings vs Conventional Steel Buildings - Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conventional Steel Buildings (CSB) — Formal Definition

A **Conventional Steel Building (CSB)** is a structural system in which all primary members (rafters, columns, purlins, girts, bracings) are designed and detailed from **standard hot-rolled sections** (I-sections, channels, angles) procured from a rolling mill, and then **cut, welded, drilled, and assembled on the construction site**. The design philosophy is based on **uniform cross-sections** along the entire span, even though internal forces vary considerably along the length.

## 1.2 Pre-Engineered Buildings (PEB) — Formal Definition

A **Pre-Engineered Building (PEB)** is a structural system engineered at the factory using a **pre-determined inventory of standard plates, coils, and sheets**, where the primary framing members are fabricated as **tapered built-up sections** whose depth and flange dimensions vary along the member length in direct proportion to the local bending moment. The entire building is shipped as a **kit of pre-punched, pre-welded, pre-drilled components** to the site, where it is mechanically bolted together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PEB concept (popularized in India by M/s. Kirby Building Systems, Zamil Steel, Tata Bluescope) is recognized in IS 875, IS 800:2007, and AISC MBMA (Metal Building Manufacturers Association) guidelines as an approved alternative to conventional steel construction for **low-rise, single-storey industrial, commercial, and warehouse applications**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The LEGO vs Custom Furniture Analogy

Imagine you are building a wardrobe for your bedroom:

| Approach | Analogy | PEB Equivalent | CSB Equivalent |
|---------|---------|----------------|----------------|
| **Pre-cut standardized panels** shipped flat, you assemble with bolts | IKEA wardrobe | PEB | — |
| Carpenter comes to site, cuts standard timber pieces one by one, welds & nails | Local carpenter | — | CSB |

In the **PEB (IKEA) approach**, the manufacturer already knows the exact load path. They send **panels of varying thickness** — thicker where loads are high (middle of beam), thinner where loads are low (supports). You just bolt them.

In the **CSB (carpenter) approach**, the carpenter uses the **same thickness timber from end to end**, even though the middle carries more load. This is **material-inefficient but designer-flexible**.
