# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Construction 3D Printing. List any four advantages over conventional construction.
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank 2024, Module 4]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction 3D Printing (C3DP)** is an automated additive manufacturing process wherein computer-controlled machines deposit construction-grade materials (cementitious mortars, geopolymers, or composites) in successive horizontal layers, as defined by a digital 3D model, to fabricate building components or entire structures without the use of conventional formwork.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Formwork Elimination:** Saves 35–60% of labor and material cost typically associated with formwork.
2. **Faster Construction:** Walls can be printed in hours instead of days; entire small houses in under 24 hours.
3. **Reduced Material Waste:** Material wastage drops from ~30% to under 5%.
4. **Architectural Freedom:** Enables complex free-form geometries (curved, organic) that are uneconomical with conventional methods.

---

### Question 2: What is thixotropy, and why is it critical for printable concrete?
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank 2024, Module 4]`

**Model Answer:**

**Thixotropy** is a rheological property of a fluid (or gel) whereby the material becomes less viscous (more fluid) when subjected to shear stress (e.g., during pumping or extrusion), and regains its higher viscosity (becomes stiff) when the shear stress is removed (i.e., after deposition).

**Why it is critical for printable concrete:**
- During **pumping through the hose and extrusion through the nozzle**, the mortar must flow smoothly — this requires low viscosity under shear.
- After deposition, the mortar must **rapidly regain stiffness** to support the weight of subsequent layers above without slumping or collapsing.
- Without thixotropy, the printed wall would deform, lose dimensional accuracy, and potentially fail structurally during printing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified for Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **various technologies used in Construction 3D Printing**, with a comparative table highlighting their working principles, materials, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A 3D-printed concrete wall has the following specifications:
- Outer plan dimensions: 8 m × 5 m (closed rectangular perimeter)
- Wall height: 3.0 m
- Wall thickness (single bead): 50 mm
- Layer thickness: 15 mm
- Nozzle travel speed: 200 mm/s
- Bead width: 50 mm

Calculate: **(i)** Number of layers, **(ii)** Total solid volume, **(iii)** Volumetric flow rate, **(iv)** Theoretical print time, **(v)** Actual print time assuming an efficiency factor of 1.3. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (a)

**The principal C3DP technologies are:**

**1. Extrusion-Based Printing (Most Common)**
- **Mechanism:** Wet mortar is pumped through a nozzle that moves along a programmed toolpath.
- **Equipment:** Gantry systems (e.g., COBOD BOD2) or 6-axis robotic arms (e.g., Apis Cor).
- **Materials:** Cementitious mortars, geopolymers, fiber-reinforced composites.
- **Advantages:** High build rate; suitable for full-scale buildings.
- **Limitations:** Requires thixotropic mix design; limited overhang capability without supports.

**2. Powder-Bed / Binder Jetting**
- **Mechanism:** A roller spreads a thin layer of dry powder; a print head selectively jets a binder liquid to bind particles where solid is desired.
- **Equipment:** Powder bed platform (similar to SLS in polymer AM).
- **Materials:** Sand, recycled glass, mineral powders + binder (water or resin).
- **Advantages:** No support structures needed for overhangs; can print complex internal lattices.
- **Limitations:** Lower mechanical strength; powder handling and recycling issues.

**3. Material Jetting / Spray-Based**
- **Mechanism:** Liquid or paste material is sprayed through fine nozzles in droplet form.
- **Equipment:** Multi-jet arrays mounted on gantries.
- **Materials:** Multi-material resins, geopolymers.
- **Advantages:** Multi-material capability, smooth surface finish.
- **Limitations:** Slower build rate, higher equipment cost.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Parameter | Extrusion | Powder-Bed | Material Jetting |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material State | Wet paste | Dry powder | Liquid/droplets |
| Build Speed | High | Medium | Low |
| Strength | High (concrete-grade) | Medium | Low-Medium |
| Support Required | Yes | No | Yes |
| Cost | Medium | High | High |
| Scale | Full buildings | Components | Components |

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (b)

**Given:**
- Plan: 8 m × 5 m rectangle
- $H = 3.0$ m, $w = 0.05$ m, $t = 0.015$ m, $v = 200$ mm/s

**(i) Number of Layers:**

$$n = \frac{H}{t} = \frac{3.0}{0.015} = 200 \text{ layers}$$

**(ii) Total Solid Volume:**

Perimeter of wall:

$$P = 2 \times (8 + 5) = 26 \text{ m}$$

Cross-sectional area per layer:

$$A_{cs} = P \times w = 26 \times 0.05 = 1.30 \text{ m}^2$$

Total solid volume:

$$V_{solid} = A_{cs} \times H = 1.30 \times 3.0 = 3.90 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Volumetric Flow Rate (in mm units):**

$$q = v \cdot w \cdot t = 200 \text{ mm/s} \times 50 \text{ mm} \times 15 \text{ mm} = 150{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iv) Theoretical Print Time:**

Convert volume to mm³:

$$V_{solid} = 3.90 \text{ m}^3 = 3.90 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$T_p = \frac{V_{solid}}{q} = \frac{3.90 \times 10^9}{150{,}000} = 26{,}000 \text{ s} = 7.22 \text{ hours}$$

**(v) Actual Print Time (with efficiency factor $k_{eff} = 1.3$):**

$$T_{actual} = T_p \times 1.3 = 7.22 \times 1.3 = 9.39 \text{ hours}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for Module 4]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **constituent materials and mix design requirements** for printable concrete. Explain how thixotropy, buildability, and open time govern the mix proportioning. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare the **sustainability and economic performance** of Construction 3D Printing with conventional construction methods using a quantitative analytical framework. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (a)

**Printable Concrete Constituents:**

| Constituent | Typical Proportion | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) | 30–50 % by mass | Primary binder |
| Silica fume / Fly ash / GGBS | 10–30 % | Supplementary cementitious material (SCM); improves pumpability |
| Fine aggregate (sand ≤ 2 mm) | 40–60 % | Skeleton; particle size critical to avoid nozzle clogging |
| Coarse aggregate | 0–20 % (limited) | Reduces shrinkage; but increases nozzle clogging risk |
| Water | 0.3–0.4 w/c ratio | Hydration; controlled for workability |
| Superplasticizers | 0.5–2 % | Rheology control |
| Viscosity modifiers (e.g., nano-clay, HPMC) | 0.1–0.5 % | Thixotropy and shape retention |
| Accelerators | 0.5–3 % | Fast early strength for buildability |

**Three Critical Rheological Properties:**

**1. Thixotropy** — discussed above (shear-thinning then rapid recovery).

**2. Buildability** — the ability of the printed layer to support the weight of subsequent layers without collapsing. Depends on:
- **Green strength development rate**
- **Layer height-to-bead-width ratio** (typical: $h/w \leq 1.5$)
- **Yield stress evolution** with time

**3. Open Time** — the time window during which the mix remains extrudable (pumpable). Beyond open time, the mix stiffens in the hose, causing blockages. Typical open time: **30 – 90 minutes**.

**Mix Design Governing Logic:**

The mix designer must balance a paradox — the mix must be **fluid enough to pump** but **stiff enough to hold shape** immediately after extrusion. This is achieved through:
- **Low w/c ratio** (high strength, low fluidity).
- **High dosage of viscosity-modifying admixtures (VMAs)**.
- **Stepwise addition of water** (delayed hydration).
- **Optimized particle packing** (Andreasen or modified Andreasen model).

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (b)

**Sustainability Comparison:**

| Parameter | Conventional | C3DP | Net Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Embodied CO₂ (kg/m² wall) | 250 | 120 | **−52 %** |
| Construction waste (kg/m²) | 30 | 6 | **−80 %** |
| Water consumption (L/m²) | 180 | 110 | **−39 %** |
| Formwork timber (kg/m²) | 25 | 0 | **−100 %** |
| Labor hours/m² | 2.5 | 0.8 | **−68 %** |

**Economic Comparison (per m² of wall):**

| Cost Head | Conventional (₹) | C3DP (₹) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material | 900 | 600 |
| Labor | 750 | 250 |
| Formwork | 500 | 0 |
| Equipment (amortized) | 100 | 400 |
| **Total** | **2,250** | **1,250** |

**Quantitative Analytical Framework — Payback Period:**

Assume C3DP equipment cost = ₹ 50,00,000 (5 million), with project cost saving of ₹ 1,000/m². Payback:

$$N = \frac{C_{eq}}{\text{Saving}/\text{m}^2 \times \text{Output m}^2/\text{year}}$$

For a printer producing 5,000 m²/year of wall:

$$N = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}000 \times 5{,}000} = 1 \text{ year}$$

> **Conclusion:** C3DP achieves payback in **~1 year** under high-utilization scenarios, making it economically attractive for housing developers, prefab yards, and large-scale infrastructure projects.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in C3DP questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing m and mm in the same formula. Always convert to a **single unit system** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Efficiency Factor:** Theoretical print time is **not** the same as actual print time. Always multiply by $k_{eff} = 1.2$ to $1.5$.
> 3. **Confusing Extrusion with Powder-Bed:** Examiners will deduct marks if you describe powder-bed behavior using extrusion terminology.
> 4. **Ignoring Thixotropy:** Any answer about printable concrete mix design that does **not** mention thixotropy is incomplete — examiners expect this as a key term.
> 5. **Missing the Layer Interface Bond:** In strength questions, students forget that printed elements have **anisotropic strength** (weaker along layer interfaces). State this clearly.
> 6. **Wrong Volume Definition:** "Wall volume" can mean gross, net solid, or enclosed volume. Always define your terms explicitly in step 1.
> 7. **No Mention of Reinforcement:** C3DP concrete is brittle and weak in tension. Examiners expect mention of embedded rebar, fibers, or post-tensioning as a structural limitation.

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Memorize these 20 key bullets before every KTU C3DP exam:**

- ✅ **C3DP = Additive Manufacturing at construction scale**, using layer-by-layer material deposition without formwork.
- ✅ ASTM F2792 is the foundational standard defining Additive Manufacturing.
- ✅ **Three main C3DP technologies:** Extrusion, Powder-Bed (Binder Jetting), Material Jetting.
- ✅ **Layer thickness** is typically 10–50 mm in C3DP (vs. 0.1 mm in polymer AM).
- ✅ **Thixotropy** = shear-thinning + rapid recovery → critical for printable concrete.
- ✅ **Three rheological properties:** Pumpability, Buildability, Open Time.
- ✅ **G-code** is generated by the slicer from the 3D BIM/CAD model.
- ✅ **Key pioneers:** Contour Crafting (Khoshnevis), COBOD, Apis Cor, Winsun.
- ✅ **Formwork savings:** 35–60% of conventional cost eliminated.
- ✅ **Material waste reduction:** from ~30% to under 5%.
- ✅ **Construction speed:** 3–10× faster than conventional for walls.
- ✅ **Anisotropy:** Printed elements are weaker along layer interfaces (factor 0.7–0.9).
- ✅ **Nozzle diameters:** 20–50 mm for C3DP (vs. 0.4 mm for polymer).
- ✅ **Print Time formula:** $T_p = V_{solid} / (v \cdot w \cdot t)$.
- ✅ **Volumetric flow rate:** $q = v \cdot w \cdot t$.
- ✅ **Layer count:** $n = H / t$.
- ✅ **Curing:** Accelerated via mist, heat, or chemical accelerators to gain green strength within minutes.
- ✅ **Reinforcement is a challenge** — post-embedding rebar, fibers, or mesh is mandatory for structural use.
- ✅ **Sustainability:** ~50% reduction in embodied CO₂; ~80% reduction in construction waste.
- ✅ **Real-world milestones:** Yhnova (France), Dubai Office, Project Milestone (Netherlands), NASA Mars habitat research.

> **FINAL EXAM ADVICE:** For Part A questions, always begin with a **one-line crisp definition** followed by a **numbered list** of advantages/limitations. For Part B numericals, **state given data → list formulas → solve step-by-step → provide final answer in highlighted units**. KTU examiners reward clarity, not just final answers.
